Coverage for privacy breaches, data security incidents, business interruption from cyberattacks, cyber extortion. Covered above in the cyber insurance deep dive.
Cyber Liability Insurance
Coverage for privacy breaches, data security incidents, business interruption from cyberattacks, cyber extortion, and media liability. Typically includes first-party (own damages) and third-party (claims by others) coverages.
Cyber Risk
Risk of financial loss, operational disruption, or reputation damage from unauthorized access, disclosure, or disruption of computer systems and data. Controls include firewalls, endpoint protection, access controls, employee training, incident response plans, and cyber insurance.
Cybersecurity Framework (NIST CSF) ↗
A voluntary framework of cybersecurity best practices developed by NIST. Built around five functions: Identify, Protect, Detect, Respond, Recover. Widely adopted across industries as a baseline for cyber risk management.
D
Damages — General
Intangible losses, such as pain and suffering or mental anguish.
Damages — Special
Tangible, financial losses that can be documented.
Danger Tree
A standing tree with defects that make it likely to fail — dead, diseased, leaning, or damaged. Must be assessed and often removed before any work near it.
Dangerous Instrumentality Doctrine
States that anyone involved in the use of inherently dangerous products or machines is 100% liable for their own damages.
DART Rate (Days Away, Restricted, or Transferred)
An OSHA metric measuring the rate of injuries causing days away from work, restricted activity, or job transfer per 100 full-time workers per year. Formula: (DART cases × 200,000) ÷ total hours worked.
Dealers' Open Lot Coverage
Specialized physical damage coverage for auto dealers' inventory parked on the lot. Covers damage from weather (hail is a major exposure), theft, and vandalism. Written at agreed value per unit or blanket limit.
Declarations Page (Dec Page)
The summary page of a policy. Shows named insured, address, policy period, coverage limits, deductibles, premium, and any endorsements. The first page to check when answering any client question about the policy.
Declaratory Judgment Action
A lawsuit filed to have a court declare the rights and obligations of parties under an insurance policy. Common when insurer and insured disagree about whether a claim is covered.
Declination
An insurer's refusal to write a risk. Documenting declinations from admitted carriers is typically required before surplus lines coverage can be placed.
Declinations
A record of the insurers that have refused to write a risk. Required documentation for surplus lines placements in most states — typically 3-5 declinations from admitted carriers must be proven before surplus lines coverage can be bound.
Declined Coverage
Takes place when an insurance company rejects an application for coverage.
Deductible
The amount the insured must pay in a loss before any payment is due from the company.
Deductible — Fixed
One specific, predetermined amount that a policyholder must pay out-of-pocket before his policy coverage kicks in.
Deductible — Franchise
The policyholder only pays if the total damages come out to less than his deductible. If the cost of damages equals or exceeds the deductible, the insurer pays the full amount and the policyholder pays nothing.
Deductible — Percentage
A deductible that is calculated as a percentage of the value of the insured risk.
Default Judgement
A judgement entered by the court against a party who fails to present his case or defense in a civil action. Most commonly, this applies when the defendant fails to show up and answer the claim brought against him, so the court decides in favor of the plaintiff.
Defense Inside Limits
A liability policy provision where legal defense costs reduce the available coverage. Contrasts with "defense outside limits," where defense costs are paid in addition to the limit. Common in professional liability, uncommon in CGL.
Defense Outside Limits
Defense costs are paid by the insurer in addition to the policy limit — not subtracted from coverage available for settlement. Standard in CGL policies.
Definitions Section
The section that assigns specific, often narrower meaning to key words. Words like "occurrence," "accident," "property damage," "insured," and "suit" have specific policy meanings that determine coverage in close cases. Never assume the everyday meaning of a word equals the policy meaning.
Deluge Sprinkler System
All heads are open; water discharges from all heads when a detection system activates the valve. Used in high-hazard areas (aircraft hangars, chemical processing) where rapid, broad coverage is needed.
Depreciation
The act of lowering an item's value due to use or wear and tear. Depreciation is based on Age, Condition, and Life Expectancy.
Depreciation — Accumulated
The total decrease of an item's value over a period of time. Formula: annual depreciation × number of years used. Subtract this number from the item's replacement cost to get its actual cash value (ACV).
Depreciation — Annual
An item's replacement cost divided by the number of years in its expected lifespan.
Determinable and Measurable Loss
One of the requirements of an insurable risk — the loss must be definite in time, place, cause, and amount. Emotional distress, for example, is harder to assign a precise dollar value.
Diminished Value Claim
A claim for the reduction in a vehicle's market value after it has been involved in an accident and repaired. Even properly repaired vehicles sell for less than identical vehicles without accident history. Laws on recovery vary significantly by state.
Direct Bill
A billing arrangement where the insurance company bills the policyholder directly. The agent receives a commission check from the carrier.
Direct Bill Commission Statement
A carrier report showing commissions earned on direct-bill policies. Typically paid monthly by the carrier to the agency.
Direct Writer
An insurance company that sells policies to the insured through salaried representatives or exclusive agents only — not through independent brokers.
Directors & Officers (D&O) Liability
Coverage for claims against directors and officers for acts (or failures to act) in their corporate capacity. Protects personal assets of board members and executives. Three coverage parts: Side A (individual directors/officers when corporation won't or can't indemnify), Side B (corporation's indemnification obligations), Side C (corporate entity liability for securities claims, publicly traded companies).
Disability Insurance
A type of insurance paid to an individual if they are injured and unable to work for a specified length of time.
Disadvantage of Self Insurance
One large catastrophic loss could deplete reserves and potentially wipe out the organization. Often paired with stop-loss insurance.
Disaster Recovery Plan (DRP)
A subset of a business continuity plan focused on restoring IT systems and data after a disruption. Includes backup procedures, recovery time objectives (RTO), and recovery point objectives (RPO).
Dividend (Insurance)
A refund of a portion of the premium paid by the insured from insurer surplus, typically paid by mutual insurance companies when claim experience and investment returns are favorable.
Doctrine of Reasonable Expectations
The contract must provide the coverage that a policyholder might reasonably expect, despite the actual policy language.
Document and Insure Your Property
A core loss-prevention practice: create and maintain a detailed record of property and possessions (photos, videos, receipts, serial numbers) and confirm that insurance coverage is adequate. After a disaster, documentation dramatically speeds up claims and ensures accurate settlement.
Dog Bite Exclusion
An endorsement or policy provision excluding liability coverage for injuries caused by certain dog breeds or specific dogs. Insurers vary on which breeds are excluded; some insurers exclude dogs based on prior bite history rather than breed.
DOT Number
A unique identifier assigned by FMCSA to commercial motor vehicles operating in interstate commerce. Required on the vehicle and used to track safety records.
Drive Other Car Endorsement
Extends business auto coverage to non-owned vehicles operated by the person (or spouse) named in the endorsement. Coverage does not apply to autos owned by the person named in the endorsement or spouse. Also called "Broadened Coverage for Named Individuals."
Driver Qualification File (DQF)
Required by FMCSA for each commercial driver. Contains: employment application, previous employer inquiries (3 years), MVR (annually), road test or equivalent, medical examiner's certificate, annual driver review, and any training certificates. Must be maintained for duration of employment plus 3 years.
Drop-Down Coverage
A feature of umbrella policies that provides coverage for certain losses not covered by the underlying policy (subject to a self-insured retention). For example, an umbrella may cover personal injury liability broader than the underlying CGL's definition.
Drug and Alcohol Testing
FMCSA requires commercial drivers to be tested pre-employment, post-accident, randomly, reasonably suspicious, return-to-duty, and follow-up. Results go into the FMCSA Clearinghouse.
Dry Pipe Sprinkler System
Used in unheated spaces where wet systems would freeze. Pipes contain pressurized air instead of water. When a head opens, air escapes, pressure drops, a valve opens, and water flows. Slower response than wet systems (a few seconds).
Duty to Defend
The insurer's obligation to provide legal defense for covered claims, even claims that may ultimately prove groundless. Triggered by any allegation that could potentially be covered. Broader than the duty to indemnify. Defense costs are typically outside the policy limits in CGL — paid in addition to, not within, the limit.
Duty to Indemnify
The insurer's obligation to pay damages on behalf of the insured. Requires actual covered loss (judgment or settlement). Narrower than duty to defend — defense is triggered by allegations, indemnification requires proven damages.
Dwelling Coverage (Coverage A)
The homeowners policy coverage for the physical structure of the home itself, including attached structures. Limit should generally equal the cost to rebuild, not the market value of the home.
E
E&O (Errors & Omissions) Reporting
Internal agency requirement to promptly notify the agency's E&O carrier of any claim, potential claim, or circumstance that could give rise to a claim. Failure to timely report can void coverage.
Earned Premium
The portion of premium for which the insurance company has already provided coverage. If a 12-month policy has been in force for 6 months, 50% of the premium has been earned.
Earthquake Coverage
Generally excluded from standard property policies; purchased as a separate policy or endorsement. Usually has a significant deductible (often a percentage of the insured value rather than a dollar amount).
Earthquake Exclusion
Standard property policies exclude earthquake damage. Requires a separate policy or endorsement, typically with a significant deductible (often 10-15% of dwelling value).
Effective Date
The date on which an insurance policy or bond goes into effect — and from which protection is furnished.
ELD (Electronic Logging Device)
Federally mandated device that automatically records driver hours of service. Replaced paper logs for most drivers in December 2019. Connects to vehicle engine to capture driving time automatically. Data is available to enforcement during roadside inspections.
Electronic Logging Device (ELD)
FMCSA-mandated electronic device that records driver hours of service. Replaced paper logs for most commercial drivers as of December 2019.
Elevation Certificate
A document prepared by a licensed surveyor showing the elevation of a structure relative to the base flood elevation. Required for flood insurance in high-risk zones and determines the premium.
Elevators and Escalators Liability
Liability coverage for bodily injury or property damage arising from the use of elevators or escalators operated, maintained, or controlled by the insured.
Emergency Action Plan (EAP)
An OSHA-required written plan that details what employees should do in case of emergency. Covers evacuation procedures, emergency contacts, critical operations, and accountability. Required for most employers with more than 10 employees.
Emergency Response Plan (ERP)
A documented plan describing specific actions to be taken during emergencies — natural disasters, evacuations, attacks, technology failures. Covers roles, responsibilities, communication protocols, evacuation routes, and recovery procedures. Required for many OSHA-regulated operations.
Employee Benefit Liability
Liability protection for an employer for claims arising from errors in administration of employee benefit plans — pension plans, group life, group health, group disability, and AD&D.
Employee Dishonesty (Fidelity)
Coverage for loss of money, securities, or property caused by dishonest acts of employees. A component of most commercial crime policies.
Employee Dishonesty Exclusion
Property and liability policies typically exclude losses from employee dishonesty (theft, embezzlement). Requires separate fidelity/crime coverage.
Employment Practices Liability (EPLI)
Coverage for employment-related claims — wrongful termination, discrimination, sexual harassment, retaliation, failure to hire or promote. Excluded from CGL. Most EPLI policies include claims by current, former, and prospective employees.
Employment-Related Practices Exclusion
Standard CGL policies exclude employment-related claims — discrimination, wrongful termination, harassment. Requires separate Employment Practices Liability Insurance (EPLI) coverage.
EMR / Experience Mod / X-Mod
The workers compensation experience modifier. A number that adjusts premium based on a business's claim history versus industry peers. Below 1.00 = better than average (discount); above 1.00 = worse than average (surcharge). Critical to many commercial contracts that require a Mod below 1.0.
Endorsement
A written amendment to a policy that adds, removes, or modifies coverage. Endorsements can be standardized (ISO endorsement forms) or manuscript (custom-drafted for a specific risk). Once attached to a policy, an endorsement becomes part of the contract and supersedes conflicting policy language.
Enterprise Risk Management (ERM)
An integrated approach that sees risk as an organization-wide issue to be managed across divisions and functions — not in silos. Frameworks include COSO ERM and ISO 31000.
Enterprise Risk Management (ERM) Maturity Model
A framework describing stages of ERM development — from ad-hoc risk management (isolated, reactive) to optimized (integrated, predictive, strategically aligned). Common maturity levels: Initial, Repeatable, Defined, Managed, Optimized.
Environmental Impairment Liability (EIL)
Site-specific pollution coverage for owned or operated premises — covers gradual and sudden pollution conditions, including unknown pre-existing conditions in some forms.
Environmental Pollution Liability
Liability coverage for bodily injury or property damage arising from acids, fumes, smoke, toxic chemicals, waste materials, or other pollutants.
EPA — Risk Assessment Portal ↗
The Environmental Protection Agency's central resource for risk assessment guidance — environmental hazards, human health risks, and ecological risks. Useful for pollution and environmental liability analysis.
Equipment Breakdown (Boiler & Machinery)
Coverage for loss or damage to equipment from mechanical or electrical breakdown. Covers the equipment itself plus resulting business income and extra expense. Essential for HVAC, production equipment, and electrical systems.
Ergonomics
The science of fitting the job to the worker to prevent musculoskeletal injuries from repetitive motion, awkward postures, heavy lifting, or vibration. A major focus of loss control in manual-intensive industries.
ERISA Bond
A fidelity bond required by ERISA for anyone handling employee benefit plan funds. Typically 10% of the plan assets handled (minimum $1,000, maximum $500,000 for most plans). Covers fraud and dishonesty — not the same as fiduciary liability.
Errors & Omissions (E&O) / Professional Liability
Coverage for professionals against claims of negligence, error, or omission in professional services — architects, engineers, consultants, accountants, insurance agents, real estate agents, IT professionals. Fills the professional services exclusion in CGL.
Errors & Omissions (E&O) Insurance — Agent's
Professional liability coverage for insurance agents and brokers against claims of negligence or errors in their services — failing to procure requested coverage, misquoting, errors in policy servicing, etc. Essential coverage for any insurance agency.
Estoppel
A legal principle that bars a party from asserting something contrary to what has been implied by its previous actions or statements. This is a form of "implied waiver"; for example, if the insurer regularly accepts late payments, it waives its right to deny coverage due to late payment.
Evaluation
The process of determining the approximate value of damages relevant to a claim (i.e., total of medical bills, cost of repair & replacement of damaged property) and considering the financial provisions of the insurance policy to decide on a fair settlement amount.
Event Cancellation Insurance
Coverage for financial loss because of the cancellation or postponement of a specific event due to weather or other unexpected cause beyond the control of the insured.
Excess & Surplus (E&S) Lines
The non-admitted market for coverage that the admitted market won't or can't write — high-risk accounts, unusual exposures, large limits, or new industries. More flexible pricing and terms but without state guaranty fund backing.
Excess Liability Policy
Similar to an umbrella but narrower — provides higher limits above a specific primary policy but does not drop down to cover gaps in the underlying. Typically written on a pure following-form basis.
Excess of Loss Reinsurance
Non-proportional reinsurance that responds when a loss exceeds the primary insurer's retention. The reinsurer pays the excess up to its limit. Used to protect against large individual losses or catastrophes.
Exclusion
Provision in an insurance policy that indicates what is denied coverage. Examples of Exclusions are: Wear and Tear, Rust, Rot, Contamination, Mechanical Breakdown.
Exclusions Section
The section listing what the policy does NOT cover. Exclusions exist for several reasons: to manage catastrophic exposures (war, nuclear), to avoid duplicating coverage available elsewhere (auto liability excluded from homeowners), to eliminate uninsurable risks (intentional acts), and to keep premiums reasonable by excluding unusual or high-cost exposures.
Exclusive Provider Organization (EPO)
A plan requiring in-network providers (no out-of-network coverage except emergencies) but not requiring PCP referrals. Middle ground between HMO and PPO.
Expense Ratio
The percentage of premium used to run the insurance operation — commissions, underwriting expenses, taxes, and general operating costs. Formula: Underwriting Expenses ÷ Written Premium.
Experience Modification Factor (EMR / Mod)
A multiplier that adjusts workers comp premium based on the insured's own three-year loss history versus the industry average. A mod of 1.00 is average. Below 1.00 (credit mod) means the employer's losses are better than average; above 1.00 (debit mod) means worse. Calculated annually by NCCI or state rating bureau.
Experience Rating
A rating plan that adjusts premium based on the insured's own past loss experience. Most common in workers compensation (the Mod) and commercial auto fleets.
Expiration Date
The date on which an insurance policy expires.
Expirations (Ownership of)
The right to solicit renewal of insurance accounts. In independent agency, the agency owns the expirations; in captive agency, the company typically owns them. Critical distinction affecting agency value and producer mobility.
Exposure (Underwriting)
Anything on or near a property that increases the risk of loss — a neighboring gas station, an adjacent vacant building, proximity to wildfire-prone areas, a chemical plant nearby. External exposures can make an otherwise good risk unacceptable.
Extended Period of Indemnity
An endorsement extending business income coverage past the time operations are physically restored, to cover the period needed to rebuild the customer base. Typical options: 30, 60, 90, 180, 365 days or more.
Extended Reporting Period (ERP / Tail)
A provision in claims-made policies allowing claims to be reported after policy expiration for occurrences during the policy period. Can be a short basic extended reporting period (60 days free) or a purchased supplemental ERP (sometimes permanent). Essential when switching from claims-made to occurrence, or when ceasing operations.
Extended Warranty
An additional form of protection that can be purchased to extend/enhance a manufacturer's warranty on consumer goods, like phones and electronics.
Extra Expense Coverage
Pays extraordinary costs incurred to continue operations after a covered property loss — renting temporary space, leasing temporary equipment, paying overtime, expedited shipping. Often sold together with business income or as a stand-alone coverage for businesses that can't afford any interruption.
Extra Expense Insurance
Property insurance for extraordinary expenses incurred to continue operations after a loss — for example, a backup generator rental during a power failure, or leasing temporary facilities.
F
Face Amount
The death benefit payable on the insured's death. Set when the policy is issued; can be adjusted up or down on some forms subject to underwriting. Does not include additional amounts from riders or dividend accumulations.
Face Value
The value printed on the face of a stock, bond, or other financial instrument or document.
Facultative Reinsurance
Reinsurance placed on an individual risk, with terms negotiated case-by-case. Used for risks outside treaty criteria or for additional coverage on large or unusual risks.
Fair Plan (FAIR Plan)
A state-mandated insurance pool that writes property coverage for applicants who cannot obtain coverage in the voluntary market due to high risk (e.g., wildfire-prone areas, older urban buildings). "Fair Access to Insurance Requirements."
Fall Protection
Systems to prevent or arrest falls from heights. OSHA requires fall protection when workers are 4' above lower levels (general industry) or 6' (construction). Options include guardrails, safety nets, and personal fall arrest systems.
Faller Certification
Many states require training and certification for professional fallers (tree cutters). Programs cover tree assessment, cut planning, escape routes, hinge-wood technique, dealing with lean and bind, and hung-up trees. Recertification periodically required.
FBI — Workplace Violence: Issues in Response ↗
FBI guidance on preventing and responding to workplace violence. Covers warning signs, threat assessment teams, response protocols, and post-incident support.
Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation (FDIC)
The U.S. corporation insuring deposits in US banks against bank failure.
Felling
The process of cutting down standing trees. Highest-risk activity in logging. Controlled felling techniques, proper notch and backcut, planned escape routes, and buddy system are core controls.
FEMA — Incremental Protection for Existing Commercial Buildings (FEMA 459) ↗
Describes cost-effective protective upgrades that can be added to existing buildings incrementally. Balances protection with practicality and cost.
FEMA — Multi-Hazard Mapping Information Platform ↗
A FEMA online tool (MHMIP) that maps multiple hazards — flood, earthquake, wildfire, hurricane — for any location in the U.S. Useful for underwriting, risk surveys, and site selection.
FEMA — Primer for Design of Commercial Buildings to Mitigate Terrorist Attacks (FEMA 427) ↗
A design-focused companion to FEMA 426 for architects and engineers of new commercial construction. Covers structural protection, blast mitigation, and integrated security design.
FEMA — Protect Your Property from High Winds ↗
FEMA publishes a series of free guides showing property owners how to reduce wind damage — roof strapping, impact-rated windows, garage door reinforcement, tree management. Useful reference for property underwriting and loss control in hurricane-exposed regions.
FEMA — Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426) ↗
FEMA's baseline reference for reducing building vulnerability to terrorist attacks. Covers site design, building hardening, and operational security. Foundational document for high-exposure commercial properties.
FEMA — Site and Urban Design for Security (FEMA 430) ↗
Guidance for planners and property owners on site-level security — setbacks, bollards, vehicle barriers, landscape design. Useful for evaluating access-control exposures on commercial accounts.
Fidelity Bond
Insurance purchased by an employer to protect against financial loss from dishonest acts by persons entrusted with valuable property or funds.
Fiduciary Liability
Coverage for claims against individuals who administer employee benefit plans for breaches of fiduciary duty under ERISA. Distinct from ERISA bond (which covers fraud/dishonesty). Protects HR executives, benefits administrators, plan trustees.
Fiduciary Liability Insurance
Coverage for claims against those who manage employee benefit plans for breaches of fiduciary duty under ERISA. Separate from D&O and crime coverage.
File-and-Use Law
A state rate regulation allowing insurers to use new rates immediately upon filing them with the insurance department, subject to later review and potential disapproval.
Financial Examination
A state regulatory review of an insurer's financial condition — reserves, capital, investment quality, reinsurance, governance. Typically conducted every 3-5 years. Shared across states via NAIC coordination.
Financial Responsibility Law
A state statute requiring motorists to show financial capacity to pay for automobile-related losses — typically satisfied by carrying auto liability insurance at state-required minimums.
Financial Risk
Risk related to an organization's financial structure and markets — credit risk, liquidity risk, interest rate risk, foreign exchange risk, and market risk. Managed through capital structure decisions, hedging, diversification, and cash reserves.
Fire Alarm System
Detection (smoke detectors, heat detectors, flame detectors), notification (horns, strobes, public address), and monitoring (central station connection). Testing and maintenance per NFPA 72 is required for insurance credit and code compliance.
Fire Extinguisher Classes
Class A (ordinary combustibles — wood, paper, cloth), Class B (flammable liquids/gases), Class C (electrical equipment), Class D (combustible metals), Class K (cooking oils). Most commercial extinguishers are ABC (multi-purpose dry chemical). Restaurants need Class K for fryers.
Fire Insurance
Coverage for loss of or damage to a building and/or contents due to fire or lightning. Often includes business interruption and loss of rents.
Fire Legal Liability
Coverage for property loss liability resulting from negligent acts or omissions of the insured that allow a fire to spread and cause damage to others' property — common for tenants who may damage a landlord's building.
Fire Pump
A pump that increases water pressure to sprinkler or standpipe systems when municipal pressure is insufficient. Driven by electric motor or diesel engine. Must be tested weekly (no-flow/churn test) and annually (full flow test). Failure to maintain can compromise sprinkler effectiveness.
Fire-Resistive Construction
ISO Construction Class 6. Structural components have specific fire-resistance ratings (typically 2+ hours). Most favorable property rates.
First Named Insured
The first person or entity named on the declarations page of a policy.
First Notice of Loss (FNOL)
The initial report of a loss or claim to the insurer. Should be made promptly — policies typically require "prompt" notice, and delay can prejudice coverage. Agencies typically assist by gathering initial information and filing the FNOL with the carrier.
First Party Claim
A claim that a policyholder files with her own insurer (as opposed to a third party claim, which the claimant files with someone else's insurer).
First Party Loss
A situation involving only the insurer and insured.
First Step in Risk Management Decision Process
Analyze loss exposures — identifying what the organization could lose, how it could happen, and what the financial consequences would be.
First-Party Cyber Coverages
Cover losses to the insured's own business: data restoration costs, business income from cyber events, cyber extortion (ransomware payments and response costs), notification expenses (notifying affected individuals of a breach), credit monitoring for affected individuals, regulatory fines and penalties, and forensics/investigation costs.
Flat Cancellation
Cancellation of a policy effective back to its inception date, with a full premium refund. Treats the policy as if it never existed.
Fleet Safety Program
Structured program to reduce auto losses in a business with multiple vehicles. Core elements: driver selection criteria (MVR review, background check, pre-employment drug test), driver qualification file, ongoing MVR monitoring, defensive driver training, pre-trip inspection protocol, accident investigation, disciplinary action, and ongoing safety communication.
Flexible Spending Account (FSA)
An employer-sponsored tax-advantaged account for medical expenses. Contributions are pre-tax; withdrawals for qualifying expenses are tax-free. Funds typically do NOT roll over year to year (use it or lose it), though some plans allow a small carryover.
Flood Coverage
Generally excluded from standard property policies. Available through the National Flood Insurance Program (NFIP) or private flood insurers. Has a waiting period (typically 30 days) unless purchased with a mortgage closing.
Flood Exclusion
Standard property policies exclude flood damage. Must be covered separately through NFIP or private flood insurers. One of the most common and costly coverage gaps.
Flood Insurance
Coverage protecting the insured against loss or damage to real or personal property from flood. Flood is typically excluded from standard property policies and must be purchased separately, usually through the National Flood Insurance Program (NFIP).
Flood Zone
A FEMA-designated area based on flood risk. Zone A and V are high-risk (Special Flood Hazard Area / SFHA); Zone X is moderate or minimal risk. Mortgage lenders require flood insurance in SFHAs.
FMCSA (Federal Motor Carrier Safety Administration)
The federal agency within the Department of Transportation that regulates interstate commercial motor carriers. Issues safety regulations, investigates carriers, and tracks safety performance.
FMCSA Clearinghouse
A federal database of drug and alcohol violations by commercial drivers. Employers must query the Clearinghouse before hiring and annually for current drivers.
FMCSA Drug & Alcohol Clearinghouse
Federal database of drug/alcohol program violations by commercial drivers. Employers must query pre-employment and annually for current drivers. Drivers with unresolved violations cannot operate commercial vehicles.
FMEA (Failure Mode and Effects Analysis)
A systematic method for identifying potential failures in a process or system and evaluating their effects. Each failure mode is scored for severity, occurrence, and detection; the product is a Risk Priority Number (RPN) used to prioritize corrective action.
Following Form
An excess liability policy that tracks the terms of the underlying primary policy. Provides consistent coverage terms across primary and excess layers without gaps.
Forced Retention
Retention of a risk that occurs because insurance is unavailable, too expensive, or the exposure was not identified before the loss.
Forecasting in Risk Management
Establishing priorities by predicting frequency and severity of potential losses and estimating the costs of different risk management techniques.
Forestry Code (State-Specific)
State forestry regulations (Oregon OAR 437, Washington WAC 296-54, California 8 CCR 6250, Idaho IDAPA 17, Maine 12 MRSA) that govern logging safety, worker training, equipment standards, and environmental protection.
Forestry Mulching
Using a specialized machine to grind vegetation in place rather than removing it. Different hazard profile than traditional logging — thrown debris, machine rollover on slopes.
Form MCS-90
The federal endorsement to a motor carrier's liability policy that guarantees payment of public liability (bodily injury, property damage, environmental) judgments up to statutory minimums — even if the underlying policy would otherwise exclude coverage. Required for for-hire interstate carriers.
Form Regulation
State approval of policy forms (the actual contract language). Required for most personal lines and most commercial lines; commercial forms for large accounts and surplus lines are often exempt.
Fortuity
The principle that insurance covers only uncertain, fortuitous events — not known losses or inevitable events. Foundational to how insurance contracts are interpreted.
Four Requirements of a Legal Contract
Agreement, Consideration, Competent Parties, and Legal Purpose.
Four Types of Auto Insurance
Collision, Comprehensive, Medical, and Liability.
Four Types of Business Risk
Strategic, Operational, Reputational, and Compliance.
FR-44 Filing
Similar to SR-22 but required in Florida and Virginia with higher minimum liability limits. Typically required after DUI convictions.
Frame Construction
ISO Construction Class 1. Wood-framed buildings. Highest fire loss potential; highest rates.
Fraternal Benefit Societies
Non-profit, mutual aid organizations that engage primarily in charitable or benevolent activities. They offer their members insurance against death, disease, and disability. Also known as Fraternal Associations.
Fraud
Fraud is any intentional lying or misrepresentation by policyholders or claim adjusters in order to inflate a claims payment or receive a claims payment that would otherwise not be paid.
Fraud — Hard
Deliberately faking or causing an incident in order to collect money.
Fraud — Soft
Inflating the gravity or the extent of claims in order to receive a higher indemnification.
FSA
Flexible Spending Account. An account set up through an employer to cover health costs with pretax dollars that has very little or no rollover from year to year.
Full Coverage (Auto)
Not a specific coverage — an informal term usually meaning liability + collision + comprehensive. Often also includes medical payments and uninsured/underinsured motorist. "Full coverage" does NOT mean unlimited coverage.
Full-Scale Exercise
A full operational drill that tests all aspects of a plan with actual resource deployment — emergency teams, evacuation, drills with local responders. The highest-fidelity test of preparedness.
Functional Exercise
A more advanced drill that tests specific functions in a simulated environment — activating emergency communications, running through evacuation, testing backup systems. More realistic than TTX but still without full physical deployment.
G
Gap Coverage
Coverage that pays the difference between what you owe on a vehicle loan and the vehicle's actual cash value if the vehicle is totaled. Prevents the borrower from owing money on a car they no longer have. Especially important for new vehicles that depreciate rapidly.
Garage Coverage Form
Designed to cover dealerships, auto repair shops, and garages for day-to-day operations. Includes auto, truck, motorcycle, RV, truck-tractor, and trailer dealers. Includes liability (casualty), garage keepers (property), and physical damage (property) coverage. UM, UIM, and medical payments may be added by endorsement.
Garage Keepers Coverage
Covers damage to customers' property that the insured has for servicing, repair, parking, or storage.
Garage Liability Coverage
Covers both auto and business liability arising out of: (1) the ownership, maintenance, or use of covered autos; (2) garage operations (e.g., a customer falling at the insured's premises).
Garage Physical Damage Coverage
Comprehensive, specified causes of loss, and collision. Typically auto dealers purchase physical damage coverage on a blanket basis.
Garagekeepers Legal Liability
Coverage for customers' vehicles in the care, custody, or control of an insured garage operation (repair shop, body shop, valet service). Covers damage to customer vehicles from fire, theft, collision, and vandalism while at the insured's premises.
GDPR (General Data Protection Regulation)
European Union regulation on data protection and privacy. Applies to any organization processing data about EU residents. Includes breach notification requirements, data subject rights, and significant penalties for violations.
General Aggregate Limit
The maximum a CGL policy will pay for all covered claims during the policy period, except those under the products/completed operations aggregate. Typically 2x the per-occurrence limit.
General Liability Insurance
Covers bodily injury or property damage caused during business operations. Covers incidents on the business premises, slip-and-fall accidents, damage to customer property, and advertising injury. Sometimes called Commercial General Liability (CGL).
Generally Accepted Accounting Principles (GAAP)
The accounting rules most companies use for general financial reporting. Insurers use GAAP for SEC filings and SAP for state regulators. GAAP is more focused on matching revenue to expenses in the period earned.
Georgia Minimum Auto Liability Limits
25/50/25 — $25,000 bodily injury per person, $50,000 bodily injury per accident, $25,000 property damage per accident. Minimum legal limits; most agents recommend higher (100/300/100 is common) to protect personal assets.
Georgia Required Auto Liability Limits
25/50/25 — $25,000 bodily injury per person, $50,000 bodily injury per accident, $25,000 property damage per accident.
Good Driver Discount
A discount offered to drivers who have been licensed for a specified period (often 3+ years), have minimal violations on their record, and have not been found at fault in accidents causing bodily injury. Required in some states.
Good Faith
Acting honestly and fairly, with good intentions and full disclosure.
Government Insurance
An insurance program where risks are transferred to a government agency. Also known as social insurance. It usually exists in situations where the exposure to loss is too high for private insurers to reliably insure risks.
Grace Period
A specified period immediately following the premium due date during which a payment can be made to continue a policy in force without interruption. Applies only to life and health policies. Check your policy for specifics.
Gross Premium (Price of Insurance)
The total cost of an insurance policy. Formula: Expected Losses + Risk Charge + Administrative Loading.
Group Captive — Tax Advantage
Ability to write off premiums as a true risk transfer, provided the parent's own risk constitutes no more than 30% of the total risk portfolio.
Guaranty Fund
A state-operated fund that pays claims of insolvent insurers. Funded through assessments on licensed (admitted) insurers. The reason buying from an admitted insurer provides extra consumer protection.
H
Hand Crew / Hand Fallers
Workers who fell trees with chainsaws rather than mechanized equipment. Highest exposure to struck-by hazards; required to complete state-certified training in many jurisdictions.
Hard Market
A period in the insurance cycle characterized by high demand and low supply — premiums rise, underwriting tightens, coverage becomes harder to place.
Hartford Steam Boiler — Equipment Start-Up, Shutdown & Maintenance Fact Sheets ↗
Technical guidance from Hartford Steam Boiler Inspection and Insurance Company on safe operation and maintenance of boilers, pressure vessels, and industrial equipment. Core reference for equipment breakdown loss control.
Hazard
A condition that increases the likelihood or severity of a loss from a peril. Physical hazards (icy walkway), moral hazards (dishonesty), and morale hazards (carelessness).
Hazard Assessment
Evaluating identified hazards to determine their potential severity and likelihood of causing loss. Prioritizes hazards for treatment based on risk ranking (probability × severity).
Hazard Communication Standard (HazCom)
OSHA's "right to know" standard requiring employers to inform workers about chemical hazards in the workplace through labels, Safety Data Sheets, and training. Also called the Globally Harmonized System (GHS) after the 2012 update.
Hazard Control
Actions taken to eliminate or reduce hazards. Follows the hierarchy of controls: elimination first, then substitution, engineering controls, administrative controls, and PPE last.
Hazard Identification
The process of finding hazards in the workplace — walk-throughs, employee interviews, incident analysis, job hazard analysis, SDS review, and external references (OSHA standards, industry benchmarks). The first step of any hazard management program.
Hazard Information Sheets ↗
Condensed reference materials covering specific hazards — active shooter, avalanche, cyberattack, earthquake, extreme heat, financial emergency, flood, hurricane, landslide, pandemic, power outage, thunderstorm, tornado, tsunami, volcano, wildfire, winter storm. Free downloadable sheets are published by FEMA and Ready.gov.
Hazard Management (General)
The systematic process of identifying hazards, assessing their likelihood and severity, implementing controls to prevent or mitigate them, and monitoring results over time. Many workplace hazards — accidents, chemical spills, equipment breakdown, fires — can be prevented through this structured approach.
Hazard — Legal
Any legal action that increases the chance of a loss.
Hazard — Moral
Conscious and deliberate action on the part of the insured that increases the chance of a loss. All conscious acts committed with a hope to defraud the insurance company are moral hazards.
Hazard — Morale
Unconscious tendency of insured people to engage in riskier behavior.
Hazard — Physical
Any physical condition that increases the chance of loss.
Health Insurance Deductible
The amount the insured pays each year before the plan begins paying. After the deductible is met, the plan shares costs through copays or coinsurance until the out-of-pocket maximum is reached. Higher deductibles = lower premiums.
Health Insurance Overview
Coverage for medical expenses. May be purchased individually or through group plans (employer-based). Coverage is subject to deductibles, copays, coinsurance, and out-of-pocket maximums. Key coverage categories: hospital, surgical, physician, prescription drug, preventive care, mental health, maternity.
Health Maintenance Organization (HMO)
A managed care plan requiring members to use a network of approved providers (except emergencies). Members typically choose a Primary Care Physician (PCP) who coordinates care and refers to specialists. Lowest premiums among managed care options; least provider flexibility.
Health Savings Account (HSA)
A tax-advantaged savings account available only to people enrolled in an HDHP. Contributions are tax-deductible; growth is tax-free; withdrawals for qualifying medical expenses are tax-free. Funds roll over year to year. Effectively a triple tax benefit.
Hierarchy of Controls
The preferred order for controlling workplace hazards, from most to least effective: (1) Elimination — remove the hazard entirely; (2) Substitution — replace with something safer; (3) Engineering controls — isolate people from the hazard; (4) Administrative controls — change how people work; (5) PPE — personal protective equipment as the last line of defense.
Hierarchy of Hazards
A categorization of workplace hazards commonly used in risk assessment: physical, chemical, biological, ergonomic, and psychosocial. Each requires different controls.
High Deductible Health Plan (HDHP)
A plan with a high deductible (set annually by the IRS — for 2024: at least $1,600 individual / $3,200 family) and lower premium. Qualifies the insured to contribute to a Health Savings Account (HSA).
HIPAA Security Rule
Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act rules requiring safeguards for electronic protected health information (ePHI). Applies to healthcare providers, health plans, and their business associates.
HMO
Health Maintenance Organization. A health insurance plan in which patients receive health care from designated providers.
HO-3 Policy
The most common homeowners policy form. Covers the dwelling on an open-perils (all risk) basis and personal property on a named-perils basis. The "standard" coverage for single-family owner-occupied homes.
HO-3 Special Form (Most Common)
The most commonly written homeowners form. Covers the dwelling and other structures on an open-perils basis (all causes of loss except exclusions). Covers personal property on a named-perils basis (16 listed perils). A balanced form suitable for most owner-occupied single-family homes.
HO-4 Policy (Renters)
Homeowners form for renters. Covers personal property, personal liability, and additional living expenses. Does NOT cover the structure itself, which is the landlord's responsibility.
HO-4 Renters / Tenants Form
Designed for renters. Covers personal property, personal liability, medical payments, and additional living expenses. Does NOT cover the structure — that is the landlord's responsibility. Renters insurance is inexpensive and strongly recommended.
HO-5 Comprehensive Form
Covers both the dwelling AND personal property on an open-perils basis. Broader contents coverage than HO-3 — no need to prove the cause of loss was a named peril. More expensive but recommended for higher-value homes and contents.
HO-5 Policy
A more comprehensive homeowners form that covers BOTH the dwelling AND personal property on an open-perils basis. More expensive than HO-3 but provides stronger contents protection. Often the recommended form for higher-value homes.
HO-6 Condominium Unit Owners
Designed for condo owners. Covers interior improvements and betterments (flooring, cabinetry, fixtures inside the unit), personal property, personal liability, and loss assessment from the condo association. The exterior and common areas are covered by the master policy purchased by the association.
HO-6 Policy (Condo Owners)
Homeowners form for condominium unit owners. Covers interior fixtures and improvements, personal property, personal liability, and loss assessment. The condo association's master policy covers the exterior structure and common areas.
HO-8 Policy (Older Homes)
Homeowners form designed for older homes where replacement cost would far exceed market value. Provides "functional replacement" coverage rather than full replacement cost — replaces damaged materials with modern equivalents rather than matching ornate original features.
HO-A
A type of Homeowner's policy that provides coverage to both Coverage A (Structure) and Coverage B (Personal Property) on a Named Peril basis.
HO-B
A type of homeowner's policy that provides coverage to Coverage A (Structure) for All Risks of Physical Loss, unless specifically excluded, and to Coverage B (Personal Property) on a Named Peril basis.
Hold Harmless Agreement
A contract provision where one party agrees to bear financial responsibility for specified losses to another party. Often combined with an indemnification clause. Some states (e.g., via anti-indemnity statutes) limit enforceability of broad hold-harmless clauses in construction contracts.
Home-Sharing Endorsement
An endorsement covering occasional short-term rental (Airbnb, VRBO) of a home. Standard homeowners policies typically exclude commercial use; this endorsement adds coverage for qualifying rental activity.
Homeowners Coverage A — Dwelling
Coverage for the physical structure of the main home, including attached structures (attached garage, deck). Written at a limit equal to the cost to rebuild. Open-perils on HO-3 and HO-5 (all risks except exclusions); named perils on HO-4 and HO-8.
Homeowners Coverage B — Other Structures
Coverage for detached structures on the residence premises — detached garages, sheds, fences, gazebos, pools. Written as a percentage of Coverage A (typically 10%). Can be increased by endorsement if the detached structures are valuable.
Homeowners Coverage C — Personal Property
Coverage for the insured's belongings — furniture, clothing, electronics, appliances. Typically 50-70% of Coverage A, but can be increased. Coverage applies anywhere in the world, though off-premises coverage is usually sub-limited. Named perils on HO-3 and HO-4; open perils on HO-5.
Homeowners Coverage D — Loss of Use
Coverage for additional living expenses when the home is uninhabitable due to a covered loss. Pays hotel, rental housing, additional food costs above normal, and other costs needed to maintain the insured's standard of living while the home is being repaired. Typically 20-30% of Coverage A.
Homeowners Coverage E — Personal Liability
Liability coverage for the insured's personal activities and premises. Covers bodily injury and property damage to third parties for which the insured is legally responsible — dog bites, slip-and-fall on property, damage caused away from home, and accidental property damage. Typically $100,000-$500,000.
Homeowners Coverage F — Medical Payments to Others
A small-limit coverage (typically $1,000-$5,000) paying medical expenses for guests injured on the premises or by the insured's activities, regardless of fault. A "goodwill" coverage designed to pay minor medical bills to discourage lawsuits.
Homeowners Insurance
A form of property insurance, available to property owners, that covers losses to the physical structure as well as to their personal property and protects the insured person from liability claims.
Host Liquor Liability
Coverage within the CGL for business liability arising from serving alcohol at occasional events (company parties, client functions) by a business not in the liquor business. Liquor Liability (separate coverage) is required for businesses that manufacture, distribute, sell, or serve alcohol for profit.
Hot Work Permit
A document issued before cutting or welding, listing required precautions: removal of combustibles, fire extinguisher present, fire watch during and 30+ minutes after work. Signed by a supervisor.
Hot Work Permit Program
Written program controlling cutting, welding, brazing, soldering, and grinding. Requires a written permit, fire watch (30+ minutes after work completion), removal/shielding of combustibles within 35 feet, fire extinguisher present, and supervisor sign-off. Hot work is a leading cause of industrial fires.
Hot Work Program
A written program controlling cutting, welding, brazing, grinding, and other activities that generate heat or sparks. Includes hot work permits, fire watch, and area preparation. Required to prevent fires — a leading cause of industrial losses.
Hours of Service (HOS)
FMCSA regulations limiting the hours commercial drivers can operate to reduce fatigue-related crashes. Includes the 11-hour driving limit, 14-hour on-duty window, 30-minute break requirement, and 60/70-hour weekly limits.
Hours of Service (HOS) Rules
Federal limits on how long a commercial driver can operate. Key limits: 11 hours of driving after 10 hours off-duty, 14-hour on-duty window after 10 hours off, 30-minute break after 8 hours of driving, 60/70-hour weekly limits (60 in 7 days or 70 in 8 days depending on carrier operation).
Housekeeping
The practice of maintaining an orderly, clean workplace. Critical for fire safety (removing combustibles), slip-and-fall prevention, and efficient operations. A common loss-control recommendation.
How Insurance Works
Insurance is a contract-based mechanism for transferring risk. A policyholder pays a premium to an insurance company in exchange for the insurer's promise to pay for certain losses if they occur. The insurer collects premiums from a large number of policyholders, invests those premiums, and uses the pool of funds to pay claims. Because losses are statistically predictable across large groups even though unpredictable for any one individual, the insurer can charge a premium that reflects the expected cost of claims plus expenses plus a profit margin.
HSA
Health Savings Account. An account where you contribute pre-tax dollars for expected medical expenses.
Hull Insurance
Coverage for damage to a vessel (ship, boat, or aircraft) and affixed items. The marine equivalent of property damage coverage for the vehicle itself.
Hung-Up Tree
A felled tree that has not fallen fully to the ground because it was caught on another tree. Extremely dangerous; requires specialized release techniques. Never climbed or worked under.
I
IFTA (International Fuel Tax Agreement)
A compact among U.S. states and Canadian provinces that simplifies fuel tax reporting for motor carriers operating across jurisdictions. Carriers report once and taxes are allocated to states.
Impact (Risk)
The possible effect on the organization if a risk event occurs. Measured in dollars, lost operational capacity, reputation damage, or other terms depending on the risk.
Implementation of Risk Management Techniques
Involves both technical decisions (which controls to install, what coverage to buy) and managerial decisions (budget, staffing, communication).
Incident Command System (ICS) ↗
A component of NIMS that provides a standardized hierarchical structure for managing incidents. Used during emergencies to establish clear chains of command, coordinate resources, and manage communications. Businesses can use ICS to manage disruptions, public information, and crisis communications.
Incident Investigation
The structured process of examining an accident, injury, or near miss to determine what happened, why it happened, and what should be done to prevent recurrence.
Incident Management
The coordinated process of responding to and managing an incident — from initial detection through recovery. Required for any organization with emergency response teams or interfaces with public emergency services.
Incontestability Clause
A life insurance and annuity provision limiting the time within which the insurer has the legal right to void the contract on grounds of material misrepresentation in the application — usually two years.
Incurred But Not Reported (IBNR)
Claims that have occurred but the insurer has not yet been notified of them at the reporting date. Insurers maintain reserves for IBNR to fund future claim payments. Critical for accurate financial reporting.
Incurred Expense
Expenses that have already been sustained and that have not yet been paid. Additional Living Expenses payments are a reimbursement of Incurred Expenses.
Indemnification
Reimbursement for a loss, which leaves the claimant in the same financial position that she was in before the loss.
Indemnification Clause (Contract)
A provision requiring one party (the indemnitor) to compensate another (the indemnitee) for specified losses. Three common scope levels: limited (indemnitor covers only its own negligence); intermediate (covers indemnitor's and shared negligence); broad (covers indemnitee's sole negligence). Enforceability varies by state and contract type.
Indemnify
To restore by payment, repair, or replacement.
Indemnity Insurance
Insurance that indemnifies loss as opposed to liability.
Indemnity, Principle of
A general legal principle that an individual recovering under an insurance policy should be restored to approximately the same financial position they were in prior to the loss — not profit from the loss.
Independent Adjuster
A contract adjuster retained by the insurer to handle specific claims. Common during catastrophe situations when staff adjuster capacity is overwhelmed. Still represents the insurer.
Independent Agent
A representative of multiple insurance companies who sells and services policies. Owns the book of business and operates under the American Agency System.
Individual Named Insured Endorsement
Extends personal auto coverage to immediate family members of the named insured.
Industrial Hygiene
The science of recognizing, evaluating, and controlling occupational health hazards — noise, chemicals, biological agents, ergonomic stressors. Industrial hygienists conduct air sampling, noise monitoring, and ergonomic assessments.
Inflation Guard Endorsement
Automatically increases coverage limits over the policy period by a stated percentage to keep pace with inflation. Important because construction costs have risen significantly — static limits leave policyholders underinsured at renewal.
Ingress/Egress Coverage
Pays lost business income when physical access to the insured's premises is blocked by a covered cause of loss, even when the premises itself is not damaged.
Inland Marine Insurance
Coverage for property that may be in transit, held by a bailee, at a fixed location, a movable good often at different locations (e.g., off-road construction equipment), or scheduled property (antiques, fine art). Also covers instrumentalities of transportation and communication such as bridges, tunnels, piers, pipelines, and radio/TV towers.
Installation Floater
Coverage for materials, equipment, and fixtures being installed at a job site. Covers in transit, at temporary storage, and at the job site until installation is complete and the item is accepted by the owner.
Insurable Interest
A right or relationship with the subject matter of the insurance such that the insured can suffer a financial loss from damage to, or destruction of, it. Required for an insurance contract to be legally valid — you cannot insure something you do not have a stake in.
Insurance
An agreement where you make regular payments to a company and the company promises to pay you money if you suffer a specified injury, illness, loss, etc.
Insurance Agent
An employee of an insurance company who sells insurance policies and provides customer service.
Insurance Agent E&O
Professional liability for insurance agents and brokers. Covers failure to procure requested coverage, misquoting, failure to explain exclusions, misrepresentation of coverage. Essential for any agency — E&O claims can exceed an agency's net worth.
Insurance Commissioner
The head of a state's department of insurance. Elected in some states, appointed in others. Responsible for enforcing the state's insurance code, licensing, and consumer protection.
Insurance Company
Company which sells insurance policies to individuals or to other companies.
Insurance Fraud
An act on the part of either the buyer or seller of an insurance contract in an attempt to gain money illegally.
Insurance Policy
A contract in which an individual or entity receives financial protection or reimbursement against losses.
Insurance — Uncertainties as a Risk Financing Method
Inadequate policy limits, disagreements with insurers over coverage, delays in claim payment, and insurer insolvency.
Insurance-Linked Securities (ILS)
Financial instruments that transfer insurance risk to capital markets investors. Catastrophe bonds ("cat bonds") are the largest category — bonds that pay normal interest unless a defined catastrophe loss occurs, in which case the principal is paid to the insurer.
Insured
The party to an insurance arrangement whom the insurer agrees to indemnify for losses, provide benefits for, or render services.
Insurer
The insurance company.
Insurer — Admitted
An insurer licensed by the state to sell specific lines of insurance within the state (cannot sell surplus lines insurance). Also referred to as an Authorized Insurer.
Insurer — Alien
An insurer that adheres to the laws of another country besides the U.S. and its territories.
Insurer — Domestic
An insurer that adheres to the laws of a particular state and is housed in that state.
Insurer — Foreign
An insurer that has permission to issue policies in a state, but is not housed in that state.
Insurer — Illegitimate/Fraudulent
Nonadmitted (or unauthorized) insurers that do not have permission to sell surplus lines insurance.
Insurer — Nonadmitted
An insurer not licensed in the state (can sometimes sell surplus lines insurance). Also referred to as an Unauthorized Insurer.
Insuring Agreement
The section where the insurer promises what it will do. For a property policy, it's the promise to pay for damage to covered property from covered perils. For a liability policy, it's the promise to defend the insured and pay damages the insured is legally obligated to pay.
Intangible Property
Property that consists of legal rights rather than physical objects, such as securities, trademarks, copyrights, patents, and licenses.
Intentional Acts Exclusion
Liability policies exclude losses caused by intentional acts of the insured. Protects the insurance pool from being used to fund deliberate harm. Important in coverage disputes when the insurer alleges intentional misconduct.
Interest (Insurable)
Direct financial interest in protecting a unit.
Intervening Cause
A separate occurrence that "intervenes" between a defendant's actions and damage or loss to another person. An intervening cause may decrease the defendant's liability, if the defendant can show that it was the intervening cause (rather than the defendant himself) that caused the damage or loss.
IRP (International Registration Plan)
A reciprocal agreement that allows apportioned registration of commercial vehicles based on miles operated in each jurisdiction. One plate and cab card covers all participating jurisdictions.
Irrevocable Beneficiary
A life insurance beneficiary who has a vested interest in the policy proceeds even during the insured's lifetime. The policyholder may only change the beneficiary with the beneficiary's consent.
ISO (Insurance Services Office)
An advisory organization that develops standard insurance policy forms (such as the Commercial General Liability form), provides rating information, and compiles industry loss statistics. Most U.S. commercial insurers use ISO forms or close variations.
ISO 31000
International standard for risk management. Proactive and applicable to a broad range of organizations; maximizes value across a range of organizational stakeholders. Defines risk as the effect of uncertainty on objectives (positive OR negative).
ISO 31000:2018
The international standard for risk management. Provides principles, a framework, and a process applicable to any organization regardless of size, industry, or sector. Defines risk as "the effect of uncertainty on objectives" — encompassing both positive and negative outcomes.
ISO Definition — Risk
"The effect of uncertainty on objectives." This definition embraces both positive (upside) and negative (downside) outcomes.
ISO Definition — Risk Management
"Coordinated activities to direct and control an organization with regard to risk."
ISO/IEC 27001
International standard for information security management systems (ISMS). Provides requirements for establishing, implementing, maintaining, and improving an ISMS. Certification indicates a robust information security program.
J
Job Hazard Analysis (JHA) / Job Safety Analysis (JSA)
A technique that breaks a job down into its basic steps, identifies hazards at each step, and develops controls. Foundational tool in industrial safety and required for many OSHA-regulated operations.
K
Key Risk Indicator (KRI)
A metric that signals when risk exposure may be increasing. KRIs are forward-looking — they help management spot emerging risks before they cause losses.
Key-Person Insurance
A life (and sometimes disability) policy purchased by a business on the life of a person integral to its operations — a founder, top executive, or star salesperson. The business is both the owner and beneficiary.
Kidnap and Ransom Insurance
Coverage for ransom or extortion costs and related expenses when an insured person or employee is kidnapped or the business is extorted.
Known Known
A risk that is expected and involves little uncertainty — for example, employee injuries at a factory. Managed with routine controls and workers' compensation.
Known Loss Rule
A principle that an insurer will not cover losses the insured knew had already occurred before the policy took effect. Often stated in applications ("Do you know of any circumstance...?").
Known Unknown
A risk that is known to exist but whose probability or impact is uncertain — for example, knowing that a hurricane could strike but not knowing when or how severe.
L
Lagging Indicators
Backward-looking metrics measuring actual loss performance — injury rates, claim counts, lost workdays, OSHA recordable incidents. Useful for measuring results but cannot prevent the losses they measure.
Landing
The area where logs are gathered, sorted, and loaded onto trucks. Hazards include struck-by (log movement, equipment), caught-between, and traffic exposure.
Lapse
Termination of a policy due to failure to pay the required renewal premium.
Large Loss Principle
A principle of insurance: it is more efficient to insure against large, infrequent losses than small, predictable ones. Administrative cost of small claims often exceeds the benefit.
Large Number of Similar Objects
One of the requirements of an insurable risk — insurers need a large pool of similar exposures to accurately predict losses. Example: a life insurer selling only to one age bracket and health class to get reliable statistics.
Law of Large Numbers
The statistical principle that makes insurance possible. As the number of similar risk exposures grows, the actual loss experience converges toward the expected (predicted) loss experience. A life insurer with one policyholder cannot reliably predict when that person will die. A life insurer with one million similar policyholders can predict next year's total death claims with high accuracy.
Leading Indicators
Forward-looking metrics that predict future safety performance — percentage of employees trained, number of safety inspections completed, near-miss reports filed. Allow intervention before losses occur.
Legal Action Against Insurer
The insured cannot take legal action against the insurance company unless the insured has fully complied with all terms of the policy.
Legal Purpose
One of the four qualifications of a legally binding contract. The purpose of the contract must be legal (e.g., no contracts for illicit drug deals).
Legal Risk
A type of compliance risk that occurs when a company breaks the government's rules for companies. Companies facing legal risks may be exposed to expensive lawsuits, criminal penalties, injunctions, or loss of operating licenses. Mitigated through legal counsel, contract review, and proactive compliance programs.
Liability
Responsibility to another for one's negligence.
Liability Coverage
Auto insurance that protects you against medical costs for the bodily injury of others and damages to the property of others if you are at fault in an accident.
Liability Insurance
Liability insurance indemnifies a third party for damages caused by the insured's negligence.
Liability Loss — Financial Consequences
Legal actions against the insured, including investigation costs, defense costs, and damages (general and special).
Life Insurance
Insurance paid to named beneficiaries when the insured person dies.
Limit
The limits of an insurance policy represent the highest amount an insurer will pay. Any cost above the limits of insurance is the responsibility of the policyholder.
Limit — Aggregate
The total amount the insurer will pay for all losses during the policy period. Often appears preceded by an occurrence limit (e.g., 100,000/1,000,000). The first number indicates the maximum payout for covered losses in any single occurrence; the second number indicates the total amount the insurer will pay for all covered occurrences during the policy term.
Limit — Single
Establishes one all-inclusive limit, or maximum payout, for liability damages caused by the policyholder. Contrast with split limits.
Limit — Split
An insurance provision that specifies different coverage limits for different parts of a claim. The most common example is split limits in a personal auto policy, which includes three different limits of liability: (1) maximum limit for bodily injury for any one individual, (2) maximum limit for bodily injury to multiple people in any one accident or occurrence, and (3) maximum limit for property damage in any one accident or occurrence.
Liquor Liability Insurance
Coverage for an entity involved in the retail or wholesale sale of alcoholic beverages, or the serving of alcoholic beverages, to persons who have incurred bodily injury or property damage arising from an intoxicated person. Also called "dram shop" coverage.
Loader Safety (Log Landing)
Log loaders at landing areas present struck-by and caught-between hazards. Workers on foot must stay outside the swing radius; operators must verify no one is in the path before moving. Hard hats and high-visibility clothing are essential.
Loan Value
The amount that can be borrowed at a specified interest rate from the issuing company by a life insurance policyholder, using the policy's cash value as collateral. If the policyholder dies with the loan outstanding, the amount borrowed plus interest is deducted from the death benefit.
Lockout / Tagout (LOTO)
OSHA-required procedure for controlling hazardous energy during equipment maintenance. Workers apply physical locks and warning tags to prevent unexpected startup that could cause injury or death.
Log Loader
A mobile crane or grapple used to load logs onto trucks at the landing. Requires operator certification and daily pre-use inspection.
Log Truck Cargo Insurance
Specialty motor truck cargo coverage for trucks hauling logs. Addresses unique risks: cargo slip-offs, binder failures, bridge weight overloads, and extended exposures on private forestry roads. Usually written by specialty programs.
Logger's Broad Form
A specialty inland marine coverage designed for logging operations. Covers standing timber, felled timber, logs in transit from the forest to the mill, and certain logging equipment. Different from standard property coverage because of the unique exposures of forestry operations.
Logging Operations (General)
The high-hazard industry involving felling, bucking, yarding, loading, and hauling timber. Subject to specialized OSHA standard 29 CFR 1910.266 and state-specific logging codes (OR-OSHA, WA L&I, CA Cal/OSHA).
Logging Safety Program Elements
A compliant logging safety program includes: written safety plan, trained and certified fallers and equipment operators, hazard communication, PPE program (hard hats, chaps, boots, eye/ear protection), daily equipment inspection, first aid and rescue plan, weather protocols, and incident investigation.
Logging Truck Driver Hazards
Narrow forest roads, steep grades, off-highway tire-to-highway tire transitions, load securement, bridge weight limits, and wildlife strikes. Often requires specialty driver training and specific endorsements.
Long-Term Care Insurance
Coverage for services provided in a setting other than an acute care unit of a hospital — nursing home care, home health care, assisted living, or community-based care. Often includes benefits for cognitive impairment or loss of functional capacity.
Long-Term Disability Insurance
Replaces a portion of income for extended disability. Benefits typically begin after short-term disability ends (90-180 days) and can continue for years or to retirement age. Own-occupation coverage (can't do your specific job) is broader than any-occupation (can't do any reasonable work).
Loss
When someone with insurance endures a negative outcome, such as injury or theft, and expects payment from the insurance company to compensate them.
Loss Adjustment Expense (LAE)
Costs of investigating and settling claims — adjuster fees, legal defense, expert witness fees, court costs. Allocated LAE (ALAE) is specific to a particular claim; Unallocated LAE (ULAE) is general overhead. Both are factors in pricing.
Loss Assessment Coverage
A homeowners (especially HO-6) provision covering assessments levied by a condo or homeowners association for damage to common areas or liability claims that exceed the association's master policy limits. Typically a small limit ($1,000-$5,000) but can be increased by endorsement.
Loss Control
The practice of identifying, evaluating, and reducing the frequency and/or severity of losses. Performed by insurance company loss control consultants, brokers, and internal risk managers. Includes surveys, recommendations, training, and follow-up audits.
Loss Control Report
A written report documenting a loss control survey — the risk's operations, existing controls, hazards identified, recommendations, and the consultant's overall risk evaluation. Often used by underwriting to price and accept the account.
Loss Control Survey
An on-site visit by a loss control consultant to observe operations, identify hazards, evaluate controls, and make written recommendations. Results in a loss control report shared with underwriting.
Loss Development
The change over time in estimates of what an insurer will ultimately pay for claims from a given accident period. Newer claims are often underestimated; as time passes, more accurate figures emerge. Actuaries use "loss triangles" to track development.
Loss Exposure
The chance of financial loss due to some peril. Every loss exposure has three elements: the exposed value (what could be lost), the peril (what could cause the loss), and the financial consequence.
Loss Forecasting
Estimating the frequency and severity of future losses using historical data, trend analysis, industry benchmarks, and stochastic modeling. Essential for retention decisions, budget planning, and insurance buying strategy.
Loss Frequency vs. Severity
Usually an inverse relationship — as frequency increases, severity typically decreases, and vice versa. High-frequency/low-severity losses are candidates for retention; low-frequency/high-severity losses are candidates for transfer via insurance.
Loss History
Refers to an insured's history of losses (claims) with other companies, or the company they are currently with. A company will consider "loss history" when underwriting a new policy or considering a renewal of an existing policy. Companies view "loss history" as an indication of an insured's propensity for a claim in the future.
Loss History / Loss Run
A formal report from a carrier showing all claims an insured has had over a period — typically 3-5 years. Includes dates, descriptions, amounts paid, and reserves. Standard document requested during underwriting and at renewal.
Loss of Use (Coverage D)
Homeowners coverage for additional living expenses incurred when the home is uninhabitable due to a covered loss — hotel costs, temporary housing, restaurant meals in excess of normal food costs. Typically 20-30% of dwelling coverage.
Loss Payee
The party to whom money or insurance proceeds is to be paid in the event of loss, such as the lien holder or an automobile or the mortgagee on real property.
Loss Prevention
Measures taken BEFORE a loss to reduce the frequency of losses (e.g., fire safety training for kitchen staff before their first shift).
Loss Ratio
The percentage of premium an insurer pays out in losses and loss adjustment expenses. Formula: Incurred Losses ÷ Earned Premium. A key measure of how well an insurer is underwriting.
Loss Ratio Calculation
Loss ratio = (Losses incurred + LAE) ÷ Earned premium. A loss ratio under 65-70% is generally profitable for personal lines; commercial lines vary by class.
Loss Reduction
Measures taken to reduce the severity of losses. Pre-loss reduction (e.g., sprinkler systems installed in advance) or post-loss reduction (e.g., quick emergency response).
Loss Reserve
An amount the insurer sets aside on its books to cover claims that have been reported but not yet paid, plus estimates for IBNR. Critical to measuring financial solvency.
Loss Triangle
A table showing how claims from each accident year (or reporting period) develop over successive valuation dates. Used by actuaries to project ultimate losses and set reserves.
Loss — Direct
Physical harm to tangible property caused by a peril.
Loss — Indirect
An economic loss that results from the direct, or physical, loss.
M
Malpractice Insurance
Coverage protecting a licensed health care provider or professional against legal liability resulting from death, injury, or loss to a patient or client caused by the insured's negligence, error, or omission in rendering professional services. Also called Medical Professional Liability.
Management Liability Insurance Suite
An umbrella term for coverages protecting managers and the entity from management-related claims: D&O (directors and officers), EPLI (employment practices), Fiduciary Liability (employee benefit plan administration), and Crime.
Margin Clause
A provision in a blanket property policy limiting recovery at any one location to a percentage (typically 110-125%) of the reported value at that location — preventing all the blanket limit from going to a single loss.
Marine Insurance
Coverage for goods in transit and the vehicles of transportation on waterways, land, and air. Split into Ocean Marine (ships, cargo across oceans) and Inland Marine (goods moving over land and scheduled personal property).
Market (Verb)
In agency operations, to "market" an account is to solicit quotes from multiple carriers to find the best coverage and price. Distinct from direct-writing with a single carrier.
Market Conduct Examination
A state regulatory review of an insurer's practices — underwriting, claims handling, advertising, producer oversight, complaint handling. Separate from financial examinations (which focus on solvency). Can result in fines, corrective action orders, and restitution to policyholders.
Market Value
The price something will sell for in the open market, or "fair market value."
Market Value vs. Replacement Cost
Market value is what a willing buyer would pay for the home; replacement cost is what it would cost to rebuild. These are different numbers — especially for older homes, homes in declining areas, or unique properties. Insurance should be based on replacement cost.
Material Misrepresentation
A significant misstatement in an application form. If a company had access to the correct information at the time of application, the company might not have agreed to accept the application.
MC (Motor Carrier) Number
An authority number issued by FMCSA for for-hire motor carriers transporting federally regulated commodities interstate. Separate from the DOT number.
McCarran-Ferguson Act
Federal law enacted in 1945 confirming state regulation of insurance. Exempts the insurance business from most federal anti-trust laws as long as states actively regulate. Foundation of the state-based regulatory system.
MCS-150
The form motor carriers file biennially to update their operational information with FMCSA. Required to maintain active operating authority.
MCS-90 Endorsement
