Provides public liability coverage for bodily injury, property damage, and environmental protection. Federally required endorsement for motor carriers.
Mechanical Breakdown Exclusion
Property policies generally exclude losses from mechanical or electrical breakdown. Requires Equipment Breakdown (boiler and machinery) coverage.
Mechanical Breakdown Insurance
Coverage for repair or replacement of property that fails due to defects in materials, workmanship, or normal wear and tear. Commonly applied to motor vehicles, mobile equipment, appliances, and electronics.
Mechanized Logging
Logging using feller-bunchers, harvesters, forwarders, and processors — equipment that replaces much of the hand-cutting with enclosed-cab machine operation. Generally safer than hand-cutting but introduces equipment-specific hazards.
Media Liability
Coverage for content-related claims — defamation, copyright infringement, invasion of privacy. Relevant for publishers, broadcasters, content creators, and businesses with active digital marketing.
Medicaid
A federal and state assistance program that pays for health care services for people who cannot afford them.
Medical Payments (Auto)
Auto coverage that pays reasonable expenses incurred for necessary medical and funeral services because of bodily injury caused by an accident and sustained by you or any other person while occupying a covered automobile — regardless of fault.
Medical Payments to Others (Coverage F)
Homeowners coverage that pays reasonable medical expenses for guests injured on the insured's premises — regardless of fault. Small limit (typically $1,000-$5,000) intended to avoid lawsuits by covering minor medical bills.
Medicare
A federal assistance program, passed under Title XVIII of the Social Security Amendments of 1965, that provides hospital and medical expense insurance to people over 65 and certain disabled individuals.
Medicare Supplement (Medigap)
Insurance coverage sold on an individual or group basis to help fill the "gaps" in Medicare coverage. Pays part or all of Medicare's deductibles and co-payments and may cover services not covered by Medicare. Cannot duplicate Medicare benefits.
Methods for Analyzing Loss Exposure
Standardized questionnaires, review of financial statements, flowcharts of operations, and personal inspections of the premises.
MGA (Managing General Agent)
An agent or agency with greater authority from an insurance company than a typical agent — may include underwriting, policy issuance, claims handling, and producer appointment authority. Effectively operates as an outsourced underwriting arm.
Minimum Liability Limit
The lowest amount of auto insurance coverage one can have to be legally allowed to drive in each state.
Misrepresentation
Any false or misleading statement.
Mitigation
Activities that reduce the severity of an event if it occurs. Different from prevention, which tries to stop the event entirely. Examples: installing sprinklers (fire mitigation), elevating buildings above flood plains, installing surge protection.
Mitigation Planning
Planning of specific actions to reduce the likelihood or impact of identified risks. Occurs after analysis of probability, impact, and speed of onset.
Mobile Equipment Endorsement
When this endorsement is attached, mobile equipment is considered a covered auto.
Modified Duty / Light Duty
Temporary alternative job assignments within the injured worker's medical restrictions while they recover. Enables return-to-work before full release to normal duties.
Mold Exclusion / Sub-Limit
Many property policies exclude or severely limit mold-related losses. Often subject to a $5,000-$10,000 sub-limit. Can leave insureds exposed to significant remediation costs.
Monitoring Risk Management Results
After implementation, results must be monitored and adjusted as exposures change, new risks emerge, or techniques prove ineffective.
Moody's Insurance Rating
Moody's Investors Service's rating of an insurer's claims-paying ability. Ranges from Aaa (highest quality) to C. One of several rating agencies commercial and reinsurance buyers consult.
Moral Hazard
A hazard arising from the insured's dishonesty or intent. Example: a business owner in financial distress intentionally burning down a warehouse to collect insurance. Controlled through underwriting (declining applicants with suspicious motivations), investigation (scrutinizing suspicious claims), and exclusions (no coverage for intentional acts).
Morale Hazard
A hazard arising from carelessness or indifference because of insurance. Example: leaving a car unlocked because "the insurance will pay if it's stolen." Different from moral hazard — the insured doesn't want the loss but is less careful. Controlled through deductibles (insured shares the loss) and coinsurance.
Mortgage Guaranty Insurance
Insurance that indemnifies a lender for loss upon foreclosure if a borrower fails to meet required mortgage payments. Also called Private Mortgage Insurance (PMI).
Mortgage Insurance (Life)
A form of life insurance payable to a third-party lender upon the death of the insured mortgagor. Pays off the remaining mortgage balance.
Motor Carrier Act of 1980
Requires trucking companies to certify that they are able to meet financial obligations if they become liable for injury or damage arising from their trucking operations.
Motor Carrier Act — Limits of Liability
Affects vehicles with a gross weight of 10,000 lbs or more. Required limits are divided into three categories: (1) $750,000 for interstate transportation of non-hazardous property; (2) $5,000,000 for interstate/intrastate transportation of large quantities of certain hazardous materials (compressed gas, radioactive materials, explosives); (3) $1,000,000 for interstate/intrastate transportation of oil or other hazardous materials.
Motor Carrier Coverage Form
For businesses that transport their own goods and property and may haul the goods of others. Owned passenger autos may be covered autos in the motor carrier coverage form.
Motor Truck Cargo Insurance
Covers commodities being transported by a motor carrier for hire while in the carrier's care, custody, or control. Required by many shippers and by FMCSA for certain commodities. Can be written on all-risk or named-perils basis; common exclusions include loss from temperature failures and improper loading.
Municipal Bond Guarantee Insurance
Coverage sold to municipalities to guarantee the principal and interest payments on bonds issued — enhances the bond's credit rating.
Mutual Insurance Company
An insurance company that is owned by its policyholders who participate in dividends. These companies have no shareholders and are not traded publicly.
Mutual Insurance Holding Company
A parent company organized as a mutual, owning one or more stock insurers. Allows the benefits of both mutual ownership (by policyholders) and stock access to capital markets.
MVR (Motor Vehicle Record)
A state-issued driving record showing violations, accidents, license status, and endorsements. Reviewed as part of driver hiring and ongoing fleet management.
MVR Screening Standards
Written criteria for hiring and retaining drivers. Typical thresholds: no DUI in 5 years, no more than 2 moving violations in 3 years, no at-fault accidents in 3 years. Stricter standards for heavy trucks and hazmat. Periodic MVR pulls (annual or quarterly) catch post-hire violations.
N
NAIC (National Association of Insurance Commissioners)
The U.S. standard-setting body created by the chief insurance regulators of all 50 states, D.C., and 5 U.S. territories. Sets model laws, financial reporting standards, and conducts peer review. Insurance is state-regulated, but the NAIC coordinates standards across states.
NAIC — National Association of Insurance Commissioners
The coordinating organization of state insurance regulators. Develops model laws and regulations that states can adopt, operates the Insurance Regulatory Information System (IRIS), maintains national databases, and addresses multi-state issues. Not a federal regulator but influences uniformity.
Named Driver Exclusion
An endorsement that excludes a specifically named individual from coverage under an auto policy. Common when a household member has a poor driving record that would otherwise make insurance unaffordable or unavailable. Any accident caused by the excluded driver results in no coverage.
Named Insured
The individual, persons, or entities defined as the insured on the declarations page of the policy. The "first named insured" generally has additional rights and responsibilities.
Named Peril Policy
A policy that names all perils that will be covered. Contrasts with open-peril policies.
Named Perils vs. Open Perils
Named perils (also "specified perils") coverage lists exactly which perils are covered — anything not listed is NOT covered. Open perils (also "all risk" or "special form") coverage covers all perils EXCEPT those specifically excluded — broader coverage. Most commercial property and high-end homeowners are written on open perils.
National Incident Management System (NIMS) ↗
A standardized approach to incident management established by FEMA. Provides a common framework for agencies and businesses to work together during emergencies. Includes the Incident Command System (ICS).
NCCI (National Council on Compensation Insurance)
The rating and statistical organization for workers compensation insurance in most U.S. states. Develops class codes, loss costs, and the experience rating formula that produces a business's Experience Mod (EMR).
Near Miss
An unplanned event that did not result in injury, illness, or damage but had the potential to do so. Reporting and investigating near misses is a key leading indicator.
Needs Assessment (Preparedness)
An evaluation conducted to determine what resources and steps are required to meet program goals. Assess the organization's current state, define program goals and performance objectives, identify gaps, and document the steps needed to close them. The foundation of any preparedness or risk management initiative.
Negligence
The failure to use that degree of care which an ordinary person of reasonable prudence would use under similar circumstances.
Negligence Checklist
The defendant had a legal duty to act (or not act) in a prescribed manner; the defendant failed to act accordingly (called breach of duty); the plaintiff suffered actual loss or injury due to the defendant's action or inaction; and the loss or injury to the plaintiff was a direct result of the breach of duty of the defendant.
Net Income Loss
Reduction in net income caused by an event that either decreases revenues, increases expenses, or both. Often covered by business income insurance.
NIST Cybersecurity Framework (CSF) 2.0
A voluntary framework of cybersecurity best practices developed by the National Institute of Standards and Technology. Built around six functions: Govern, Identify, Protect, Detect, Respond, and Recover. Widely adopted across industries.
NIST — Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) ↗
The central NIST portal for cybersecurity publications, including the Special Publications (800 series), Federal Information Processing Standards (FIPS), and NIST Interagency Reports (IRs).
NIST — Generally Accepted Principles and Practices for Securing IT Systems (SP 800-14) ↗
The NIST reference on fundamental security principles — defense in depth, least privilege, separation of duties, accountability — that apply to any IT environment.
NIST — Information Security Handbook: A Guide for Managers (SP 800-100) ↗
A practical guide for managers responsible for information security programs. Covers governance, risk management, and program management in plain language.
NIST — Introduction to Computer Security (SP 800-12) ↗
A foundational introduction to computer security concepts for non-specialists. A good starting point for building organizational cyber literacy.
NIST — Risk Management Guide for Information Technology Systems (SP 800-30) ↗
The foundational federal document for IT risk assessment. Provides a structured methodology for identifying, analyzing, and managing IT security risk. Referenced by most cybersecurity frameworks.
Non-Admitted Carrier
An insurance company that is NOT licensed in the state where the risk is located. Non-admitted carriers can write "surplus lines" business — coverage unavailable from admitted markets. Not backed by state guaranty funds. Also called "Unauthorized" or "Surplus Lines" carrier.
Non-Combustible Construction
ISO Construction Class 3. Walls and structural components are non-combustible but may not have extensive fire-resistance ratings (e.g., bare steel-framed building).
Non-Commercial Insurer
Not-for-profit insurance companies that return profits to policyholders by reducing premiums or expanding benefits.
Non-Contributory Wording
Policy or certificate language stating the insurance will not seek contribution from the certificate holder's own insurance. Paired with "primary wording" in contract requirements.
Non-Owner Auto Policy
Auto liability coverage for someone who drives but doesn't own a vehicle — often purchased by drivers who need SR-22 filing, drive rental cars frequently, or borrow vehicles. Does not provide physical damage coverage.
Non-Proportional (Excess) Reinsurance
Reinsurer pays only when losses exceed a specified threshold (the attachment point). Includes Excess of Loss Per Risk (per individual loss), Excess of Loss Per Occurrence (per event, often used for catastrophes), and Aggregate Excess (when total losses exceed a threshold).
Non-Renewal
An insurer's decision not to renew a policy at expiration. Most states require advance written notice (typically 30-60 days). Different from cancellation, which ends a policy mid-term.
Non-Waiver Agreement
An agreement that the insurer asks the claimant to sign which states that the insurer's investigation of the loss can't be used later on as proof that the loss is covered under the policy.
Not Catastrophic to Insurer
One of the requirements of an insurable risk — the peril cannot cause simultaneous losses to a large portion of the insured pool. This is why private insurers decline to offer standalone flood coverage.
Notice of Loss
Notice required by insurance companies immediately after an accident or other loss. Part of the standard provisions defining a policyholder's responsibilities after a loss.
O
Obligee
The party protected by a surety bond. The person, firm, or corporation for whose benefit the bond is issued.
Obligor (Principal)
The party whose performance is guaranteed by a surety bond. Under a bond, strictly speaking, both the principal and the surety are obligors.
Occupancy Class
A classification of a building based on what's happening inside — office, retail, warehouse, manufacturing, restaurant. Drives both property rates (fire hazard) and liability rates (public exposure).
Occurrence
An event that results in an insured loss or damages.
Occurrence Form
A liability insurance form that covers claims arising from an incident that occurred during the policy period, regardless of when the claim is reported. Contrasts with a "claims-made" form.
Ocean Cargo Insurance
Coverage for goods in transit by ocean vessel, typically written on a "warehouse-to-warehouse" basis. Covers perils of the sea, fire, jettison, and usually extended to include more perils under institute cargo clauses.
Ocean Marine Insurance
Coverage for ocean and inland water transportation exposures: cargo, ships and hulls, earnings, and liability. One of the oldest forms of insurance.
OCIP (Owner Controlled Insurance Program)
A single insurance program purchased by a project owner that covers all contractors and subcontractors on a construction project. Provides consistent coverage and avoids gaps between multiple contractor policies.
OEM Parts Coverage
An endorsement or policy option that requires the insurer to pay for Original Equipment Manufacturer parts (rather than aftermarket parts) when repairing a vehicle. Common on newer or leased vehicles.
Open Peril Policy
An open perils policy covers all causes of loss except for those explicitly excluded. Also known as "All Peril."
Operational Risk
The risk that day-to-day business activities drain profits or cause losses. Both internal systems (process failures, human error, technology outages, fraud) and external factors (supplier failure, utility outage, weather) can cause operational risks. Controlled through process design, redundancy, training, and business continuity planning.
Optional Retention
A deliberate decision to retain a risk, usually because the expected loss is small, predictable, or cheaper to absorb than to insure.
Options for Claim Settlements
The insurer has three options for settling physical damage claims: (1) pay for, repair, or replace the damaged or stolen property; (2) return stolen property to the insured at the company's expense and pay for any damages resulting from the theft; (3) take the damaged or stolen property at an agreed or appraised value.
Ordinance or Law Coverage
An endorsement covering the increased cost to rebuild to current building code. Three coverages: (A) undamaged portion of the building that must be demolished; (B) demolition costs; (C) increased cost of construction to meet current code. Critical for older buildings in jurisdictions with aggressive code updates.
OSHA 300 Log
The record of workplace injuries and illnesses that most employers with more than 10 employees must maintain. Includes a 300 log (detailed record), 300A annual summary (posted Feb 1 – Apr 30), and 301 incident reports.
OSHA Recordable
An injury or illness that must be recorded on the OSHA 300 log. Generally includes cases involving medical treatment beyond first aid, lost time, restricted work, loss of consciousness, or significant diagnosed conditions.
OSHA — Protecting Workers from Heat Illness ↗
OSHA guidance on heat stress prevention — hydration, rest breaks, shade, acclimatization, emergency response. Increasingly important for outdoor and industrial operations.
OSHA — Survey Your Workplace for Additional Hazards ↗
Part of OSHA's Compliance Assistance Quick Start for General Industry. A structured walkthrough for identifying common workplace hazards beyond the obvious. Useful as a loss control baseline for any commercial account.
Other Insurance Condition
States that the business auto coverage form provides primary coverage for covered autos owned by the insured; the coverage is excess for losses involving non-owned covered autos.
Other Structures (Coverage B)
Homeowners coverage for detached structures on the property — fences, sheds, detached garages, gazebos. Typically a separate sub-limit, often 10% of the dwelling limit.
Out of State Coverage Extensions
Modify the policy's liability coverage to meet other states' financial responsibility requirements (minimum limits of liability) when the covered auto is being driven in that state.
Out-of-Pocket Maximum
The most the insured will pay in a policy year for covered in-network services. Once reached, the plan pays 100% of covered services for the rest of the year. Does not include premiums.
Out-of-Service (OOS)
A declaration that a vehicle or driver cannot operate until violations are corrected. Issued during roadside inspections or compliance reviews for serious safety defects.
Overhead & Profit
The amount of money dedicated to a general contractor's cost of doing business and earning a profit. Insurance industry standard is 10% and 10%.
Owner Controlled Insurance Program (OCIP)
A single insurance program purchased by a project owner covering all enrolled contractors and subcontractors on a construction project. Provides consistent coverage terms, eliminates gaps between contractor policies, and centralizes claims handling.
P
Package Modification Factor
A credit applied to a commercial package policy reflecting the combined account approach. Acknowledges that packaging multiple coverages with one carrier reduces administration cost.
Package Policy
Two or more distinct coverages combined into a single contract — for example, a Homeowners policy combines property, personal property, and liability coverages. Typically cheaper than buying each coverage separately.
Pandemic Preparedness Plan
A documented plan for maintaining operations and protecting employees during widespread disease outbreaks. Includes communication protocols, remote work capability, PPE supplies, cleaning procedures, and workforce continuity.
Parts of an Insurance Policy
Every policy contains the same core structure: Declarations (who, what, how much, when), Insuring Agreement (the insurer's promise), Exclusions (what isn't covered), Conditions (what the insured must do), and Definitions (specific word meanings). Knowing this structure makes reading any policy faster.
PCI DSS (Payment Card Industry Data Security Standard)
A proprietary information security standard for organizations that handle credit card data. Includes requirements for network security, cardholder data protection, vulnerability management, and access control.
Per Occurrence Limit
The maximum a liability policy will pay for any single claim or accident. Contrasts with the aggregate limit, which is the most the policy will pay for all claims during the policy period.
Peril
The direct cause of loss — fire, windstorm, theft, collision. Sometimes confused with "hazard."
Peril vs. Hazard
A peril is the actual cause of loss (fire, theft, wind, collision). A hazard is a condition that increases the likelihood or severity of a peril causing loss. Fire is a peril; oily rags are a hazard. Policies cover perils; loss control addresses hazards.
Perils Insured Against
These are the specific causes of loss that are covered under the policy.
Personal Auto Policy (PAP)
Coverage designed to insure private passenger automobiles and certain types of trucks owned by an individual or married couple. Includes liability, medical payments, uninsured/underinsured motorist, comprehensive, and collision coverages.
Personal Auto Policy Part A — Liability
Pays damages the insured is legally responsible for after an auto accident: bodily injury to others and property damage to others. Includes defense costs for lawsuits. Does not cover damage to the insured's own vehicle or injuries to the insured. Required by state financial responsibility laws.
Personal Auto Policy Part B — Medical Payments
Pays medical and funeral expenses for the insured, family members, and passengers injured in a covered auto, regardless of fault. A small-limit coverage (typically $1,000-$10,000 per person) designed to cover immediate medical costs without a lengthy fault determination.
Personal Auto Policy Part C — Uninsured Motorists
Pays bodily injury damages (and property damage in some states) caused by a motorist who has no insurance, has insufficient insurance, or is a hit-and-run driver. Some states offer stacked UM coverage (combining limits across multiple vehicles).
Personal Auto Policy Part D — Coverage for Damage to Your Auto
Covers damage to the insured's own vehicle. Split into Collision (impact with another vehicle or object) and Other Than Collision / Comprehensive (fire, theft, hail, vandalism, flood, falling objects, animal strikes). Each has its own deductible.
Personal Auto Policy Part E — Duties After an Accident or Loss
The policyholder's obligations after an accident: notify the insurer promptly, cooperate with the investigation, forward legal documents, provide proof of loss, submit to an examination under oath if requested, and protect the vehicle from further damage. Failure can void coverage.
Personal Auto Policy Part F — General Provisions
The general rules of the contract: policy territory (US and Canada typical), termination and cancellation procedures, transfer of interest on the insured's death, fraud consequences, and the insurer's right of subrogation.
Personal GAP Insurance
Credit insurance that pays the difference between the outstanding balance owed on a vehicle loan and the actual cash value of the vehicle in the event of a total loss. Prevents the borrower from owing money on a car they no longer own.
Personal Injury Liability
Liability coverage for claims arising from discrimination, false arrest, illegal detention, libel, slander, malicious prosecution, mental anguish, identity theft, alienation of affections, or violation of privacy. Broader than "bodily injury."
Personal Injury Protection (PIP)
Auto insurance that protects you against the cost of your own medical bills and, in some states, lost wages and replacement services if you are involved in a car accident, regardless of fault. Required in no-fault states.
Personal Inspections
First-hand site visits to assess risk exposure, observe operations, and identify hazards that may not appear in documents.
Personal Liability (Coverage E)
Homeowners coverage for claims by third parties for bodily injury or property damage for which the insured is legally responsible — dog bites, slip and fall on property, damage caused by household members away from home. Typically $100,000-$500,000 limit.
Personal Property (Coverage C)
Homeowners coverage for contents — furniture, clothing, electronics, appliances. Typically written as a percentage of the dwelling coverage (often 50-70%). May be written on replacement cost or actual cash value basis.
Personal Protective Equipment (PPE)
Equipment worn by workers to minimize exposure to hazards — hard hats, safety glasses, hearing protection, gloves, respirators, high-visibility vests, fall protection harnesses. Required by OSHA when engineering and administrative controls are insufficient.
Personal Umbrella Policy
Extra liability coverage above the underlying auto and homeowners policies. Typical limits $1M-$5M. Responds when the underlying limit is exhausted OR for certain losses not covered by the underlying policy. Requires underlying coverage at specified minimum limits (usually $250K/$500K auto and $300K homeowners).
Personnel Loss Exposure
The financial exposure created when key personnel die, quit, retire, or become disabled. Addressed through succession planning and key person insurance.
Pet Insurance
A veterinary care insurance policy providing coverage for a pet animal (dog, cat, etc.) in the event of illness or accident. May cover routine care, accidents, or both.
Physical Damage Coverage
Contains three principal coverages: (1) Collision, (2) Comprehensive, (3) Specified Causes of Loss.
Physical Damage Trailer Interchange
Coverage for damage to a trailer in the insured's possession under a trailer interchange agreement. Common in trucking where carriers exchange trailers to maintain efficient operations.
Physical Damage — Exclusions
Excluded: sound reproducing and receiving equipment, tapes, and records; wear and tear, freezing, mechanical or electrical breakdown, or road damage to tires; covered autos while being used for organized or professional racing, demolition, and stunting activities.
Physical Hazard
A tangible condition that increases the probability of a loss — icy sidewalks, unguarded machinery, oily rags near a heat source, faulty wiring, overgrown brush near a building. The primary focus of loss control programs.
Point of Service Plan (POS)
A hybrid of HMO and PPO. Requires a PCP and referrals for specialists (HMO-like), but allows out-of-network coverage at higher cost (PPO-like).
Policy
Contract between an insurance company and policyholder that explains the amounts and types of coverage.
Policy Administration System / Agency Management System (AMS)
The software system an agency uses to manage policies, customer records, billing, claims, and workflow. Major systems include Applied Epic, Applied TAM, Vertafore AMS360, and Vertafore Sagitta.
Policy Loan
A loan against a permanent life insurance policy, using the cash value as collateral. Interest is charged but the loan does not have to be repaid; unpaid loan balance is deducted from the death benefit. Tax-free while the policy remains in force.
Policy Period
The period a policy is in force, from the beginning or effective date to the expiration date.
Policy Territory
The geographic area where a policy provides coverage. Common definitions include "United States and Canada," "Worldwide," or named countries. Significant for cargo, travel, and international operations.
Policyholder
The individual or firm buying the policy.
Pollutants
Any solid, liquid, gaseous, or thermal irritant or contaminant including smoke, vapor, soot, fumes, acids, alkalis, chemicals, and waste. Waste includes material to be recycled, reconditioned, or reclaimed.
Pollution Exclusion (CGL)
Standard Commercial General Liability policies exclude pollution liability (with limited exceptions). Even minor chemical operations (pool chlorine, HVAC refrigerants) can create uncovered exposures. Pollution coverage often purchased separately.
Pollution Liability Insurance
Coverage for third-party bodily injury, property damage, and cleanup costs from pollution incidents. Most general liability policies exclude pollution; a separate policy is required for operations with environmental exposure.
Post-Accident Protocol
Written procedures for post-accident actions: driver safety, scene documentation, law enforcement report, injury care, post-accident drug/alcohol test (required by FMCSA within 8 hours for alcohol, 32 hours for drugs for certain accidents), carrier notification.
Power of Attorney
Authority given to one person or corporation to act for and obligate another, to the extent laid down in the instrument creating the power.
PPO
Preferred Provider Organization. A health insurance plan in which providers agree to deliver services for discount fees; patients can go to any provider, but using nonparticipating providers results in higher costs to the patient.
Pre-Action Sprinkler System
A two-stage system combining features of wet and dry. Water is held back by a valve that requires both heat detection AND a sprinkler head activation. Used where accidental water discharge would be catastrophic — data centers, archives, museums.
Pre-Trip Inspection (DVIR)
Daily inspection required by FMCSA. Driver inspects brakes, lights, tires, mirrors, load securement, fluids, and other safety items. Documented on Driver Vehicle Inspection Report (DVIR). Any defects must be repaired before next trip if they affect safety.
Preferred Provider Organization (PPO)
A managed care plan that provides financial incentives (lower deductibles, lower coinsurance) for using in-network providers but also covers out-of-network care at a higher cost. No PCP or referrals typically required. More flexible than HMO.
Premises and Operations Coverage
Coverage for liability of an insured to persons who have incurred bodily injury or property damage on an insured's premises during normal operations, routine maintenance, or from the insured's business operations either on or off the premises. Core coverage in a CGL policy.
Premises and Operations Exposure
CGL coverage for liability arising from the insured's business premises (slip-and-fall) and day-to-day operations away from the premises (damage to a customer's property during a service call). The baseline exposure for most commercial accounts.
Premises Pollution
Pollution liability coverage for sudden and accidental releases on or from the insured's premises. Standard CGL policies have pollution exclusions; premises pollution coverage is often needed by businesses with even minor chemical use (HVAC coolants, cleaning supplies).
Premium
The amount paid by an insured to an insurance company to obtain or maintain an insurance policy.
Premium Audit (Workers Comp)
An annual review by the insurer at policy end to determine actual payroll by class code. Initial premium is based on estimated payroll; audit determines actual and results in additional premium or return premium. Audit noncompliance can result in substantial penalties.
Premium Calculation Basics
Premium = Rate × Exposure units. Rate is expressed per unit of exposure (per $100 of payroll for workers comp, per $1,000 of building value for property, per vehicle for auto). Modifiers (schedule credits/debits, experience mod, package discount, loss control credits) adjust base premium up or down.
Preparedness
Activities that ensure an organization can respond effectively when an event occurs — planning, training, drills, supplies, communication systems. Closes the gap between prevention and response.
Prevention
Activities designed to stop an event from occurring. The most effective level of risk treatment — eliminating hazards entirely or reducing their likelihood to near zero.
Prevention Program
A structured organizational program focused on stopping losses before they occur. Includes hazard identification, controls, training, monitoring, and continuous improvement. The most cost-effective approach to risk treatment.
Primary & Non-Contributory
A policy provision (and certificate requirement) that the insurance will respond before and without seeking contribution from the certificate holder's own insurance. Common contract requirement when one party is an additional insured.
Primary Goal of Risk Management
Minimizing loss — reducing the financial impact of uncertainty on an organization through identification, assessment, and treatment of risk exposures.
Primary Insurance
Coverage that takes precedence when more than one policy covers the same loss. Primary pays first; excess or umbrella policies kick in after primary limits are exhausted.
Primary Wording
Policy or certificate language stating the insurance responds first, before any other applicable insurance. Required in many commercial contracts to ensure the designated party's coverage is not depleted first.
Prior Approval Law
A state rate regulation requiring insurers to obtain explicit approval from the insurance department before using a new rate or form. Contrasts with "file-and-use" states where insurers can use rates upon filing.
Probability
The chance of something happening or not happening within a certain number of occurrences.
Producer
An industry term for a licensed insurance salesperson — often used to distinguish between agents (carrier-appointed) and brokers (representing buyers). Legal licensing category in many states.
Producer Agreement
The contract between an agent and an insurance company defining terms of the relationship — authorities granted, commission schedule, termination rights, and ownership of expirations.
Producer License
The license issued by a state that permits an individual or agency to sell, solicit, or negotiate insurance. Requires pre-licensing education, passing an exam, and continuing education to maintain.
Product Liability Insurance
Coverage protecting a manufacturer, distributor, seller, or lessor of a product against legal liability resulting from a defective condition causing personal injury or damage to any individual or entity.
Product Recall Insurance
Coverage for the costs of recalling a defective product — transportation, storage, disposal, and customer communications. Often excluded from general liability; purchased separately by manufacturers.
Products / Completed Operations Exposure
CGL coverage for liability arising from the insured's products after they leave the insured's control or completed work after it is turned over to the customer. Critical for manufacturers, contractors, restaurants, and any business that creates products or performs work for others. Has its own aggregate limit.
Products/Completed Operations Aggregate
A separate aggregate limit for claims arising from the insured's products or completed work. Typically equals the general aggregate limit. Important for manufacturers and contractors.
Professional Liability Insurance (Errors & Omissions / E&O)
Protects professionals (designers, architects, engineers, consultants, insurance agents) against claims of negligence, errors, or omissions in their professional services. Essential for anyone providing advice or specialized services for a fee.
Professional Liability — Design Professionals
Coverage for architects, engineers, and surveyors against claims of professional negligence in their designs, plans, and specifications.
Professional Services Exclusion
CGL policies exclude claims arising from professional services — requires Errors & Omissions (professional liability) coverage. Applies to architects, consultants, accountants, medical professionals, real estate agents, insurance agents.
Proof of Loss
A sworn statement by the insured detailing the loss — what was damaged, when, cause, and amount claimed. Required by most policies within a specified time (60-180 days). Delay or failure can affect recovery.
Property Damage
Damage to another person's property. The purpose of liability insurance is to cover property damage to a third party resulting from the negligent or unintentional acts of the insured.
Proportional (Pro-Rata) Reinsurance
Reinsurer takes a percentage of every premium and pays the same percentage of every loss. Includes Quota Share (fixed percentage of every risk) and Surplus Share (percentage of amounts above the cedent's retention).
Prorata Cancellation Math
Earned premium = (Days coverage provided ÷ Days in policy period) × Total premium. Example: $1,200 annual policy canceled after 60 days → earned premium = (60 ÷ 365) × $1,200 = $197. Refund = $1,003.
Protection and Indemnity (P&I) Insurance
A broad form of marine legal liability insurance coverage. Covers third-party injuries and damages arising from vessel operations.
Protection Class (ISO)
A 1-10 classification assigned to a community's fire protection capability. Class 1 is best (excellent fire department, hydrants, water supply); Class 10 means little or no recognized fire protection. Directly impacts property insurance rates.
Protective Safeguards Warranty
A commercial property provision where coverage depends on the insured maintaining specified safety systems (sprinkler system active, burglar alarm functional, central station monitoring). If the system is disabled without notifying the insurer, coverage for related losses can be voided.
Provisions (Policy)
The clauses that make up the contract language of an insurance policy — define rights, duties, conditions, and limitations of both insurer and insured.
Proximate Cause
The direct, dominant cause that sets into motion a chain of events resulting in a loss, without the intervention of any other independent or unrelated source. Determines whether a loss is covered under a policy.
Proximate Cause Doctrine
A legal principle determining which cause of a loss triggers coverage when multiple causes are involved. The proximate cause is the direct, dominant cause that sets into motion an unbroken chain of events leading to the loss. If the proximate cause is a covered peril, the loss is generally covered even if later causes are excluded.
Public Adjuster
An adjuster retained by the policyholder (not the insurer) to advocate for the insured during a claim. Licensed separately in most states. Typically compensated as a percentage of the settlement. Useful for complex or large losses.
Pure Premium
The portion of a premium equal to expected losses, without any loading for insurance company expenses, premium taxes, contingencies, or profit margin.
Pure Risk
A situation including possibility of loss or no loss, but no possibility of gain. Only pure risks are insurable — speculative risks (stock market gains/losses) are not.
Q
Quota Share Reinsurance
A proportional reinsurance arrangement where the reinsurer takes a fixed percentage of every risk written by the primary insurer. Simple and predictable; provides broad capacity relief.
Quote
An estimate of the cost of insurance based on information supplied by the applicant. A quote is not a binding contract — it is subject to underwriting approval.
Quote / Indication
A price estimate for coverage. A firm quote is subject to underwriting and terms; an "indication" is preliminary pricing given before full underwriting has been done.
R
Rate
The price per unit of insurance exposure. Premium = Rate × Exposure units. For example, $0.50 per square foot × 2,500 sq. ft. = $1,250 annual premium.
Rate Filing
A formal submission to a state insurance department seeking approval (or providing notice) of proposed rates, rules, or forms. States use one of several approval methods: prior approval, file-and-use, use-and-file, or flex rating.
Rate Regulation
State oversight of insurance rates. Three common approaches: Prior Approval (rates must be approved before use), File and Use (rates may be used once filed), Use and File (rates may be used before filing with subsequent review). Most states have modernized toward file-and-use or flex rating.
Ratemaking
The process of calculating how much premium to charge for a type of insurance coverage. Considers expected losses, expenses, profit margin, and investment returns. Regulated by state insurance departments.
Rating Bureau
An organization that collects loss data from member insurers and develops loss costs and advisory rates used in pricing. ISO (property-casualty) and NCCI (workers compensation) are the largest.
Ready.gov — 12 Ways to Prepare ↗
A postcard-style reference listing 12 key preparedness actions. Signed up for alerts, documents safeguarded, plans made, supplies ready, etc. Easy to distribute to employees and clients.
Ready.gov — Are You Ready? Guide ↗
A comprehensive FEMA/Ready.gov guide on preparing for disasters within families and communities. Covers general preparedness, specific hazards, and recovery. A common starting point for personal and small-business preparedness programs.
Rebate (Illegal)
A refund or offering of any part of the agent's commission to the insured as an inducement to buy. Illegal in most states because it discriminates between policyholders.
Recovery
The process of restoring operations and conditions to normal (or better than normal) after an incident. Includes business continuity, claims management, rebuilding, employee support, and lessons learned.
Recovery Point Objective (RPO)
The maximum acceptable amount of data loss measured in time — e.g., "we can afford to lose no more than 4 hours of data." Drives the frequency of backups and replication.
Recovery Time Objective (RTO)
The target time within which a critical system or process must be restored after a disruption. Drives the design of recovery strategies and tools.
Referral Submission
A submission that exceeds an underwriter's individual authority and must be referred to a senior underwriter or home office. Common for larger accounts or unusual risks.
Reinstatement
Restoring a lapsed policy to full force and effect. Some insurers require evidence of insurability and payment of past-due premiums plus interest.
Reinsurance
A transaction between a primary insurer and another licensed insurer (the reinsurer) where the reinsurer agrees to cover all or part of the losses of the primary insurer in exchange for a premium. Allows insurers to spread their own risk.
Reinsurance Overview
Insurance for insurers. A primary insurer (the cedent) transfers part of its risk to a reinsurer in exchange for a share of the premium. Allows insurers to manage accumulation, write larger policies than their capital would otherwise support, and smooth loss experience over time.
Reinsurer
The insurance company that assumes reinsurance risk from another insurer (the ceding company).
Renewal Commission
Commission paid on policy renewals, typically at a lower rate than the new-business (first-year) commission in life insurance; generally the same rate in property-casualty.
Rental Reimbursement
Auto coverage paying for a rental car while your vehicle is being repaired after a covered loss. Typically subject to a daily dollar limit (e.g., $30/day) and a total limit (e.g., $900).
Renters Insurance
A form of property insurance available to renters that covers losses to their personal property and protects them from liability claims. Does not cover the physical structure — that is the landlord's responsibility.
Replacement Cost
The cost associated with replacing property at current market prices.
Replacement Cost Endorsement (Personal Property)
An endorsement that converts Coverage C from actual cash value (depreciated value) to replacement cost. Pays the current cost of similar new property without deduction for age or wear. Strongly recommended — a standard ACV settlement can leave the insured with a fraction of what was lost.
Replacement Cost Value (RCV)
Property coverage that pays the cost to repair or replace damaged property with new property of like kind and quality, without deduction for depreciation — subject to policy limits. Generally preferred over ACV for buildings and contents.
Representation
A statement by an applicant about a fact material to the insurance contract. Unlike a warranty, misrepresentations generally only void coverage if material and relied upon by the insurer.
Reputational Risk
A risk that can negatively impact a company's standing or public opinion. Reputational damage can result in profit losses, decreased shareholder confidence, customer attrition, and difficulty attracting talent. Triggered by product failures, ethical lapses, data breaches, executive misconduct, or poor crisis response. Mitigated through culture, crisis communications planning, and media monitoring.
Reservation of Rights (ROR)
A letter from the insurer to the insured stating the insurer will investigate or defend a claim but reserves the right to deny coverage if coverage questions are resolved against coverage. Protects the insurer from waiving coverage defenses while providing interim defense.
Reservation of Rights (ROR) Letter
A letter from an insurer to an insured stating that the insurer will investigate or defend a claim but reserves the right to later deny coverage based on policy terms. Required when there are coverage questions so the insurer doesn't waive defenses.
Reserves
A company's best estimate of what it will pay for claims. The amount representing actual or potential liabilities kept by an insurer to cover debts to policyholders.
Residual Market
State-mandated coverage programs for risks the voluntary market won't write — Assigned Risk Pools (auto, workers comp), FAIR Plans (property), and windstorm pools in coastal states.
Residual Risk
The amount of uncertainty that remains after all possible risk management strategies have been applied. You cannot eliminate all risk; the residual is what the organization must live with (or transfer via insurance).
Resource Management (Preparedness)
The process of identifying and organizing the resources needed to support all stages of preparedness and recovery — prevention, mitigation, emergency response, business continuity, crisis communications, and disaster recovery. Resources include people, supplies, facilities, equipment, and information.
Response
The immediate actions taken during and shortly after an incident to protect life, property, and operations. Includes emergency response, evacuations, medical care, containment, and initial assessment.
Retail Agent / Retail Broker
The agent or broker who deals directly with the insurance buyer (the insured). Contrasts with wholesale broker, who deals between retail and the carrier.
Retention (Insurance Company)
The amount of risk an insurance company keeps for its own account rather than transferring to reinsurers. High retention means the insurer absorbs more of each loss; low retention means more is ceded to reinsurance.
Retention Limit
The maximum amount of any one claim, or total claims, that an insurer will carry on its own without turning to reinsurance.
Retroactive Date (Claims-Made)
The earliest date a covered occurrence can have taken place for a claims-made policy to respond. Only events on or after the retroactive date are covered. Critical in professional liability and D&O coverage.
Retrocession
Reinsurance of reinsurance. A reinsurer transfers part of its assumed risk to another reinsurer (the retrocessionaire). Distributes risk across multiple layers of the global reinsurance market.
Retrospective Rating (Retro)
A rating plan that determines final premium based on actual losses during the policy period, within a minimum and maximum range. Rewards insureds for low losses, penalizes for high losses. Common for large commercial accounts.
Return-to-Work Program
A formal program that brings injured workers back to modified duty as soon as medically appropriate. Reduces workers compensation costs, improves worker recovery, and maintains productivity.
Risk
A situation involving exposure to danger, harm, or loss.
Risk Appetite
The amount and type of risk an organization is willing to take in pursuit of its objectives. Expressed quantitatively (maximum loss tolerance) or qualitatively (risk categories to avoid entirely).
Risk Assessment Matrix
A grid plotting the probability of loss against the severity of loss. Used to prioritize risks — high-probability / high-severity risks get top attention; low-probability / low-severity may be retained or ignored.
Risk Capacity
The maximum amount of risk an organization can bear — tied to capital, liquidity, and operational resilience. Differs from risk appetite, which is the amount the organization *chooses* to take within its capacity.
Risk Control (Operational)
Any measure that modifies risk by decreasing the likelihood that a risk event will occur, or reducing the impact the event would have on the organization.
Risk Equation
Level of Risk = Probability of Occurrence × Magnitude of Impact. The fundamental formula used in risk assessment.
Risk Financing
The category of risk management techniques that deal with paying for losses after they occur. Includes retention (self-funding) and contractual transfer (to an insurer or another party).
Risk Maturity Assessment
A structured evaluation of an organization's risk management capabilities against a defined maturity model. Used to identify gaps and plan improvements.
Risk Pooling
The core mechanism of insurance. Many policyholders with similar risks contribute premiums to a common pool. Losses for the unfortunate few are paid from the pool. Each individual transfers financial uncertainty to the group in exchange for a relatively small, predictable cost (the premium).
Risk Recommendation
A specific written suggestion from a loss control consultant to the insured to reduce exposure to loss. May be mandatory (required for continued coverage) or advisory. Tracked for completion.
Risk Register
A central document listing identified risks, their owners, probability, impact, existing controls, and treatment plans. A core tool of enterprise risk management.
Risk Tolerance
The amount of risk the organization can survive if an event occurs. Tolerance defines the boundary past which a risk becomes unacceptable.
Risk Transfer Hierarchy
The order of preference for addressing contractual risk: (1) avoid the activity; (2) structure the contract to eliminate exposure; (3) transfer risk through indemnification by the counterparty; (4) transfer risk through insurance; (5) retain the risk. Most real-world contracts use combinations.
Risk-Based Capital (RBC)
A regulatory formula determining the minimum capital an insurer should hold based on its risks (underwriting, investment, credit, operational). Four action levels with increasing regulatory response: Company Action Level, Regulatory Action Level, Authorized Control Level, Mandatory Control Level.
Risk-Sharing Pool
An association organized for the purpose of absorbing losses through a risk-sharing mechanism, thereby limiting any single member's exposure. Common in high-risk markets (e.g., FAIR Plans for property insurance in high-risk regions).
Road Safety — Forest Roads
Forest roads present unique hazards: narrow widths, steep grades, limited sight distance, wildlife, mud, icy conditions, and one-way traffic protocols. Truck driver training should specifically address forest road operations.
Roadside Assistance
Auto coverage paying for towing, battery jump-start, tire changes, lockout services, and fuel delivery when you break down. Often sold as a low-cost add-on.
Root Cause Analysis (RCA)
A problem-solving method used to identify the underlying cause(s) of an incident — not just the immediate cause but the system or process failure that permitted it. Common tools include the "5 Whys" and fishbone (Ishikawa) diagrams.
Round-Out (Account Rounding)
The practice of adding additional lines of business to an existing client's portfolio — for example, writing the auto and homeowners for a client who only has life insurance, or adding workers compensation for a commercial property client. Key retention strategy.
S
Safe Felling Distance (Two Tree Lengths)
The minimum safe distance from active felling operations — two times the height of the tree being cut. Applies to other workers, equipment, and structures. A 100-foot tree requires a 200-foot safe zone.
Safeguard Critical Documents and Valuables
Protect important records — insurance policies, deeds, titles, identification, financial records, medical records — against loss or damage. Options include fireproof safes, off-site safety deposit boxes, and encrypted cloud storage. Maintain duplicate copies in separate locations.
SAFER System
FMCSA's public database where shippers, brokers, and insurers can look up carrier safety records, insurance filings, and operational authority.
Safety Audit
An FMCSA review of a new entrant carrier's safety operations within the first 12 months. Checks compliance with regulations on driver qualification, HOS, vehicle maintenance, and drug/alcohol testing.
Safety Committee
A group of management and employee representatives that meets regularly to review safety performance, investigate incidents, and promote safe work practices. Required by law in some states for certain employer sizes.
Safety Culture
The shared values, attitudes, and behaviors that determine an organization's commitment to safety. A strong safety culture is a leading indicator of reduced losses. Measured through perception surveys and behavioral observations.
Safety Data Sheet (SDS)
A standardized document that communicates hazards of chemicals in the workplace. Required by OSHA's Hazard Communication Standard. Replaces the older "MSDS" (Material Safety Data Sheet). Must be accessible to all employees exposed to hazardous chemicals.
Salvage
The value recoverable after a loss. For example, after an insurer pays a claim on a totaled vehicle, the insurer may take the wreck and sell it for salvage value.
Salvage Title
A vehicle title designation indicating the vehicle was declared a total loss by an insurer. Signals to future buyers that the vehicle has significant damage history. Affects resale value and insurability.
Schedule of Insurance
A list attached to a policy identifying specific property or persons covered. For example, an inland marine schedule lists each piece of covered equipment; a scheduled personal property endorsement lists each jewelry item.
Schedule of Locations
A list attached to a commercial policy identifying each insured location, its address, and insurance details (construction, occupancy, protection, values). Required for multi-location accounts.
Schedule Rating
A rating plan that allows the underwriter to adjust the premium up or down based on specific characteristics of the risk that aren't reflected in the base rate — management controls, loss control programs, housekeeping, or adverse features.
Scheduled Personal Property Endorsement (Floater)
An endorsement that lists individual high-value items with appraised values — jewelry, fine art, furs, silverware, firearms, collectibles. Provides higher limits, open-perils coverage, and coverage for mysterious disappearance. Appraisals may be required every 3-5 years.
Selection Criteria for Risk Management Techniques
Effectiveness (how well the technique reduces risk) and economy (cost of the technique vs. expected loss).
Self Insurance
A formal program where an organization retains its own risk and pays losses from its own funds. Works best when losses are predictable based on historical data.
Self-Insured Retention (SIR)
The amount an insured pays from its own funds for losses that are not covered by the underlying primary but are covered by the umbrella's drop-down. Functions like a deductible but is typically paid directly to the claimant by the insured.
Service Line Coverage
An endorsement covering repair costs for underground utility lines that serve the home — water, sewer, electrical, cable, internet. Standard policies generally exclude these repairs.
Settlement Options
The methods by which the death benefit can be paid: lump sum (most common); interest only (beneficiary earns interest, principal paid later); fixed period (equal payments over a set number of years); fixed amount (fixed periodic payments until funds are exhausted); life income (payments for the beneficiary's lifetime).
Severity
Size of a loss; the extent of damages.
Sewer Backup Exclusion
Standard homeowners policies exclude water backing up through sewers or drains. Water backup coverage must be added by endorsement. Common cause of basement damage.
Short-Rate Cancellation Math
Short-rate refund is less than prorata refund to compensate the insurer for acquisition costs. Approximately 10% less refund than prorata. Used when the policyholder (not the insurer) cancels.
Short-Term Disability Insurance
Replaces a portion of income if the insured cannot work due to a non-work-related illness or injury. Benefits typically begin after a short waiting period (7-14 days) and last 3-24 months. Distinct from workers comp (work-related) and long-term disability.
Silent Cyber
Coverage ambiguity in traditional property and liability policies regarding cyber-related losses. Many carriers are clarifying policies with explicit cyber exclusions or affirmative endorsements. Agents should confirm cyber coverage in writing — don't assume a traditional policy responds to cyber losses.
Skidder Safety
Ground-based skidders present caught-between, rollover, and struck-by hazards. Core controls: ROPS (rollover protective structure), FOPS (falling object protective structure), seat belts, visibility training, and separation from workers on foot.
Skidding
Ground-based yarding using a tractor or skidder to drag logs along the ground. High-risk operation requiring trained operators and proper equipment.
Slash
The residual woody debris left after logging — tops, branches, broken pieces. Fire hazard; often must be piled and burned or treated per state requirements.
SMS (Safety Measurement System)
The FMCSA database that compiles CSA data for carriers, assigning percentile scores in each BASIC category. Publicly viewable; affects insurance underwriting, shipper qualification, and enforcement priority.
SOC 2 (System and Organization Controls 2)
An auditing standard from the American Institute of CPAs (AICPA) addressing security, availability, processing integrity, confidentiality, and privacy of customer data. Common requirement for SaaS providers and service organizations.
Social Engineering / Funds Transfer Fraud
Coverage for losses from fraudulent transfer instructions that trick the insured into voluntarily transferring funds to a criminal. Different from computer fraud (unauthorized access); involves the insured being deceived into authorizing the transfer. Often a separate sub-limit.
Social Security
A federal program that provides monthly benefits to millions of Americans, including retirees, military families, surviving families of deceased workers, and disabled individuals.
Soft Market
A period in the insurance cycle characterized by abundant supply of insurance capital driving premiums down. Underwriting becomes more flexible; coverage is easier to obtain.
Solvency
An insurer's ability to pay all claims and debts as they come due. State regulators monitor solvency through financial statement filings and capital/surplus requirements.
Special Sub-Limits in Homeowners
Standard policies sub-limit certain categories of personal property: jewelry and watches (typically $1,500-$2,500 for theft), firearms ($2,500), silverware ($2,500), money and coins ($200-$500), securities ($1,500), business property at home ($2,500), trailers ($1,500), and electronic data. Higher limits require scheduling individual items.
Specific Limit (Scheduled)
A limit applied individually to each location or item. Subject to the coinsurance penalty if values are under-reported at a specific location.
Specified Disease Coverage
Policy that provides benefits only for the diagnosis and/or treatment of a specific named disease such as cancer. Benefits can be paid as expenses incurred, per diem, or as a lump sum.
Speculative Risk
A risk with the possibility of gain, loss, or no change — such as investing in a business or the stock market. Generally NOT insurable. Contrasts with pure risk, which has only loss or no loss and IS insurable.
Spoilage Coverage
Coverage for loss of perishable stock due to power outage, refrigeration breakdown, or contamination. Critical for grocery, restaurants, pharmaceutical, and temperature-sensitive goods.
Sprinkler Leakage Insurance
Coverage for property damage caused by accidental, untimely discharge from an automatic sprinkler system.
Sprinkler System
An automatic fire suppression system. Types include wet pipe (water always in pipes), dry pipe (air in pipes until a head activates), pre-action (two-stage activation), and deluge (all heads open at once). Properly maintained sprinklers are one of the most effective property loss controls.
SR-22 Filing
A document an insurance company files with a state department of motor vehicles certifying that a driver carries the minimum required liability insurance. Required for drivers with DUI convictions, multiple violations, or uninsured accidents. Usually required for three years after an incident.
Stacking (Uninsured Motorist Coverage)
The ability to combine uninsured/underinsured motorist limits from multiple vehicles or policies to create a larger pool of coverage after an accident. Some states permit stacking, others explicitly prohibit it, and some allow it only if the premium has been paid for each stacked limit.
Staff Adjuster
An employee of the insurance company who investigates and settles claims. Represents the insurer's interests. Typically handles routine claims.
Stand-Alone Form
An excess policy with its own terms, conditions, and exclusions — not tied to the underlying primary. Used when specialty excess terms are needed.
Standard & Poor's (S&P) Insurance Rating
A credit rating assigned to insurance companies reflecting their ability to pay claims. Ranges from AAA (highest) down to D (default). Commonly required alongside or in place of AM Best ratings for certain accounts.
Standard Risk
A person or property that, according to a company's underwriting standards, is considered a normal risk and insurable at standard rates. Better-than-standard risks may qualify for discounts; worse-than-standard may pay higher rates or be declined.
Standing Timber
Trees that have not yet been cut. Property of the landowner; valued per board-foot or cord. Insurable under standing timber policies.
Standing Timber Insurance
Coverage for growing timber against specified perils — typically fire and lightning. Essential for large landowners, timber investment management organizations (TIMOs), and timber real estate investment trusts (REITs). Valued per board-foot or cord.
Standpipe
A piping system that delivers water to fire hoses within a building. Class I (fire department use), Class II (building occupant use), Class III (both).
Standpipe System
Piping system delivering water to fire hoses within a building. Class I (fire department connection and outlets), Class II (smaller hose for building occupants), Class III (combined, both types of outlets). Required in most buildings over a certain height.
State Guaranty Association
A state-operated pool that pays the claims of insolvent licensed (admitted) insurers. Funded through assessments on other admitted insurers in that state. Coverage limits vary by line of business. The existence of guaranty associations is a key reason admitted insurance provides additional consumer protection.
State Guaranty Fund
A state-mandated pool funded by assessments on admitted insurers that pays claims to policyholders if an admitted insurer becomes insolvent. Coverage limits vary by state and line of business. One key reason admitted coverage provides extra consumer protection.
State Insurance Commissioner
The head of a state's insurance department. Elected in some states, appointed in others. Enforces the state's insurance code, licenses insurers and producers, approves rates and forms, investigates complaints, and can take regulatory action against companies (market conduct exams, fines, license suspension).
State Insurance Department
The state-level agency that regulates insurance companies, agents, and brokers doing business in that state. Issues licenses, approves rates and forms, monitors solvency, and handles consumer complaints. Every state has one — often called the Department of Insurance (DOI).
State Regulation of Insurance
Insurance is regulated primarily at the state level in the U.S. (McCarran-Ferguson Act of 1945 affirms state regulation). Each state has an insurance department headed by a commissioner. Regulators license companies and agents, approve rates and forms, monitor solvency, and handle consumer complaints.
Statutory Accounting Principles (SAP)
The accounting rules insurance companies must use for financial statements filed with state regulators. More conservative than GAAP — prioritizes solvency monitoring over profit measurement. Requires certain assets to be "non-admitted" (excluded from the balance sheet).
Stop Loss Insurance
A policy designed to protect a self-insured organization from the risk of a single catastrophic loss or cumulative losses above a predetermined attachment point.
Strategic Risk
The risk resulting from a company's faulty business strategy — or lack of one. Includes entering wrong markets, misreading customer needs, underinvesting in innovation, poor mergers and acquisitions, or failing to respond to competitive threats. Often the largest category of risk by potential impact. Governed at the board and executive level.
Struck-By Hazards (Logging)
The category of accidents involving a person being hit by a moving object — falling trees and limbs ("widowmakers"), rolling logs, swinging cables, falling loads. The leading cause of logging fatalities.
Submission
A complete package sent to an underwriter by a producer seeking a quote — applications, loss runs, financials, supplemental questionnaires, schedules, and narrative information about the risk.
Subrogation
The legal process by which an insurance company, after paying a loss, seeks to recover the amount of the loss from another party who is legally liable.
Subrogation Rights
The insurer's right to pursue recovery from a third party responsible for a loss after paying the insured. Flows from the equitable principle that the ultimately responsible party should bear the loss. Insureds should avoid settling with responsible parties or signing releases without insurer input.
Substandard Risk
A risk that presents a higher-than-average chance of loss due to medical condition, hazardous occupation, or other factors. Requires the use of a waiver, a special policy form, or a higher premium charge.
Succession Plan
A documented plan for managing personnel loss — replacing key people due to retirement, disability, death, or resignation — so the organization can continue operating.
Supplementary Payments
Payments made IN ADDITION to the policy's limit of liability. Includes: cost of bail bonds up to $2,000 for violations because of a covered accident; expenses the insured incurs at the insurer's request, including lost earnings up to $250 a day; court costs the insured is required to pay because of a suit; interest that accrues after a judgment and before it is paid; all expenses incurred by the insurer.
Supply Chain Risk
Risk of disruption to an organization's supply of goods or services from vendors, suppliers, or logistics providers. Can arise from supplier bankruptcy, geopolitical events, natural disasters, or concentration risk (relying on a single source). Mitigated through supplier diversification, inventory buffers, and contingency sourcing.
Surcharge
An extra charge applied by the insurer — commonly for accidents, moving violations, or other adverse factors in auto insurance.
Surety
An arrangement where one party becomes answerable to a third party for the acts of a second party. Customarily an insurance company, the surety holds itself responsible to the obligee for the acts of the principal.
Surplus Lines Broker
A specially licensed broker authorized to place business with non-admitted carriers when coverage is unavailable in the admitted market. Required to document the diligent effort to first seek admitted coverage.
Surplus Lines Insurance
Specialized property or liability coverage placed with an insurer not licensed in the state, used when coverage is unavailable from any licensed (admitted) insurer. Often used for unusual or high-risk exposures.
Surplus Lines Market
Non-admitted insurance markets available for risks unacceptable to or unavailable from the admitted market. Accessed through specially licensed surplus lines brokers. Not backed by state guaranty funds but offers greater flexibility in pricing and terms. Typical of specialty risks, catastrophic exposures, and unusual classes.
Surplus Share Reinsurance
A proportional reinsurance arrangement where the reinsurer takes a portion of risks exceeding the primary insurer's retention. Used to handle larger policies without burning excess capacity on smaller ones.
Surrender (Life Policy)
To terminate or cancel a life insurance policy before the maturity date. For a cash-value policy, the policyholder receives the cash surrender value (less any outstanding loans and surrender charges).
Symbol 1 (Any Auto)
Broadest form. Any auto the insured will use during the policy period, including autos that are owned, leased, hired, rented, or borrowed.
Symbol 19 (Mobile Equipment Subject to Motor Vehicle Laws Only)
Autos that would be considered mobile equipment if they were not subject to compulsory or financial responsibility laws or other motor vehicle laws.
Symbol 2 (Owned Autos Only)
Any auto the insured owns.
Symbol 3 (Owned Private Passenger Autos Only)
Any private passenger auto the insured owns.
Symbol 30 (Garage Keepers)
Customers' autos left with the insured for service, repair, storage, or safekeeping (called garage keepers coverage).
Symbol 31 (Dealers' Autos)
Physical damage coverage only for the dealers' autos, including owned autos, autos for sale, and autos on consignment.
Symbol 4 (Owned Autos Other Than Private Passenger Autos Only)
Other types of vehicles the insured owns, such as trucks, trailers, buses, and motorcycles.
Symbol 48 (Trailer Interchange — Borrowed Trailers)
Trailers borrowed or leased by the named insured for which liability for loss has been assumed under a written trailer interchange agreement.
Symbol 49 (Trailer Interchange — Loaned Trailers)
Trailers owned or hired by the named insured while the trailers are in someone else's possession under a written trailer interchange agreement.
Symbol 5 (Owned Autos Subject to No-Fault Law)
Designates owned autos required to have no-fault benefits in a particular state.
Symbol 6 (Owned Autos Subject to Compulsory UM Law)
Designates owned autos required to have uninsured motorists coverage in a particular state.
Symbol 7 (Specifically Described Autos)
Most restrictive form. Applies only to autos specifically listed in the business auto coverage form declarations.
Symbol 8 (Hired Autos Only)
Only used for autos the insured has leased, hired, rented, or borrowed. Does not include autos rented or borrowed from employees.
Symbol 9 (Non-Owned Autos Only)
Only used for autos used in the insured's business that are not leased, hired, rented, or borrowed. Includes autos owned by employees but used in the insured's business or personal affairs.
Symbol Designations (Commercial Property)
Letter codes on property declarations identifying the covered cause-of-loss form: "Basic" (named perils), "Broad" (named perils plus falling objects, weight of snow/ice), and "Special" (open perils except exclusions). Special form is most common for commercial.
T
Table-Top Exercise (TTX)
A discussion-based drill in which managers walk through a scenario to test plans, decision-making, and coordination — without physical deployment of resources. Most cost-effective way to test preparedness plans.
Tangible Property
Property with physical existence. Includes real property (land, buildings, fixed improvements) and personal property (movable property such as equipment, inventory, vehicles).
Telematics
Electronic devices or smartphone apps that capture vehicle data — location, speed, hard braking, acceleration, cornering, idle time. Used in fleet safety programs to coach drivers and in commercial auto underwriting to price policies based on actual behavior.
Telematics / ELD Data
Electronic data captured from vehicles — location, speed, hard braking, rapid acceleration, harsh cornering, hours of service, idle time. Used for safety coaching, fuel efficiency, dispatch optimization. Increasingly used by insurers for pricing based on actual behavior.
Term Life Insurance
Life insurance providing coverage for a specified term (10, 20, 30 years) with no cash value. Premiums are lower than permanent insurance. If the insured survives the term, coverage ends unless renewed (usually at higher rates). Best for temporary needs — covering a mortgage, supporting young children until adulthood.
Third Party
A person other than the insured or insurer who has incurred losses, or who is entitled to receive payment due to acts or omissions of the insured. A third-party claim is made against someone else's insurance.
Third Party Loss
A situation involving a person other than the insurer and insured; i.e., a person making a liability claim against the insured.
Third-Party Cyber Coverages
Cover liability to others: liability for breaching third-party personal data, regulatory defense, transmission of malware to a customer's system, media liability (online content), and payment card industry (PCI) fines and assessments.
Three Lines Model (Institute of Internal Auditors)
An updated governance model replacing the "Three Lines of Defense." First line: operational management. Second line: risk management, compliance, and other support functions. Third line: internal audit providing independent assurance to the board.
Three Lines of Defense
A governance model for risk management: (1) operational management owns and manages risk; (2) risk and compliance functions provide oversight; (3) internal audit provides independent assurance. Widely adopted in financial services.
Timber Rattler / Binder
The chain-and-lever device used to secure logs to a truck. Improper tensioning or release causes many injuries. Standing to the side of released binders is the rule.
Time Element Coverage
A category of coverages triggered by the time it takes to recover from a loss — business income, extra expense, extended period of indemnity, civil authority, and ingress/egress.
Title Insurance
Coverage that guarantees the validity of a title to real property (and sometimes personal property). Protects buyers and mortgage lenders against losses from undiscovered defects — liens, encumbrances, or ownership disputes — that existed before the policy was issued. Two types: Lender's Policy and Owner's Policy.
Title Insurance — Lender's Policy
When you take out a mortgage, the lender requires lender's title insurance to protect the lender's investment against losses from title claims. This policy protects the lender only — not the buyer.
Title Insurance — Owner's Policy
Separate title insurance policy that protects the buyer's financial interest in the property. Strongly recommended because the lender's policy does not protect the owner.
Total Cost of Risk (TCOR)
The full cost of risk to an organization: insurance premiums + retained losses + loss control expenses + risk management administration + indirect costs (lost productivity, reputation damage). TCOR analysis is the foundation of strategic risk management.
Total Loss Threshold
The point at which an insurer declares a damaged vehicle a total loss rather than repairing it. Calculated as a percentage of actual cash value (typically 70-80%) or by comparing repair cost plus salvage value to pre-loss value. Varies by state law and insurer.
Total Recordable Incident Rate (TRIR)
The rate of all OSHA-recordable incidents per 100 full-time workers per year. Formula: (Recordable incidents × 200,000) ÷ total hours worked. Key benchmark for comparing workplace safety performance.
Trailer Interchange Insurance
Covers damage to a specific trailer under the policy of the trucker in whose possession the trailer is at the time of loss. For coverage to apply, the trucker must be liable under a written interchange agreement, and the damage must be caused by a covered peril.
Travel Insurance
Covers financial loss due to trip cancellation or interruption; lost or damaged baggage; trip or baggage delays; missed connections or itinerary changes; and losses due to rental vehicle damage or medical emergencies while traveling.
Treaty (Reinsurance)
A reinsurance agreement between the ceding company and reinsurer in which the reinsurer automatically accepts a share of all risks that meet specified criteria within the treaty.
Treaty Reinsurance
A standing reinsurance agreement covering an entire class of business meeting specified criteria. Risks falling within the treaty terms are automatically ceded. Simplifies the placement process for routine business.
Trucker's Bobtail Liability
Liability coverage for a truck tractor being operated without a trailer, or with an empty trailer, when not under dispatch for a motor carrier. Common for owner-operators whose primary coverage only responds when under dispatch.
Trucker's Non-Trucking Liability
Similar to bobtail — covers the tractor when used for purposes other than business of the motor carrier (personal use, going home from work). Excludes business use, which is the primary carrier's exposure.
Truckers Coverage Form
A modified version of the business auto coverage form that takes into consideration the special practices and regulations that apply to the trucking industry. Includes three coverages: (1) liability, (2) trailer interchange, (3) physical damage.
Two Main Processes of Risk Management
(1) Decision process — identifying, analyzing, and selecting risk management techniques; (2) Administrative process — implementing, monitoring, and adjusting those techniques over time.
Types of Exposed Values
Property (real and personal), personal (bodily injury, death, disability), net income (lost revenue, extra expense), and liability (third-party claims).
Types of Perils
Natural perils (weather, flood, earthquake), human perils (theft, vandalism, error), and economic perils (recession, currency fluctuation, supplier failure).
U
Umbrella Insurance (Personal)
Non-business liability protection for individuals above a specific limit set in a basic policy (auto, homeowners). Also covers known or unknown gaps in basic coverages. Typically starts at $1 million.
Umpire
