For Property coverage, if a company and a claimant fail to agree on the amount of loss, each may appoint an appraiser, and these in turn select an umpire. A decision by two of the three is binding.
Underinsurance (Homeowners)
A condition where the dwelling limit is less than the replacement cost. Triggers a coinsurance penalty on partial losses and leaves the homeowner uncovered for total losses. A common problem as construction costs rise faster than policy limits.
Underwriter
An employee of an insurance company who evaluates an individual's risk factors and assigns premium amounts.
Underwriting
The process an insurance company uses to decide whether to accept or reject an application for a policy.
Underwriting Cycle
The cyclical pattern of insurance market conditions — alternating between soft markets (abundant capacity, lower rates, relaxed underwriting) and hard markets (constrained capacity, higher rates, stricter underwriting). Typically lasts 3-10 years per cycle.
Unearned Premium
Amount of premium for which payment has been made by the policyholder but coverage has not yet been provided. If a policy is canceled, the unearned premium is refunded (pro-rata or short-rate).
Uninsured Motorist — Bodily Injury (UMBI)
Pays you and your passengers for bodily injury caused by a negligent uninsured motorist, a hit-and-run driver, or by a driver whose insurer is insolvent.
Uninsured Motorist — Property Damage (UMPD)
Pays for damage to your automobile, up to a stated limit, when caused by a negligent uninsured motorist.
Uninsured/Underinsured Coverage
Auto insurance that protects you against another driver not having enough coverage to pay the entirety of your losses.
Universal Life Insurance
Flexible permanent life insurance. Premiums can be varied within limits; death benefit can be adjusted. Cash value earns interest at a current declared rate (with a guaranteed minimum). More flexible than whole life but requires monitoring — underfunding can cause the policy to lapse.
Unknown Known
A risk we mistakenly think we understand but actually do not. Often more dangerous than unknown unknowns because organizations act with false confidence.
Unknown Unknown
A risk that is not even recognized to exist — a "blindsiding" event. Cannot be planned for directly; mitigated through flexibility, resilience, and contingency funds.
Upside Risk
An opportunity that arises out of uncertainty about outcomes. Not all risk is bad — some uncertainty creates upside potential (e.g., a new market opportunity).
US-CERT — IT Security Essential Body of Knowledge ↗
The United States Computer Emergency Readiness Team's foundational IT security guide. A practical curriculum for IT security practitioners.
USDOT Physical / DOT Medical Card
The medical certification required for interstate commercial drivers, issued by a medical examiner on the FMCSA's National Registry. Valid for up to 24 months.
USGS — Earthquake Hazards Information ↗
United States Geological Survey resources on earthquake hazards, including fault maps, ground-shaking models, and building vulnerability data. Used for property underwriting in seismic zones.
USGS — Landslide Hazards Program ↗
USGS mapping and guidance on landslide-prone areas. Relevant for properties on steep terrain, recent burn scars, and certain geological formations.
USGS — Volcano Hazards Program ↗
USGS monitoring and advisory information for active volcanoes in the U.S. Relevant for operations in Pacific Northwest, Alaska, and Hawaii.
Utility Services Interruption
Coverage for loss of business income or property damage resulting from a loss at a utility — power plant, water plant, communication facility — that serves the insured.
Utmost Good Faith (Uberrimae Fidei)
The legal principle that both parties to an insurance contract owe each other complete honesty and full disclosure. More demanding than the standard of good faith in ordinary commercial contracts.
V
Vacancy Provision
A provision reducing or eliminating coverage for certain perils once a building has been vacant for 60+ days. Applies to vandalism, glass breakage, water damage, theft, and sprinkler leakage from freezing. Vacant buildings are higher risk; insureds should notify the insurer of vacancy.
Valued Policy
An insurance contract for which the value is agreed upon in advance and is paid in full in the event of a total loss, regardless of the actual cash value at the time of loss. Common for fine art, antiques, collectibles.
Vanishing Deductible
An auto policy feature that reduces a deductible by a set amount each year of safe driving, to a minimum (sometimes zero). Rewards long-term loss-free policyholders.
Variable Life Insurance
Permanent life insurance where cash value and (in some cases) death benefit vary with the performance of underlying investment funds chosen by the policyholder. Higher upside and higher downside than other permanent life forms. Considered a security; agents need additional securities licensing.
Variable Universal Life (VUL)
Combines the flexibility of universal life (adjustable premium, adjustable death benefit) with the investment component of variable life (separate accounts chosen by policyholder). Most complex life product; requires ongoing management.
Vision Insurance
Limited-benefit expense policy providing benefits for eye care — routine eye exams, glasses, and contact lenses. May include surgical benefits for eye injury or illness.
Voluntary Market
The standard commercial insurance market where risks are written by carriers voluntarily at market rates. Contrasts with the residual or assigned-risk market.
Vulnerability
The degree of probability that a loss will occur. Higher vulnerability means a given peril is more likely to result in loss.
Vulnerability Assessment
A structured evaluation of an organization's susceptibility to specific threats. Used in facility security, cybersecurity, and property risk engineering. Complements hazard identification by focusing on organizational weaknesses rather than external threats.
W
Waiting Period
A period of time set forth in a policy that must pass before some or all coverages begin. Common in disability, long-term care, and health insurance policies.
Waiver of Premium Rider
A life insurance rider that waives premium payments if the insured becomes totally disabled. Coverage continues without premium payments until the insured recovers or reaches a specified age.
Waiver of Subrogation
A provision (in a contract or as a policy endorsement) in which the insurer gives up its right to pursue recovery from a specified third party after paying a claim. Commonly required in leases and construction contracts. Requires both contract language AND carrier endorsement.
Waiver of Subrogation Endorsement
An endorsement to a commercial policy committing the insurer not to pursue recovery from specified parties. Commonly required by contracts — leases, construction contracts, vendor agreements.
Warranty (Insurance Contract)
A statement by the insured that certain facts are or will be true. Historically, any breach of warranty voided the contract; modern law in many states requires materiality. A "protective safeguard warranty" requires the insured to maintain specified safety systems.
Water Backup Coverage
An endorsement covering damage from water backing up through sewers or drains, or overflowing from a sump pump. Excluded from standard policies — frequently needed in basements and lower-level construction.
Water Damage vs. Flood
Standard homeowners covers sudden and accidental water damage from plumbing failures (burst pipes, appliance leaks). Flood — water rising from the ground, overflow of natural water bodies, mudflow, or water backup from sewers — is excluded. Flood requires a separate NFIP or private flood policy; water backup requires a separate endorsement.
Wear and Tear Exclusion
Standard property policies exclude losses from gradual deterioration, wear and tear, or inherent vice. Property is insured against sudden and accidental events, not maintenance issues.
Wet Pipe Sprinkler System
The most common sprinkler type. Pipes are always filled with water. When a sprinkler head activates from heat, water flows immediately. Fastest response. Only appropriate in buildings where freezing is not a concern.
Whole Life Insurance
Permanent life insurance with guaranteed level premiums, a guaranteed death benefit, and a guaranteed cash value that grows at a fixed rate. Premiums are higher than term because they include the savings component. Builds cash value that can be borrowed against or withdrawn.
Wholesale Broker
A broker who places business for retail agents, often accessing surplus lines markets or specialty carriers not available to retail. Retail agent brings the relationship and risk; wholesaler brings market access.
Widowmaker
A dead or broken branch, or a partially fallen tree, that can fall unexpectedly. A major hazard in logging operations. Inspection and removal before felling and yarding is a core control.
Wildfire Risk (Forestry Operations)
Logging operations can ignite wildfires. Controls include: spark-arrested exhausts on equipment, fire extinguishers on equipment, water supply on-site, daily fire danger assessment, shutdown during extreme fire danger, and post-operation monitoring.
Workers Comp — Part One (Workers Compensation)
Provides statutorily required benefits to employees for work-related injuries or diseases. Pays according to the state's workers compensation statute — benefits are set by law, not by policy limits. The insurer is obligated to pay whatever the state requires.
Workers Comp — Part Two (Employers Liability)
Provides coverage for employer liability for work-related injuries NOT covered under the state's workers comp statute — typically for third-party actions (the worker's spouse sues for loss of consortium), gaps in the workers comp system, or claims in states where the employer has not purchased workers comp coverage. Limits typically $100,000/$500,000/$100,000.
Workers Compensation Benefits (Four Categories)
(1) Medical — covers all necessary medical treatment for work-related injuries; (2) Indemnity — partial wage replacement while the worker is unable to work; (3) Rehabilitation — vocational or physical rehabilitation to return the worker to productive employment; (4) Death Benefits — compensation to surviving dependents for work-related deaths.
Workers' Compensation Insurance
State-mandated insurance that provides coverage for employee injuries and illnesses that occur on the job. Pays medical expenses and lost wages for injured workers and protects employers from most employee injury lawsuits.
Worry Value
The psychological cost of bearing uncertainty. Increases with: higher variability of possible losses; lower decision-maker confidence; larger possible loss size; higher probability of maximum loss; weaker financial strength; less insurance coverage. Insurance reduces worry value even when actual expected losses are unchanged.
Written Premium
The total premium contractually charged by the insurer to the policyholder for the effective period of the contract. Differs from earned premium — written premium is contractually owed; earned premium is what the insurer has "earned" through time in force.
Y
Yarding
Moving felled and bucked logs from the stump to a landing area where they can be loaded. Ground-based (skidder, forwarder), cable (yarder), or aerial (helicopter) yarding systems.
Risk Management

Risk Management Fundamentals

Two Key Definitions of Risk

  • Definition 1
  • “Risk” can refer to a situation in which there is an expected loss (i.e., possibility of something happening)
  • Definition 2
  • “Risk” can also refer to a situation in which there is variability around an expected result/value/loss
  • Uncertainty (variability around the expected loss)
  • Expected Loss
  • One situation is riskier than the other if it has greater

Risk: Expected Loss Definition

  • Definition 1
  • “Risk” can refer to a situation in which there is an expected loss
  • Example
  • Assume two identical homes in equally attractive locations, both worth $100K
  • Announcement that a meteor will strike the earth and completely destroy one home within the next week with a probability of 0.1 (10%)
  • Expected Loss for Home in Impact Zone = $100,000 x 0.1 = $10,000
  • Expected Loss for Home NOT in Impact Zone = $100,000 x 0 = $0
  • Q: How much would you pay for home in the impact zone?
  • A: Rational person would pay at least $10,000 less (i.e., pay only $90K or less: (Value of House) – (Expected Loss) = $100,000 – [0.1($100,000)+0.9($0)] = $100,000 – 10,000 - $ = $90,000

Risk: Variability Definition

  • Definition 2
  • “Risk” can also refer to a situation in which there is variability around an expected loss/result
  • Example
  • Value of house NOT in impact zone = $100K with certainty
  • House in impact zone worth either $0 or $100K
  • Such a high degree of uncertainty will likely impose additional costs on the owner
  • Many potential buyers would offer less than $90K (i.e., maybe only $85K to reflect their unease with the uncertainty. How much that penalty is depends on the buyer’s degree of risk aversion.
  • Q: Are you risk averse?
  • A: Generally yes, but sometimes not. Also, humans are historically and notoriously terrible at evaluating most risks.

Defining Risk — Expected Loss & Variability

Insurance vs. Gambling

  • Insurance
  • Handles an already existing “pure” risk
  • Is always socially productive
  • both parties have a common interest in the prevention of a loss
  • Gambling
  • Creates a new speculative risk
  • Is not socially productive
  • The winner’s gain comes at the expense of the loser
  • Yeah, Yeah—Gambling is not socially productive but with $1.2 billion at stake I’m willing to ignore that fact. Anyway, this is South Carolina’s “Education Lottery” so it’s okay to play!

Hurricane Hugo (Sept. 1989): Once the Costliest Natural Disaster in US History in Terms of Insured Losses

Hurricanes Debbie: Summary

  • Hurricane/TS Debbie Idalia made landfall on August 5th, 2024 along the Florida Gulf Coast as a CAT 1 storm (winds > 74 mph and again as TS in SC
  • Heavy rain and storm surge occurred along its path
  • Storms with similar tracks/strength usually produce several billion dollars in insured losses
  • Grace
  • Henri

Can You Name Other Recent Big Disasters Around the World so Far in 2023?

  • Maui Wildfire (8/21/23)
  • 115 fatalities (as of 8/28)
  • >2,200 damaged/destroyed buildings
  • ~$3.3B insured losses
  • Turkey/Syria Earthquake (2/6/23; 7.5 – 7.8 mag.)
  • ~58,000 fatalities
  • >160,000 collapsed/ severely damaged buildings
  • ~$5B insured losses

risk:a BRIEF HISTORY

  • Why Did a Solid Understanding of Risk Elude Humankind for So Long?
  • “Acts of God” and Insurance

Risk Is an Enormous Burden on Society

  • The Presence of Risk Results in 3 Major Burdens on Society
  • In the absence of insurance, individuals and business firms would have to maintain large emergency funds to pay for unexpected losses
  • The risk of a liability (and resulting lawsuit) may be so large that it may discourage innovation, depriving society of certain goods and services
  • Risk of untimely death can impoverish a family, destroy a business
  • Risk causes worry and fear
  • Deters investment (not just financial investments, but research, exploration, etc.)
  • Deters consumption (e.g., people afraid to dine out, stay in hotel, fly)
  • Can damage/destroy societies, economies, cultures, deplete savings
  • RECALL: Humans are inherently risk averse. Management of risk has enabled societies, economies, industries and individuals to take risks (and reap the rewards) they otherwise would not be able to do individually.

Risk Management Through Most of Human History…

  • Had this eclipse happened over the Yucatan Peninsula about 1,200 years ago—this might have been how risk management was practiced—a human sacrifice to the Mayan sun god, Kinich-Ahua
  • Total Solar Eclipse
  • Aug. 21, 2017
  • (Picture take from the rooftop of the Darla of Business)

What is the #1 Killer Disease of All Time?

  • Killed an estimated 608,000 people worldwide in 2022 (CDC)
  • Has killed an estimated 4-5% of all humans who have ever lived!!!
  • Changed the course of human history many times
  • There is still no cure
  • MALARIA

How to Stop a Plague in the Medieval Period

  • St. Sebastian Inteceding for the Plague Stricken, by Josse Lieferinxe (c. 1497).
  • Depicts the overall Christian mindset about the plague in Europe during the 14th century. God and the battle scene in the sky, as well as Saint Sebastian, the sick priest, the dying grave attendant, and the dead bodies for burial, are all important pieces of the painting because they illustrate this mindset. Late medieval Christians believed that God caused the black plague as a punishment and Saint Sebastian was one of the intercessors.
  • Why was St. Sebastian thought of a good way to protect one’s self from the plague?
  • Source:

How to Stop a Plague in the Medieval Period

  • Plague Column in Bratislava, Slovakia
  • (built in 1719).
  • Plague columns are common in Central European cities. They are intended to memorialize the victims of the plague and also thank God (and the local king) for delivering the people from the plague.

What Actually Caused the “Black Death?”

  • Yesenia Pestis—a bacteria
  • Transmitted from infected rats to humans via the bite of infected fleas
  • 14th Century Risk Management
  • 21st Century Risk Management

Risk: It’s Everywhere and Always—Why So Hard for Humans to Understand?

  • For the vast majority of human history, what happened to people, the socio-economic groups to which they belonged and the environment in which they lived was attributed the will or whim of the gods (“Acts of God” is still a commonly used phrase)
  • The view that humans are not passive before nature is a relatively new human concept!
  • Dates roughly to the early Renaissance (14th – 17thC)
  • “[N]ature has established patterns originating in the return of events, but only for the most part…”
  • --German mathematician and philosopher Gottfried Leibniz in a comment to Swiss scientist and mathematician Jacob Bernoulli, 1703
  • Leibniz
  • Bernoulli

Risk: It’s Everywhere and Always—Why So Hard for Humans to Understand?

  • 18th Century Lightbulb Moment: If risk results in patterns it is therefore measureable
  • If risk in measurable it must be treatable (i.e., we can do something about it)
  • The language, measurements and methods to evaluate risk (modern probability theory) are recent inventions
  • Institutional Resistance
  • Religious, cultural and political barriers to the interpretation of nature through the lens of science (including advances in mathematics and statistics) remained significant for centuries and to some extent exert influences even today
  • Humans are often terrible at risk perception; For most of history as a species we have been exceedingly superstitious
  • Scientific methods for mitigating against risk are largely 20th century concepts (very recent)

Modern Risk Management: Ask Taylor Swift

  • Taylor Swift’s Eras Tour
  • Decided to cancel 3 shows in Vienna in Aug. 2024
  • Threat of terrorist attack
  • 2 radicalized teenagers arrested
  • On Aug. 22, Swift spoke for the first time about the foiled attack saying that the cancelations filled her with “a new sense of fear” and a “tremendous amount of guilt”
  • In May 2017, 22 people were killed at an Ariana Grande concert in Manchester, UK
  • But Swift concert promoters had $12M in insurance to cover such losses
  • Source:

Number and Value of Deals Is Increasing

  • In Search of the Elusive Insurance ‘Unicorn’
  • InsurTech
  • INSURANCE TECHNOLOGY

InsurTech Funding Volume, Early Stage 2012:Q1 – 2019:Q2

  • Has early stage InsurTech funding reach a plateau?
  • P/C accounts for the majority of early stage InsurTech funding

Global Private Technology Investments by (Re)Insurers 2012:Q1 – 2019:Q2

  • Private technology investments by (re)insurers in 2019 should be comparable to the record investments of 2018-19

types of risk

  • Common Categorizations of Risk

Types of Risk: Pure vs. Speculative

  • Pure Risk: A chance of loss or no loss, but no chance of gain
  • Examples: A building burns or doesn’t burn; Car crashes or doesn’t
  • Always undesirable; Neither outcome produces a gain
  • Speculative Risk: Involves a chance of gain
  • Examples
  • Price Risk: Risk associated with change in price of inputs as well as market price for completed outputs
  • Credit Risk: Risk that customers and parties to whom the company has lent money delay or fail to make promised payments
  • Market Risk: Risk associated with change in price of financial securities (e.g., stocks and bonds)
  • Interest Rate Risk: Risk associated with value of a security because of changes in interest rates (e.g., If Fed raises rates, value of existing bonds may fall)
  • Liquidity Risk: Risk associated with being able to easily liquidate an investment
  • Exchange Rate Risk: Risk associated with the value of assets (financial or physical) based on fluctuations in exchange rates

Types of Risk: Objective vs. Subjective

  • Objective Risk: A risk where the degree of variation in uncertain outcomes can be measured (quantified) based on facts, data and analysis
  • Examples
  • Hurricane Risk: Based on actual history of insured losses, catastrophe modeling, climate science, etc.
  • Mortality Risk: Based on data associated with decades of statistics related to death statistics
  • Subjective Risk: Perceived degree of risk is based on an individual’s (or an organization’s) opinion
  • Some people refuse to fly and drive instead, even though flying (commercially) is far safer
  • People grossly overestimate their odds of being killed in many ways: shark attack, terrorism, flying, contracting certain diseases (e.g., Zika, Ebola)
  • People grossly underestimate many risks: home being flooded; likelihood of death from smoking or being overweight or not wearing a motorcycle helmet
  • NOTE: Bias and lack of awareness increase subjectivity

Hurricanes Idalia: Summary

  • Hurricane Idalia came ashore Wednesday morning along the Florida Gulf Coast as a CAT 3 storm (winds > 111 mph
  • Heavy rain and storm surge occurred in FL, GA, SC, NC
  • No official insured loss estimates yet, but similar usually produce several billion dollars in insured losses
  • Grace
  • Henri
  • 5PM on 8/30/23

Objective vs. Subjective Risk

Driven to Distraction: The Facts

  • Distracted driving is responsible for more than 3,100 crash deaths annually in the US
  • Approximately 10% of all fatal car crashes are attributed to distracted driving (3,477 distracted driving deaths in 2015 out 35,097 from all causes)
  • Distracted driving behaviors (all forms) lead to more than 420,000 injuries each year
  • Nearly 1/3 of all U.S. drivers 18 to 64 years old read or send text or email messages while driving
  • Simply knowing the risks of distracted driving has not yet translated into reducing the behavior
  • Implication: Awareness alone is unlikely to solve the problem

Distracted Driving Remains a Huge Problem, Accounting for an Estimate 10% of Fatal Traffic Accidents…This Guy Was Lucky

Chance of Loss vs. Objective Risk

  • Chance of loss is the probability that an event that causes a loss will occur.
  • Objective risk is the relative variation of actual loss from expected loss
  • The chance of loss may be identical for two different groups, but objective risk may be quite different!
  • City: Philadelphia · # homes: 10,000 · Average # Fires/Yr.: 100 · Range in # of Fires/Yr.: 75 – 125 · Chance of Fire: 1% · Objective Risk: 25%
  • City: Los Angeles · # homes: 10,000 · Average # Fires/Yr.: 100 · Range in # of Fires/Yr.: 90 - 110 · Chance of Fire: 1% · Objective Risk: 10%
  • Question: Which city is riskier for an insurer? Philadephia or Los Angeles? Why?

Diversification and the Volatility of Stock Portfolios

  • If hold a portfolio of firms with only non-diversifiable risk, then risk is the same no matter how many stocks are held (e.g. own stocks only of earthquake insurers)
  • If hold portfolio of firms with only of purely diversifiable risk, then risk is essentially completely eliminated as number stocks held increases
  • Typical stocks have a blend of both systemic and unsystemic risks

Application: The Effect of Diversification on Portfolio Volatility (2-Stock Portfolio)

Application: Diversification in Stock Portfolios

  • Unsystematic (Diversifiable) vs Systematic Risk (Undiversifiable)
  • Over any given period of time, the risk of holding a stock is that the dividends plus the difference purchase and sale price will be different than expected, which make holding the stock risky
  • What accounts for returns to differ from expectations? NEWS (i.e., Information)
  • Stock prices are impacted by two types of news
  • Company or Industry-Specific News
  • Market-Wide News
  • Unsystematic Risk (e.g., FB’s problems)
  • Systematic Risk (e.g., Higher interest rates)

Application: Diversification in Stock Portfolios

  • Unsystematic Versus Systematic Risk
  • Stock prices are impacted by 2 types of news
  • Company or Industry-Specific News: Unsystematic Risk
  • Good or bad news about a company (or industry) itself. Example: firm might announce that a newly introduced product has succeeded in gaining market share (FB’s data problems, modest (e.g., 10%) aluminum tariff)
  • Market-Wide News: Systematic Risk
  • News that affects the economy overall and therefore affects all stocks (Fed raises interest rates, Trump’s election, Trade War)

Application: Diversification in Stock Portfolios

  • Diversifiable Risk and the Risk Premium
  • The risk premium of a stock is not affected by its diversifiable, unsystematic risk
  • In other words, you’re not going to get an extra return (i.e., risk premium) for holding a stock whose risk can be diversified
  • The risk premium for diversifiable risk is zero
  • Investors are not compensated for holding unsystematic risk

the risk management process

  • A Methodology for Managing Risk

Additional Common Definitions of Risk

  • Traditional Definitions of Risk: Uncertainty concerning the occurrence of a loss
  • ALSO an FYI: In the insurance industry, the term “risk” is also used to identify the property or life that is being considered for insurance
  • E.g., Dave is a “bad risk” because he has 2 DUIs
  • In economics and finance
  • The term risk is used in situations where the probabilities of possible outcomes are known or can be estimated with some degree of precision (e.g., prob. of dying at a given age)
  • Uncertainty is used in situations where such probabilities cannot be (precisely) estimated (e.g., prob. of being killed by a meteor; likelihood of being killed in the US by a terrorist); Financial markets “Climb a Wall of Worry”

Loss Exposure: Any situation or circumstance in which a loss is possible (i.e., there is a risk of loss), regardless of whether a loss occurs, e.g.

  • Factory damaged in a flood or by a tsunami or an earthquake
  • Worker killed on the job by a fall from a roof
  • Computer hacked resulting in the theft of sensitive info
  • Obese patient being diagnosed with diabetes
  • CAN YOU NAME A LOSS EXPOSURE?
  • Terminology: Loss Exposure

The Enterprise Risk Management Process

  • ERM can be time consuming and costly, but done well can give a company a competitive advantage. Done poorly, can result is disaster or bankruptcy.

The Risk Management Process

  • Identification of all significant risks
  • Information collection
  • Evaluation of potential frequency and severity of losses
  • Frequency = rate of loss
  • Severity = cost of loss; usually measured in monetary terms
  • Develop and select methods for managing risk
  • Method(s) might need to be developed
  • Implementation of chosen risk management methods
  • Monitoring the performance and suitability of the methods on an ongoing basis
  • Learn by doing and make adjustments as necessary

ERM Failures

  • Can you name some companies/organizations that have demonstrated ERM shortcomings in recent years?
  • CrowdStrike
  • Delta Airlines
  • Red Lobster
  • Silicon Valley Bank
  • Boeing—Multiple Reasons
  • Norfolk Southern Railroad
  • Uvalde, TX, School District

Major Commercial (Business) Risks

  • Firms face a variety of pure risks that can have serious financial consequences if a loss occurs, including
  • Property risks: such as damage to buildings, machinery, furniture and office equipment
  • Liability risks: such as suits for defective products, pollution, privacy breach and sexual harassment
  • Loss of business income: when the firm must shut down for some time after a physical damage loss

Major Commercial Risks (Continued)

  • Other risks faced by business firms include
  • Human resources exposures, such as job-related injuries; Recently—sexual harassment claims
  • Foreign loss exposures, such as acts of terrorism
  • Intangible property exposures, such as damage to the market reputation and public image of the company
  • Intellectual Property, such as patents, computer code
  • Government Exposures, such as violation of safety standards or increased regulations
  • Supply Chain, such as an inability to procure inputs to production, logistics issues, etc.
  • Cybersecurity exposures associated with intrusions into a firm’s computer systems or those affecting its supply chain

Workplace Discrimination Complaints Filed with the EEOC

Types of Business Risk & Management Methods

Major Risk Management Methods

  • Investments in technology
  • Risk avoidance
  • Education, training

Techniques for Managing Risk

  • Risk (Loss) Control refers to techniques that reduce the frequency of losses
  • Avoidance: e.g., oil driller avoids drilling within 5 miles of homes to avoid the possibility of groundwater contamination
  • Loss prevention refers to activities to reduce the frequency of losses (e.g., driver safety training, anti-harassment training, tech)
  • Loss Reduction refers to activities to reduce the severity (cost) of losses
  • Duplication: e.g., routinely back up servers
  • Separation: e.g., don’t locate all of your manufacturing facilities at the same site
  • Diversification: e.g., don’t depend on a single supplier for a key component

Internal Risk Reduction

  • Diversification: Diversification of business activities
  • i.e., “Don’t put all of your eggs in one basket”
  • Diversify in terms of products, geography, suppliers, customers, etc. (e.g., Apple shifting some iPhone production out China to India, Vietnam)
  • Portfolio/asset diversification for financial firms
  • Invest in Information: To obtain superior forecasts in terms of expected losses (works for speculative and pure risks)

Techniques for Managing Risk (Continued)

  • Risk (Loss) Financing: Retention refers to techniques that provide for payment of losses after they occur through self-funding
  • Retention means that an individual or business firm retains part or all of the losses that can result from a given risk.
  • Active Retention means that an individual is aware of the risk and deliberately plans to retain all or part of it
  • Passive Retention means risks may be unknowingly retained because of ignorance, indifference, or laziness

Techniques for Managing Risk (Continued)

  • Loss Financing (cont’d)
  • Self Insurance is a special form of planned retention by which part or all of a given loss exposure is retained by the firm; Losses are retained using methods typically employed by insurers.
  • Captive Insurance An insurance subsidiary of a company whose primary business is not insurance. Professionally managed.

Techniques for Managing Risk (Continued)

  • Loss Financing (cont’d)
  • Noninsurance Transfer transfers a risk to another party.
  • A transfer of risk by contract, such as through a hold-harmless clause in a contract
  • Hedging is a technique for transferring the risk of unfavorable price fluctuations to a speculator
  • Incorporation of a business firm transfers to the creditors the risk of having insufficient assets and other potential liabilities
  • Insurance: For most people, insurance is the most practical method for handling major risks
  • Transfer of a pure risk to an insurer
  • Insurance is pooling technique and is used to spread the losses of the few over the entire group
  • The risk may be reduced by application of the law of large numbers

The Enterprise Risk Management Process

  • ERM can be time consuming and costly, but done well can give a company a competitive advantage. Done poorly, can result is disaster or bankruptcy.

Major Personal Risks (as Opposed to Corporate)

  • Personal Risks are risks that directly affect an individual or family. They involve the possibility of a loss or reduction in income, extra expenses or depletion of financial assets as well as loss of property value, due to
  • Premature death (mortality risk)
  • Inadequate retirement income (longevity risk)
  • Poor health (morbidity risk)
  • Unemployment
  • Property loss/damage (pure risk)
  • Liability (pure risk)
  • TO LEARN MORE—TAKE MY FINA 442 CLASS (Life & Health Insurance)

Exhibit 1.1 Total Savings and Investments Reported by Retirees, Among Those Responding

  • (not including value of primary residence or defined benefit plans)
  • 53% of retirees have less than $25K in retirement assets

Major Personal Risks (Continued)

  • Property Risks involve the possibility of losses associated with the destruction or theft of property
  • Direct loss vs. indirect loss
  • A direct loss is a financial loss that results from the physical damage, destruction, or theft of the property, such as fire damage to a home
  • Could also apply to damage to theft of files from computer system as a result of a data breach
  • An indirect or consequential loss is a financial loss that results indirectly from the occurrence of a direct physical damage or theft loss (e.g., the additional living expenses after a fire).
  • Business Interruption Loss: Loss of business (resulting in loss of profits) while business is being repaired (or moved) due to physical loss or damage occurring (e.g., after a hurricane)

  • YOUR Estimates: Collision Claim
  • Estimate 1
  • Estimate 2
  • Estimate 3

  • Estimate 1
  • Estimate 2
  • Estimate 3
  • Estimate 4

  • 2.62% = # claims per 100 insured car years  means that approximately 1 out of every 38 insured vehicles had a PD liability claim made on the policy in 2023

Collision Claim Severity: Rising to New Record Highs

  • Average Loss, 2018:Q1 through 2023:Q4
  • Severities Are Up Sharply—Inflation Is a Major Factor
  • Collision claim severity reached a record high in 2023:Q1, up 42.6% from 2020:Q1
  • Q4:2023 Collision claim severity is still up 39.4% since Q1:2020

Major Personal Risks (Continued)

  • Liability risks involve the possibility of being held legally liable for bodily injury or property damage to someone else
  • There is no maximum upper limit with respect to the amount of the loss
  • A lien can be placed on your income and financial assets
  • Legal defense costs can be enormous
  • Can you name the #1 and #2 reasons you as a homeowner are likely to be sued?
  • Slip & falls
  • Dog bites! (average dog bite costs insurers about $30,000!)

Major Types of Personal Risks

  • Individuals (and households) face many types of risk which often lend themselves to insurance solutions

components of the cost of risk

  • Risky Business—The Costs of Managing Risk
  • Managing/Controlling Risks is Not Free!

Components of the Cost of Risk

  • Risk avoidance
  • Investments in technology
  • Education, training
  • Losses are costly. But reducing the frequency and severity of loss is not free and can be quite costly as well.

5 Components of the Cost of Loss

  • Expected Losses: Includes both direct and indirect losses. In auto manufacturer example direct losses include cost of litigation and settlements as well product recall and replacement. Indirect costs include lost sales if production has to be slowed or company’s reputation is damaged.
  • Cost of Loss Control: Reflects costs of increased precautionary measures (e.g., additional testing)
  • Cost of Loss Financing: Includes cost of self-insurance, the profit and expense “loading” in insurance premiums (actual premium is part of firm’s expected losses), transaction costs in negotiating, arranging hedging and other contractual risk transfers
  • Cost of Internal Risk Reduction Methods: Includes transaction costs associated with achieving diversification and managing diverse set of activities as well as the cost of information
  • Cost of Residual Uncertainty: In general, there will always be some uncertainty “left over” even after the selection and implementation of Loss Control, Loss Financing and Internal Risk Reduction
  • Can affect, for example shareholders, pay demands of executives

loss financing

  • Funding Options and Considerations for Loss Payment or Offset

Four Methods of Loss (Risk) Financing

  • Retention: Situation when an individual/business retains the obligation to pay some or all of the losses
  • Insurance: Insurance company pays losses specified in a contract in exchange for a premium
  • Hedging: Refers to the use of derivatives contracts such as futures, forwards, options and swaps
  • Individuals, small businesses rarely use this option
  • Larger businesses do to guard against such things a commodity price fluctuation
  • Example: Fuel is the second largest cost for airlines. Airlines routinely hedge against fuel price fluctuation by entering into futures contract with a fuel producer (refinery) that guarantees delivery of a specific quantity of fuel in the future at a price established today
  • Other Contractual Risk Transfers: Parties doing business with one another routinely include in their agreements language about which party shall assume liability (e.g., hold harmless agreement)

Objectives of Risk Management

objectives of risk management

  • Goal is to Minimize the Cost of Risk to Maximize the Value of the Firm

Risk Is Costly—So Is Management of that Risk

  • Example
  • Automobile Manufacturer: Vehicles always run the risk of being manufactured with defects in parts, assembly—and increasingly software.
  • To minimize this risk, which can lead to expensive individual or class action lawsuits and fines, car manufacturers (and their suppliers) exhaustively test components and test crash vehicles
  • Safety equipment in vehicles also makes them safer, but is costly
  • These testing programs and safety equipment are expensive and time consuming and add significantly to the cost of vehicles
  • The Point: Risk and Risk Reduction are both costly so our objective is minimize the cost of risk to business, the individual or to society (depending on the entity under consideration)
  • Note that actions minimizing costs to society may differ from actions minimizing cost to the firm/individual

Pictures of New Orleans After Katrina (8/05)

Risk Management in Action Every Day

  • Lysteria outbreak—traced to a Boar’s Head plant in Jarratt, VA
  • 57 hospitalizations, 9 deaths (incl. 2 in SC) across 18 states since CDC first reported the problem on July 19
  • During inspections in the 12 months prior to the outbreak, inspectors found insects, mold and mildew in the plant
  • 7 million pounds of meat recalled

Definition of Insurance

  • Insurance: The pooling of fortuitous losses by transfer of such risks to insurers, who agree to indemnify (i.e., pay) insureds for such losses, to provide other pecuniary (i.e., monetary) benefits on their occurrence, or to render services connected with the risk

Basic Characteristics of Insurance

  • Pooling of Losses
  • Pooling involves spreading losses incurred by the few over the entire group
  • Risk reduction is based on the Law of Large Numbers
  • According to the Law of Large Numbers, the greater the number of exposures, the more closely will the actual results approach the probable results that are expected from an infinite number of exposures.

Basic Characteristics of Insurance (Continued)

  • Example of Expect Loss and SD w/o Pooling
  • Two business owners own identical buildings valued at $50,000
  • There is a 10% chance each building will be destroyed by a peril in any year (and therefore a 90% it won’t be destroyed)
  • Loss to either building is an independent event
  • Expected value and standard deviation of the loss for each owner individually is

Basic Characteristics of Insurance (Continued)

  • Example, continued, but WITH Pooling
  • If the owners instead pool (combine) their loss exposures, and each agrees to pay an equal share of any loss that might occur
  • Neither building destroyed: 0.9 x 0.9 = 0.81
  • Bldg. A Destroyed, Bldg. B no loss: 0.1 x 0.9 = .09
  • Bldg. A no loss, Bldg. B Destroyed: 0.9 x 0.1 = .09
  • Both Bldgs Destroyed: 0.1 x 0.1 = .01
  • As additional individuals are added to the pool, the standard deviation continues to decline while the expected value of the loss remains unchanged
  • $25,000 = $50,000/2 = shared amount if one structure or the other is destroyed

Basic Characteristics of Insurance (Continued)

  • Payment of Fortuitous Losses
  • A fortuitous loss is one that is unforeseen, unexpected, and occur as a result of chance
  • Risk Transfer
  • A pure risk is transferred from the insured to the insurer, who typically is in a stronger financial position
  • Indemnification
  • The insured is restored to his or her approximate financial position prior to the occurrence of the loss

6 Characteristics of an Ideally Insurable Risk

  • Large number of exposure units
  • to predict average loss based on the law of large numbers
  • Accidental and unintentional loss
  • to assure random occurrence of events
  • Determinable and measurable loss
  • to determine how much should be paid

6 Characteristics of an Ideally Insurable Risk (Continued)

  • No Exceedingly Catastrophic Loss
  • to allow the pooling technique to work (i.e., not all policyholders can suffer the same loss)
  • exposures to catastrophic loss can be managed by using reinsurance, dispersing coverage over a large geographic area, or using financial instruments, such as catastrophe bonds
  • Calculable Chance of Loss
  • to establish a premium that is sufficient to pay all claims and expenses and yields a profit during the policy period
  • Economically Feasible Premium
  • so people can afford to purchase the policy
  • For insurance to be an attractive purchase, the premiums paid must be substantially less than the face value, or amount, of the policy

Exhibit 2.2 Unemployment as an Insurable Risk

  • Many other risks are difficult to fully insure because they don’t meet all of these criteria. Examples: Terrorism, Cyber Risk, Pandemics, Home Prices

2 Factors that Can Interfere with Insurability

  • We’ve discussed a number of conditions for insurability, but other factors can have an influence as well
  • Adverse Selection
  • Moral Hazard

Examples of Underwriting Criteria

  • Personal Auto Insurance
  • Gender
  • Loss experience
  • Driving record
  • Distance driven
  • Credit profile
  • Marital status
  • Policy continuity
  • Life Insurance
  • Occupation
  • Education
  • Medical history
  • Health status
  • Income/Wealth/Credit Score
  • For Auto, there is a movement toward usage-based insurance (UBI) and telematics. This involves the use of devices in vehicles of smart phone apps that assess your driving in real time. This information is transmitted to the insurer and premiums are adjusted accordingly. Criteria might include frequency of speeding, hard breaking, rapid acceleration, night vs. day driving, etc.
  • Examples: Allstate Drivewise; Progressive Snapshot

Example of Telematics and UBI: Allstate Drivewise App

  • Source:

Example of Telematics and UBI: Allstate Drivewise App

  • Source:

Insurance vs. Hedging

  • Insurance
  • Risk is transferred by a contract
  • Involves the transfer of pure (insurable) risks
  • Moral hazard and adverse selection are more severe problems for insurers
  • Hedging
  • Involves risks that are typically uninsurable
  • Often speculative risks
  • Fewer problems of moral hazard and adverse selection for entities who buy or sell futures contracts

Types of Private Insurance (Continued)

  • Property and Liability
  • Property insurance indemnifies property owners against the loss or damage of real or personal property
  • Liability insurance covers the insured’s legal liability arising out of property damage or bodily injury to others
  • Casualty insurance refers to insurance that covers whatever is not covered by fire, marine, and life insurance

Types of Private Insurance (Continued)

  • Private insurance coverages can be grouped into two major categories
  • Personal Lines: coverages that insure the real estate and personal property of individuals and families or provide protection against legal liability
  • Personal Auto
  • Homeowners
  • Personal Umbrella (i.e., liability coverage in excess of auto/home limits)
  • Commercial Lines: coverages for business firms, nonprofit organizations, and government agencies
  • Commercial Property -Directors and Officers
  • Commercial Auto -Medical Professional Liability
  • Workers Comp -Products Liability
  • Marine -Inland Marine
  • Surety -Aviation…and many more!

Types of Private Insurance

  • Life (Mortality) and Health (Morbidity)
  • Life Insurance pays death benefits to beneficiaries when the insured dies
  • Annuities: Pays while annuitant is still alive but pricing is driven by likelihood of death
  • Health Insurance covers medical expenses because of sickness or injury
  • We will also discuss
  • Disability
  • Long-term care
  • Workers compensation

Types of Government Insurance

  • Social Insurance Programs
  • Financed entirely or in large part by contributions from employers and/or employees
  • Benefits are heavily weighted in favor of low-income groups
  • Eligibility and benefits are prescribed by statute
  • Examples: Medicare, Medicaid, Social Security, Unemployment
  • Other Government Insurance Programs
  • Found at both the federal and state level
  • Examples: Federal flood insurance (NFIP), state health insurance pools

Three Major Categories of Business Risk

  • Price Risk: Risk associated with change in price of inputs (e.g., oil, gas, iron ore, wheat, etc.) as well as market price for completed outputs (e.g., cars/trucks, plastic, steel, bread, etc.)
  • Credit Risk: Risk that customers and parties to whom the company has lent money delay or fail to make promised payments
  • Not just a banking risk; Virtually every company has accounts receivable that can become delinquent
  • Pure Risk: Risks for which there is no potential for gain
  • Damage, destruction, loss of physical assets
  • Legal liability risks (e.g., to customers, suppliers, shareholders, etc.)
  • Liability to workers (and sometimes their family members) injured or killed on the job (under state workers compensation laws)

Direct vs. Indirect Losses

  • Direct Losses: Losses (with financial consequences) that arise as the immediate consequence of an adverse event
  • Loss of value to a home destroyed by meteor or car destroyed in an accident
  • Indirect Losses: Losses that arise as the result of a direct loss that has occurred.
  • Indirect losses can be very large, especially for businesses
  • If a business is damage by a fire, it may be closed for repairs for days of weeks, resulting in a loss of income and potentially addition expenses
  • For personal risks, indirect losses can be costly too.
  • Example 1: If a home is damaged by fire, the family may need to live in a hotel or rent and apartment while repairs are made; May need t
  • Example 2: May need to take a day of from work without pay to get a car repaired that’s been damaged in an accident.

Major Types of Personal Risks

  • Individuals (and households) face many types of risk which often lend themselves to insurance solutions

RISK Minimization

  • Tradeoffs Are Necessary

Social Costs of Insurance

  • The Cost of Providing Insurance Adds to the Costs of Protecting Against Risk. Why?
  • Because of the Cost of Doing Business
  • An expense loading is the amount needed to pay all expenses, including commissions, general administrative expenses, state premium taxes, acquisition expenses, and an allowance for contingencies and profit
  • Fraudulent Claims
  • Inflated Claims
  • -Higher premiums to cover additional losses reduce disposable income and consumption of other goods and services
  • -Hence, while insurance is an efficient solution to the financing of risk, it is not costless to the consumer (or society overall)

Risk Management and Societal Welfare

  • From a societal standpoint, the question (and goal) is how risky activities and risk management by businesses and individuals can be arranged to minimize the total costs to society (all of its members).
  • Minimizing total risk to society yields an efficient level of risk.
  • The efficient level of risk requires that individuals businesses pursue risk management activities until the marginal reduction in the expected cost of losses equals the marginal cost of these risk management activities

Risk Management and Societal Welfare

Risk Management and Societal Welfare

  • As a general rule, the private cost of risk (total cost to businesses) will differ from the social cost of risk (total cost to society)
  • Example 1: In absence of workplace safety regulation and workers compensation statutes, businesses would have a greatly reduced incentive to protect workers on the job from the risk of injury or death.
  • This is clearly not optimal from a societal perspective given the high cost of injuries, workers unable to earn income if injured, families’ loss of wage earners if disabled/killed on the job, plus many other indirect costs. Can you name a few?
  • Legal liability system and govt. regulation seek to prevent this

Tradeoffs in Risk Management

  • RECALL: Risk is costly; Treatment of risk is costly
  • 2 Principal Tradeoffs: (vice versas apply)
  • The more spent on loss controllower the direct (and indirect) expected losses
  • The more spent on loss financing/internal risk reductionlower the residual uncertainty
  • Risk, in general, can never be reduced to zero (without ceasing operations); 1+ of these apply
  • Not technologically feasible
  • Not feasible in terms of cost
  • Not feasible in terms of consumer tastes and preferences
  • The Case of Jarts:

Tradeoffs in Risk Management

  • Babies and Buckets

Tradeoffs in Risk Management

  • Cribs
  • No more than 2 3/8 inches (about the width of a soda can) between crib slats so a baby's body cannot fit through the slats; no missing or cracked slats.
  • No corner posts over 1/16th inch high so a baby's clothing cannot catch.
  • No cutouts in the headboard or foot board so a baby's head cannot get trapped.
  • A firm, tight-fitting mattress so a baby cannot get trapped between the mattress and the crib

Terrorism Risk Insurance

  • Losses and Lines of Insurance Covered by Terrorism Insurance
  • 1. The Terrorism Risk Insurance Program requires all commercial property-casualty insurers to make available coverage for losses resulting from acts of terrorism as defined in the act.
  • The term “act of terrorism” means any act that is certified by the United States secretary of the Treasury, in consultation with the secretary of Homeland Security and the U.S. attorney general, to meet all these criteria
  • a. It is an act of terrorism.
  • b. It is a violent act or an act that is dangerous to human life
  • property, or infrastructure.
  • c. It resulted in damage within the U.S. (except under specific
  • instances).
  • d. It was committed by an individual or individuals as part of an effort to coerce the civilian population of the U.S. or influence U.S. government policy or conduct through coercion.

RISK Minimization (CONT’d)

  • Tradeoffs Are Necessary

Firm Value Maximization and the Cost of Risk

  • Basic Equation
  • Cost of Risk = (Value w/o Risk) – (Value w/Risk) The firm’s value w/o risk is hypothetical and represents the value of the firm when uncertainty in cash flows is reduced to zero—and at zero cost.
  • We can restate this equation to represent firm’s value to shareholders
  • Value w/Risk = (Value w/o Risk) – (Cost of Risk) Implies that the value of the firm is maximized when its cost of risk is minimized
  • As a practical matter, businesses (esp. small businesses) will not attempt to quantify all risks, but management still needs to understand risks in general and how their magnitude will be impacted by risk management efforts.

Firm Value Maximization and the Cost of Risk

  • Value of a Business: Determined primarily by expected magnitude, timing and risk (variability) associated with future net cash flows (inflows minus outflows) that will be available to provide shareholders (owners) with a return on their investment
  • Volatility (Risk) in Cash Flows: It is a fundamental business principle of business valuation that increased risk reduces firm value and increases the expected return required by investors.

Which Firm Will Have a Higher Valuation?

  • Firm A: Stock pays $100 dividend/share per year in perpetuity with an interest rate of 5% and a standard deviation in those returns of 10%
  • Firm B: Stock pays $100 dividend/share per year in perpetuity with an interest rate of 5% and a standard deviation in those returns of 15%?
  • Solution: Value (i.e. present value) of this investment in both cases is D/r = (Dividend/Interest Rate) = $100/.05 = $2,000
  • But Firm A will be worth more to risk averse investors because there’s less volatility in the stream of earnings!
  • Firm C: What if there were a Firm C, with the same D and r but where the standard deviation was 0%? Is it worth more or less than Firm A?

RISK identification and measurement

  • The Quantification of Risk

Risk Identification

  • Risk Identification: First step in the risk management process.
  • Businesses: Adherence to a solid RM program would require that a business conduct a thorough survey of risks and the direct and indirect losses that might arise (.1, p, 43 in custom text—gives representative questions that might be asked in this process for a business and its property and liability exposures)
  • Individuals: For individuals, a good way to identify risks is to identify sources and use of funds in the present and planned for use to the future

RISK measurement

  • Basic Concepts from Probability and Statistics

Risk Measurement

  • Probability and Statistics: Provide us with a way to measure both kinds of risk
  • Expected Value
  • Variance

Concepts in Probability and Statistics We’ll Study to Analyze Risk

  • Random Variable: A variable whose outcome is uncertain
  • Probability Distribution: Identifies all the possible outcomes for a random variable and the probability (likelihood) of each outcome
  • Characteristics of Probability Distributions
  • Expected value
  • Variance and standard deviation
  • Skewness
  • Correlation

Probability and Statistics (Continued)

  • The mean ( ) or expected value =
  • For example, expected losses for washing machine
  • Amount of Loss (Xi) · Probability of Loss (Pi) · XiPi
  • $ 0 · X · 0.30 · = · $ 0
  • $360 · X · 0.50 · = · $180
  • $600 · X · 0.20 · = · $120
  • = · $300

Probability and Statistics (Continued)

  • The variance of a probability distribution is
  • For the previous loss distribution

Probability and Statistics (Continued)

  • The standard deviation =
  • Higher standard deviations, relative to the mean, are associated with greater uncertainty of loss; therefore, the risk is greater

Probability and Statistics

  • The probability of an event is the long-run relative frequency of the event, given an infinite number of trials with no changes in the underlying conditions.
  • Probabilities can be summarized through a probability distribution
  • Distributions may be discrete or continuous
  • A probability distribution is characterized by
  • A mean, or measure of central tendency
  • A variance, or measure of dispersion

Law of Large Numbers

  • The Law of Large Numbers is the mathematical foundation of insurance.
  • LLN: As sample size increase  infinity, sample mean population mean
  • Average losses for a random sample of n exposure units will follow a normal distribution because of the Central Limit Theorem.
  • Regardless of the population distribution, the distribution of sample means will approach the normal distribution as the sample size increases.
  • The standard error of the sampling distribution can be reduced by increasing the sample size
  • In Plain English: If I repeatedly sample from the same population, the distribution of the means themselves in normally distributed

Exhibit A2.1 Sampling Distribution Versus Sample Size

  • Central Limit Theorem: States that as the sample size increases, the sampling distribution of the mean approaches a standard normal distribution, regardless of the population distribution

Standard Error of the Sampling Distribution Versus Sample Size (Exhibit A2.2 from text)

  • Mathematically—the standard error of the distribution mean decreases as the number of insured units (n) increases

Law of Large Numbers (Continued)

  • When an insurer increases the size of the sample of insureds (i.e., policyholders)
  • Underwriting risk increases, because more insured units could suffer a loss (i.e., the insurer could lose more).
  • But, underwriting risk does not increase proportionately. It increases by the square root of the increase in the sample size.
  • There is “safety in numbers” for insurers!
  • When an investor increases the # stocks in their portfolio
  • Investment risk increases, because more stocks could suffer a loss (i.e., the investor could lose more).
  • But, investment risk does not increase proportionately. It increases by the square root of the increase in the portfolio size (if equally weighted).
  • There is “safety in numbers” for investors too! What is this effect called?
  • Diversification

Probability distributions

  • The Shape of Things!

Two Ways of Representing Probability Distributions

  • RECALL: Probability Distribution: Identifies (i) all the possible outcomes for a random variable and (ii) the probability (likelihood) of each outcome
  • Numerical Listing (usually in a table)
  • Outcomes (the X’s in the EV equation)
  • Probabilities (the P’s in the EV equation)
  • Graphical Depiction
  • Sum of probabilities always equals 1 (i.e., 100%)

Example: Discrete Probability Distribution

  • Random variable = damage from auto accidents
  • Possible Outcomes for Damages Probability
  • $0 0.50
  • $200 0.30
  • $1,000 0.10
  • $5,000 0.06
  • $10,000 0.04
  • Sum of the probabilities = 1.00

Example of a Discrete Probability Distribution

Continuous Distributions

  • Important characteristic of continuous distributions
  • Area under the entire curve always equals one
  • Area under the curve between two points gives the probability of outcomes falling within that given range
  • Extremes values on the left and right side of the distribution are called the “tail”
  • Insurers frequently talk about tail risk when they discuss very infrequent but potentially very severe events (e.g., 1-in-1,000 year flood)

Example of a Continuous Distribution

  • “Normal” Distribution with it’s familiar bell shape
  • “Normal” Distribution is perfectly symmetric

Probabilities with Continuous Distributions

  • Find the probability that the loss > $5,000
  • Find the probability that the loss < $2,000
  • Find the probability that $2,000 < loss < $5,000
  • Probability
  • Many (most) distributions that characterize loss seen by insurers and risk managers are not symmetric
  • $2,000
  • $5,000
  • Possible Losses

Example of Actual Personal Auto Collision Claim Data for SC

Comparing Expected Values of Two Otherwise Identical Distributions

  • EV = 0
  • EV Dist. B > EV Dist. A
  • EV = 2
  • Outcome
  • Probability Density

Risk Management & Probability Distributions

  • Ideally, a risk manager would know the probability distribution of losses
  • Then, the RM would assess how different risk management approaches would change the probability distribution
  • Which distribution would you rather have?
  • Accident Cost
  • No Risk Management
  • Prob
  • Use of Insurance

Risk Management & Probability Distributions

  • Which distribution would you rather have?
  • Think about the possibility of an extreme loss that could severely disrupt or bankrupt your company.
  • You could buy insurance to protect against that possibility
  • Accident Cost
  • No Risk Management
  • Prob
  • Use of Insurance
  • Lots of risk from higher severity (costlier) accidents
  • Business left mostly with less costly accidents (which it retains); Costlier claims go to the insurer
Insurance Operations

Insurance & Pooling Fundamentals

Terrorism Risk Insurance

  • Losses and Lines of Insurance Covered by Terrorism Insurance
  • 1. The Terrorism Risk Insurance Program requires all commercial property-casualty insurers to make available coverage for losses resulting from acts of terrorism as defined in the act.
  • The term “act of terrorism” means any act that is certified by the United States secretary of the Treasury, in consultation with the secretary of Homeland Security and the U.S. attorney general, to meet all these criteria
  • a. It is an act of terrorism.
  • b. It is a violent act or an act that is dangerous to human life
  • property, or infrastructure.
  • c. It resulted in damage within the U.S. (except under specific
  • instances).
  • d. It was committed by an individual or individuals as part of an effort to coerce the civilian population of the U.S. or influence U.S. government policy or conduct through coercion.

Variance and Standard Deviation

  • Variance: Variance of a probability distribution provides information on the likelihood and magnitude that a particular outcome will differ from the expected value
  • Standard Deviation: Square root of the variance
  • More convenient to use this measure

Comparing Standard Deviations of Two Distributions w/ Same Expected Value

  • EV = 2
  • Outcome
  • Probability Density
  • Much more of the “mass” of the distribution is in the “tail”, meaning an increased probability of extreme (tail) events

Example: Calculation of Standard Deviation of 3 Distributions

  • Distribution 1 · Distribution 2 · Distribution 3
  • Loss Outcome · Probability · Loss Outcome · Probability · Loss Outcome · Probability
  • $250 · 0.33 · $0 · 0.33 · $0 · 0.4
  • $500 · 0.34 · $500 · 0.34 · $500 · 0.2
  • $750 · 0.33 · $1,000 · 0.33 · $1,000 · 0.4
  • EV = $500 · EV = $500 · EV = $500

Skewness

  • Skewness measures the symmetry of the distribution
  • No skewness  symmetric
  • Implies losses are evenly distributed on both sides of the expected value (as in the bell-shaped “normal” distribution)
  • Most loss distributions exhibit skewness
  • Most loss distributions are “skewed right” meaning that the preponderance of the losses are lower severity, but that the right-hand tail is relatively fat and therefore a reasonable chance of an large observation

Examples of Skewness

  • Right skews (aka “positive skew”) is common in loss distributions. Most extreme values are in the right tail.
  • Suggests larger losses are relatively more common than smaller losses. Most extreme losses are in the left tail.
  • Severity $
  • Losses above and below the mean loss are equally likely to occur

Many Types of Distributions Can Be Used to Model Insurance Losses

  • Log-Normal
  • Gamma
  • Poisson

Maximum probable loss and value at risk

  • Related Concepts

Maximum Probable Loss (MPL)

  • Maximum Probable Loss: Usually describes a loss distribution
  • In the figure below representing annual liability losses, if the area to the right of $20 mill represents 5% of the area in the loss distribution, then $20 mill is the MPL at the 5% level. Losses will be < $20 mill 95% of the time.
  • The MPL at the 1% level is $30 mill. Implies losses will be < $30 mill 99% of the time
  • Since this is a loss distribution, higher numbers are bad; lower numbers are good. Insurers and RMs are very focused on limiting their MPL. Will likely buy reinsurance to limit losses beyond a certain threshold.

Value at Risk (VaR)

  • Value at Risk: VaR describes the probability distribution for the value of a portfolio or the value of a firm subject to loss, usually over a specified time interval
  • If Value at Risk at the 5% level for the next month is $20 million, then
  • Prob(loss in portfolio value ≥ $20 million) = 0.05
  • In words, there is 5% chance that the portfolio will lose more $20 million over the next month

Value at Risk (VAR)

  • VAR is similar to maximum probable loss, except it is usually applied to the value of a portfolio (e.g., stocks)
  • Key distinction is that the VAR refers to the change (reduction) in portfolio value from the expected portfolio value, at some level of probability.
  • If the Value at Risk at the 5% level for the next month equals $20 million, then
  • Prob (change in portfolio value < -$20 million) = 0.05
  • In words, there is 5% chance that the portfolio will lose more than $20 million over the next month

Value at Risk

  • Example
  • Assume VAR at the 5% level =$5 million
  • And VAR at the 1% level = $7.5 million
  • Since this is a distribution of changes in portfolio values, higher values are good

Value at Risk (VaR)

  • In the figure below representing the monthly change for the value in an investment portfolio, if the area to the left of $5 mill represents 5% of the area in the distribution, then the probability that the portfolio will lose more than $5 mill over the next month is 5%. In other words, $5 mill is the monthly VaR for this portfolio at the 5% level. Losses will be < $5 mill 95% of the time.
  • The VaR at the 1% level in this portfolio is $7.5 mill meaning that the probability that the portfolio will lose more than $7.5 mill is 1%.

Useful characteristics of the normal distribution in calculating mpl and vAr

  • Applied Examples

Useful Percentile Values for the Normal Distribution

  • The probability that the gain in the portfolio exceeds +$2.33 million is 1%
  • The probability that the gain in the portfolio exceeds +$1.645 million is 5%
  • The probability that the gain in the portfolio exceeds +$1.96 million is 2.5%
  • Assume
  • Monthly change in value of portfolio has a mean of $0 and standard deviation of $1 million

Sample Question – Value at Risk (VaR)

  • A portfolio is worth $10 billion. The daily changes in the portfolio’s value are normally distributed with a mean of zero and standard deviation of $10 million
  • What is the one-day Value at Risk of the portfolio at the 1 percent level? Show your work/calculations.

Sample Question – Maximum Probable Loss (MPL)

  • Background
  • Normal Insurance Company Inc. estimates total liability losses for the coming year = $50 million.
  • Standard deviation forecast for the losses = $3 million.
  • To Find
  • What is the predicted maximum probable loss (MPL) at the 95% level?
  • At the 99% level?

Sample Question – Maximum Probable Loss (MPL)

  • Background
  • Normal Insurance Company Inc. estimates total liability losses for the coming year = $50 million.
  • Standard deviation forecast for the losses = $2 million.
  • To Find
  • What is the predicted maximum probable loss (MPL) at the 95% level?
  • At the 99% level?

Correlation and covariance

  • Applied Examples

Correlation

  • Correlation: Measures the strength and direction of the relationship between two random variables. So far we’ve only looked at distributions of single random variables.
  • RVs frequently correlate (+ or -)
  • If correlation = 0, then uncorrelated
  • If correlation = +1.0, then perfectly positively correlated (i.e., the two RVs move in the same direction and by the same proportion)
  • If correlation = -1.0, then perfectly negatively correlated (i.e., the two RVs move in the opposite direction and by the same proportion)

Correlation Coefficients ()

  • Correlation Coefficients are always between -1 and +1
  • |------------|------------|
  • -1 0 1
  • X,Y = 1  perfect positive correlation
  • X,Y = -1  perfect negative correlation
  • X,Y = 0  no correlation

Correlation: Examples

  • Gender and Auto Claim Severity
  • Males: +
  • Females: -
  • Age and Auto Claim Frequency
  • Youthful Drivers: +
  • Middle Age Drivers: -
  • Elderly Drivers: +
  • Experience of Worker and Frequency of Workplace Injury
  • Inexperienced: +
  • Experienced: -
  • Type of Construction and Home Fire Risk
  • Wood Frame: +
  • Masonry: -
  • Risk of Death and Age, Education
  • Age: +
  • Education: -
  • Vaccine Status and Health Insurance Claim
  • Vaccinated: -
  • Unvaccinated: +

Covariance

  • Economic Condition: Strong Growth · Probability: 1/3 · Revenue (X): $850 · Costs (Y): $700
  • Economic Condition: Weak Growth · Probability: 1/3 · Revenue (X): $800 · Costs (Y): $650
  • Economic Condition: No Growth · Probability: 1/3 · Revenue (X): $500 · Costs (Y): $600
  • Economic Condition: Expected Value · Revenue (X): $717 · Costs (Y): $650

Covariance: An Example (Appendix 4A, p.82)

  • Cov (Revenue, Cost) =
  • 1/3($850-$717)($700-$650)
  • +1/3($800-$717)($650-$650)
  • + 1/3($500-$717)($600-$650) =
  • 1/3($133)($50) + 1/3($83)($0) + 1/3(-$217)(-$50) =
  • 1/3($6,650) + 1/3(0) + 1/3($10,850) = 2,217.7 + $3,617.7 = +$5,834.4
  • Tells that the covariance is positiveVariables tend to move in the same direction. But covariance has little actual meaning on its own. Needs to be “scaled” relative the standard deviations of the random variables involved (e.g., revenue, costs)
  • Economic Condition: Strong Growth · Probability: 1/3 · Revenue (X): $850 · Costs (Y): $700
  • Economic Condition: Weak Growth · Probability: 1/3 · Revenue (X): $800 · Costs (Y): $650
  • Economic Condition: No Growth · Probability: 1/3 · Revenue (X): $500 · Costs (Y): $600
  • Economic Condition: Expected Value · Revenue (X): $717 · Costs (Y): $650

Correlation Coefficient: r

  • Scaling involves taking the ratio of the COV to the product of the standard deviations of each variable = r
  • r(X,Y) = Cov(X,Y)/Std(X) x Std(Y)
  • Or in our example
  • Cov(Revenue, Costs)/[Std(Revenue) x Std(Costs)]
  • Need Std. Dev of Revenue and Costs
  • Std(Rev) = {1/3[($850-$717)2 + ($800-$717)2 + ($500-$717)2]}1/2 = $154.6
  • Std(Costs) = {1/3[($700-$650)2 + ($650-650)2 + ($550-$650)2]}1/2 = $40.8
  • r(Rev, Costs) = $5,834/($154.6)($40.8) = $5,834/6,308 = +0.925
  • Correlation between Revenue and Costs is positive and strong (but not perfect)

Pooling arrangements and diversification of risk

  • Pooling of Independent Loss Exposures Can Reduce Risk

A Definition of Insurance

  • Insurance is the pooling of fortuitous losses by transfer to an insurer, who agrees to indemnify insureds for such losses and render services connected with the risk.
  • Insurance deals with risks that are pure, fortuitous, particular and diversifiable.

Main Contracting Costs In Running A Pool

  • Distribution Costs: To add participants to the pool
  • Underwriting Costs: To screen applicants and evaluate the expected loss of each potential pool participant.
  • Loss Adjustment Expense – to evaluate and settle claims fairly and to prevent inflated or fraudulent claims.

Premium Payments Versus Assessments

  • Today, policyholders pay an “ex ante” premium
  • Assessments are “ex post” payments
  • Generally, premium payments are made to the insurance company, without giving the insured the right to assess.
  • That is, the insurer obtains revenue prior to paying claims.
  • Advantage to the insurer: Positive cash flow and ability to invest the “float”.
  • Disadvantage: Cost of goods sold not known at the time of pricing. This makes pricing “risky”.

Key Points about Risk Pooling

  • Pooling arrangements
  • do not change expected loss
  • reduce uncertainty (variance decreases, losses become more predictable, maximum probable loss declines)
  • distribution of costs becomes more symmetric (less skewness)

Key Points about Risk Pooling (cont.)

  • Pooling reduces each participant’s risk
  • i.e., costs from loss exposure become more predictable
  • Predictability increases with the number of participants
  • Predictability decreases with correlation in losses

Two Assumptions Regarding Pooling

  • The Pool consists of a group of risks that are
  • Relatively homogenous
  • Losses to which the group is subjected are accidental, not intentional

Risk Pooling Example with 2 People

  • Two people (Emily and Samantha) with same distribution
  • Outcome Probability
  • $2,500 0.20
  • Loss = $0 0.80
  • Assume losses are uncorrelated
  • Expected Value = $500 = $2,500(0.2) + $0(0.8)
  • Standard Deviation =$1000 = [0.8($0-500)2 + 0.2($2500-500)2]1/2

Example: Two-Person Pooling Arrangement

  • Possible Outcomes: Neither Samantha nor Emily has an accident · Total Cost: $0 · Cost Paid by Each (Average Loss): $0 · Probability: (0.8)x (0.8)=0.64
  • Possible Outcomes: Samantha has an accident, but Emily does not · Total Cost: $2,500 · Cost Paid by Each (Average Loss): $1,250 · Probability: (0.2)x (0.8)=0.16
  • Possible Outcomes: Emily has an accident but Samantha does not · Total Cost: $2,500 · Cost Paid by Each (Average Loss): $1,250 · Probability: (0.2)x (0.8)=0.16
  • Possible Outcomes: Both Emily and Samantha have an accident · Total Cost: $5,000 · Cost Paid by Each (Average Loss): $2,500 · Probability: (0.2)x (0.2)=0.04

Risk Pooling Example with 2 People (Cont.)

  • Pooling Arrangement changes distribution of accident costs for each individual
  • Outcome Probability
  • $0 (.8)(.8) = .64
  • Cost = 1,250 (.2)(.8)(2) =.32 (i.e., 0.16+0.16)
  • 2,500 (.2)(.2) = .04
  • Expected Cost = $500 = 0.64($0) + 0.32($1,250) + .04($2,500)
  • Std. Dev. =$707 = [0.8($-500)2 + 0.2($2500-500)2]1/2

Risk Pooling Example with 2 People

  • Effect on Expected Loss
  • w/o pooling, expected loss = $500
  • with pooling, expected loss = $500
  • Effect on Standard Deviation
  • w/o pooling, standard. deviation = $1000
  • with pooling, standard. deviation = $707
  • What would happen if 3rd person added?
  • Can intuitively see that that while expected loss always remains the same at $500, the standard deviation will fall.
  • This can be see by observing that the likelihood of someone having to pay the full $2500 drops from 0.04 to 0.008 = (0.2)(0.2)(0.2)
  • i.e., likelihood of extreme outcomes is falling
  • The Expected Loss is the same under the pooling arrangement, but…
  • The risk for each individual is substantially reduced

Risk Pooling with 4 People

  • Pooling Arrangement between 4 people
  • Outcome Probability
  • $10,000 0.0016
  • $7,500 0.0256
  • Loss = $5,000 0.1536
  • $2,500 0.4096
  • $0 0.4096
  • Expected Loss = $500
  • Variance = $250,000 (calculated on next slide)
  • Standard Deviation = $500

Pooling of 4 – Excerpt From Excel

  • Variance = .0016($4,000,000) + .0256(1,890,000)+.1536(562,500)+.4096(15,625)+.4096(250,000) = 250,000

Comparing Risk Pooling with 4 and 20 Participants

  • $0 Loss for all
  • Prob = .4096
  • 1 has loss, 3 no loss $625 Avg. Loss; Prob = .4096
  • 2 have loss, 2 no loss $1250 Avg. Loss; Prob = .1536
  • All 4 have large loss $2500 Avg. Loss; Prob = .0016

Risk Pooling of Uncorrelated Losses

  • Main Points
  • Pooling arrangements
  • do not change expected loss
  • reduce uncertainty (variance decreases, losses become more predictable, maximum probable loss declines)
  • distribution of costs becomes more symmetric (less skewness)

Effects of Risk Pooling: EV Stay Same, but Variance (SD) Fall, Less Skewness*

  • *See Concept Check #2, P.73

Law of Large Numbers

  • The greater the number of exposures (e.g., people of businesses insured), the more closely will the actual result approach the expected result.
  • In other words, the mean of the sample we have approaches the true mean of the population as the number of exposures approaches infinity

The Law of Large Numbers

  • Insurance companies rely on the Law of Large Numbers to ensure accuracy when setting rates and premiums
  • They expect losses to occur.
  • Their major concern is a deviation between actual losses and expected losses.
  • By insuring large numbers of people/businesses, insurance companies reduce uncertainty (risk).
  • The risk to the whole becomes less than the sum of its parts.

Central Limit Theorem

  • The central limit theorem tells us that using the normal distribution can be appropriate if we are analyzing a sum of independent identically distributed random variables even if these random variables are not normally distributed.
  • Stated more specifically, in random samples of n observations, the distribution of the sample means will be a normal distribution.
  • And, the mean of the sample distribution will equal the mean of the population distribution!

Central Limit Theorem (cont.)

  • The standard error of the sample mean will be equal to the standard error of the population divided by the square root of n.
  • The standard error of the sampling distribution can be reduced simply by increasing the sample size.

Standard Error of the Sampling Distribution Versus Sample Size

Pooling Arrangements with Correlated Losses

Pooling arrangements with correlated losses

  • Pooling of Independent Loss Exposures Can Reduce Risk

Definition of Pooling

  • Pooling: Spreading of losses incurred by the few over the entire group so that the average loss is substituted for actual loss.
  • Concept was understood even by ancient civilizations
  • Achieves diversification (so long as risks are not perfectly correlated)
  • Pooling of losses is at the core of what insurers do

Risk Reduction From Pooling - Effect of Positive Correlation

  • Zero spread of risk—no diversification, so pooling provides no benefit
  • Partial (usually substantial) spread of risk—some diversification, so pooling provides some benefit
  • Total or near-total spread of risk—large benefits from diversification, so pooling benefits are maximized

Enterprise Risk Management

  • Today, the risk manager
  • Is involved with far more than simply purchasing insurance
  • Considers both pure risks (which are commonly insured) and speculative financial risks (which can be hedged)
  • Considers all risks across the organization and the strategic implications of the risks

Enterprise Risk Management (Continued)

  • Financial Risk Management: refers to the identification, analysis, and treatment of speculative financial risks
  • Commodity price risk
  • Interest rate risk
  • Currency exchange rate risk
  • Financial risks can be managed with capital market instruments

Exhibit 4.1: Hedging a Commodity Price Risk Using Futures Contracts

  • A corn grower estimates in May that he will harvest 20,000 bushels of corn by December.
  • The price on futures contracts for December corn is $4.90 per bushel.
  • Corn futures contracts are traded in 5000 bushel units
  • How can he hedge the risk that the price of corn will be lower at harvest time?
