  • This means that the remaining insurers in the market have to pick up the tab in the form of “assessments” for the obligations of the failed insurer if guaranty fund resources are insufficient. Sometimes insurers can pass the assessment through to consumers, sometimes not.

Risk-Based Capital (RBC) Standards

  • Risk-Based Capital Standards: Designed by National Association of Insurance Commissioners (NAIC) to reduce the risk of insolvency
  • RBC: Means that insurers must insurers must hold a certain amount of capital, depending on the riskiness of their investments and insurance operations
  • An insurer’s RBC depends on asset risk, underwriting risk, interest rate risk, and business risk
  • A comparison of the company’s total adjusted capital (TAC) to the amount of required risk-based capital (RBC Ratio) determines whether company or regulatory action is required
  • RBC Ratio = Total Adjusted Capital/RBC
  • Example: Suppose an insurer has TAC = $22 billion and RBC of $10 billion, then the RBC Ratio = 22/10 = 2.2 or 220% (has 2.2x min level of capital)

RBC (Continued)

  • Comparison of Risk Charges

RBC (Continued)

  • Comparison of Risk Charges for Different Asset Classes
  • Increasing Risk
  • Insurer has to hold 30% of the value of a low-rated asset (e.g., below investment grade bond) but 0% for a US Govt. bond

Example: RBC Calculation for Insurance

  • Investment Portfolio RBC Charge Required Capital
  • Bonds $3,000,000 10% $300,000
  • Stocks $1,000,000 15% $150,000
  • Premiums Written
  • Homeowners $1,000,000 27% $270,000
  • Personal Auto $2,000,000 7% $140,000
  • Reserves
  • Homeowners $2,000,000 21% $420,000
  • Personal Auto $3,000,000 11% $330,000
  • If ’s = Total Adjusted Capital is $4,000,000, what is its RBC Ratio?
  • TAC = $4 million and RBC of $1.61 million, then the RBC Ratio = 4/1.61 = 2.48 or 248% (has 2.48x min level of capital)

RBC (Continued)

  • Corrective Action Levels for Insurers
  • RBC Ratio: 125% and above · Zone: Adequate · Action: None
  • RBC Ratio: 100% - 124% · Zone: Red Flag · Action: Insurer must conduct trend test
  • RBC Ratio: 75% - 99% · Zone: Company Action · Action: Insurer must file plan with regulator outlining corrective steps
  • RBC Ratio: 50% - 74% · Zone: Regulatory Action · Action: Regulator must examine insurer and order corrective steps
  • RBC Ratio: 35% - 49% · Zone: Authorized Control · Action: Regulator may seize insurer, if necessary
  • RBC Ratio: Below 35% · Zone: Mandatory Control · Action: Regulator must seize insurer

What Areas Are Regulated? (Continued)

  • Policy Forms: State insurance commissioners have the authority to approve or disapprove new policy forms before the contracts are sold to the public
  • Insurance contracts are technical and complex
  • Purpose is to protect the public from misleading, deceptive, and unfair provisions
  • Some regulations getting into excruciating detail (Example: In FL homeowners insurance policies, regulation require that the policy’s flood exclusion by printed in an 18-point or larger font!)

Price regulation

  • Some Types of Insurance Are Heavily Price Regulated, Others Not at All

Price/Rate Regulation

  • Common Types of Rate Regulation
  • Effect: Restrict level or change in rates
  • Prior Approval: regulator must approve rates before they can be put into effect by the insurer
  • File and Use: Insurer “files” rates with regulators and implements immediately though regulator has authority to require adjustments
  • Use and File: Insurer implements rates and “files” rating plan with regulator afterward
  • Competitive (Open Competition/No Filing): Rates are market determined w/o regulator input
  • Restrictions on Underwriting Criteria
  • Can also have impact similar to regulating rates

Effects of Rate Suppression and Compression

  • Higher rates are unpopular with consumers
  • Rate Suppression – Regulators force insurers to keep rates below actual costs (e.g., not allowed to charge actuarially justified price for homes on the coast)
  • Rate Compression - Restrict differences in rates across classifications
  • Suppression and/or compression lead insurers to not voluntarily offer coverage to some groups (supply of insurance falls)
  • Which leads to creation of residual market mechanisms (e.g., joint underwriting associations) (JUAs)
  • Sometimes referred to as “markets of last resort”
  • While this creates some minimal level of availability, the coverage options are limited, prices are expensive, sometimes state-run

What Areas Are Regulated? (Continued)

  • Sales Practices are regulated by the laws concerning the licensing of agents and brokers
  • All states require agents and brokers to be licensed
  • All states require agents to obtain continuing education to upgrade their knowledge and skills

Some Sales Practices/Issues That Are Regulated? (Continued)

  • Insurance laws prohibit a variety of unfair sales practices, such as misrepresentation, twisting, and rebating
  • Twisting is the inducement of a policyowner to drop an existing policy and replace it with a new one that provides little or no economic benefit to the client
  • Rebating is the practice of giving an individual a premium reduction or some other financial advantage not stated in the policy as an inducement to purchase the policy (e.g., even giving someone a free fire extinguisher or smart home monitoring systems could violate these laws in some states)
  • Insurers seeking to get rebating losses changed (e.g., insurers want to provide policyholders with monitoring devices)
  • Suitability: Any insurance product sold must be appropriate for the buyer. For example, selling a 20-year term life insurance policy to a 90-year old would not meet the suitability standard.

What Areas Are Regulated? (Continued)

  • Claim Practices: State insurance departments typically have a complaint division for handling consumer complaints
  • Most complaints involve claims
  • Disagreement over amount of damage is the most common dispute
  • Timely payment of claim can be an issue
  • Claim could be complex
  • Suspicion of fraud
  • Information is provided to consumers on insurance department websites and in brochures
  • Taxes: Insurers pay numerous local, state, and federal taxes
  • Especially “premium taxes” which are often assed at 2% of direct premiums written

State versus Federal Regulation

  • Proponents for federal regulation argue that federal regulation
  • would provide uniformity in state regulations
  • is more effective in negotiations of international insurance agreements
  • is more effective in the identification and treatment of systemic risk
  • Would enable insurers to become more efficient

State versus Federal Regulation (Continued)

  • Advantages of state regulation include
  • Quicker response to local insurance problems
  • Federal regulation could lead to a dual system of regulation and increase costs
  • Poor quality of federal regulation, e.g., in the banking industry
  • Reasonable uniformity of laws can be achieved by the model laws of the NAIC
  • Greater opportunity for innovation
  • Unknown consequences of federal regulation

State versus Federal Regulation (Continued)

  • Shortcomings of state regulation include
  • Inadequate protection of consumers
  • Improvements needed in handling complaints
  • Inadequate market conduct examinations
  • Insurance availability

Current Problems and Issues in Insurance Regulation

  • Should the McCarran-Ferguson Act be repealed?
  • Critics of state regulation argue
  • The insurance industry no longer needs broad antitrust exemption
  • Federal regulation is needed because of the defects in state regulation.
  • Counterarguments include
  • The insurance industry is already competitive.
  • Small insurers may be harmed.
  • Insurers may be prevented from developing common coverage forms

Modernizing Insurance Regulation (Continued)

  • The Federal Insurance Office (FIO) has the authority to
  • Monitor all aspects of the insurance industry
  • Identify gaps in insurance regulation and identify issues that contribute to systemic risk
  • Assist the FSOC in identifying insurers that could create systemic risk
  • Represent the federal government in international discussions of insurance regulation
  • Negotiate international agreements with foreign countries that pertain to insurance regulation.

Modernizing Insurance Regulation (Continued)

  • Another approach to insurance regulation is an optional federal charter
  • Proposals would allow insurers to choose either a federal or state charter.
  • Proponents argue that national insurers are at a competitive disadvantage under the present system.
  • Opponents suggest this creates a dual system of insurance regulation which will increase the cost of insurance regulation

Areas Regulated: Market Conduct Regulation

  • Market Conduct refers to the marketing practices of insurers and agents that involve interaction with insureds, claimants, or consumers. Regulators have the authority to perform “Market Conduct Exams”
  • Practices include
  • Sales of insurance policies
  • Advertising of insurance products
  • Underwriting and rating
  • Collection of premiums
  • Policy renewals, termination, and changes
  • Claims settlement

Market Conduct Regulation (Continued)

  • Regulators are concerned that certain industry practices may have an adverse effect on policyholders, beneficiaries, claimants and insurance consumers
  • Concerns include
  • Sale of unsuitable insurance products
  • Misrepresentation of coverage
  • Excessive sales pressure
  • Rates that are excessive or unfairly discriminatory
  • Denial of legitimate claims
  • Improper termination of policies
  • Regulators have authority to conduct “Market Conduct Exam” which involves a detailed inspection of the insurer’s practices and can result in fines, penalties and other sanctions against the insurer

Market Conduct Regulation (Continued)

  • The FIO report makes several recommendations regarding market conduct, including
  • Every state should participate in the Interstate Insurance Product Regulation Commission (IIPRC) to improve the approval of products
  • Each state should adopt the Suitability in Annuities Transactions Model Regulation drafted by the NAIC
  • States should identify regulatory practices that best promote competitive markets for personal lines insurance consumers

Insight 8.1: Credit-Based Insurance

  • The credit based insurance score is derived from an applicant’s credit history and is combined with other underwriting factors
  • Proponents of credit-based insurance argue
  • There is a high correlation between an applicant’s credit record and future claims experience
  • Insurance scores permit insurers to accept more high-risk consumers for whom otherwise an appropriate premium could not be determined
  • Most consumers have good credit scores and benefit from credit scoring

Insight 8.1: Credit-Based Insurance (Continued)

  • Critics of credit-based insurance argue
  • The use of credit data in underwriting or rating discriminates against minorities and other groups
  • Credit-based insurance scores may penalize consumers unfairly during business recessions
  • Most states have enacted legislation that regulates the use of credit-based insurance scores

Insurance Pricing

Insurance industry pricing

  • 4 Major Components of Insurance Premiums

Insurance Pricing

  • For insurer to remain in operation it’s premiums must be sufficient to
  • Cover expected claim costs and administrative costs
  • Provide an expected profit to compensate for the cost of obtaining (and retaining) the capital necessary to support the sale of coverage
  • Actuarially “Fair Premium”: Premium that is just sufficient to cover an insurer’s expected costs, including a fair return to capital
  • It is the premium that would be charged in a perfectly competitive market

4 Determinants of Fair Premiums

  • 4 Determinants of the Actuarially Fair Premium
  • Expected Claim Costs
  • Administrative Costs
  • Investment Income
  • Fair Profit Loading

Expected Claim Costs & Risk Classification

  • Expected Claim Costs
  • An insurer must cover it’s expected claim costs
  • Have to recognize that assessment of risk in the real world is complex and risks need to be carefully analyzed so that they can be assigned to appropriate risk “classes”
  • “Homogeneous” (same) vs. “Heterogeneous” (different) buyers
  • Homogenous Buyers of Insurance: Assume that all buyers share the same loss distribution (but that losses are uncorrelated with one another)
  • Heterogenous Buyers of Insurance: Assume that all buyers can be can be categorized into different loss distributions (but that losses are still uncorrelated with one another)
  • OBJECTIVE: Develop a rating system that is fair

Expected Claim Costs

  • Pure Premium: The premium that just covers expected claim costs
  • Example
  • Large number of homogeneous buyers, i.e. each has the same loss distribution
  • Possible Loss Probability
  • $0 0.95
  • $10,000 0.05
  • Pure Premium = $500 = 0.95($0) + 0.05($10,000)

Premium Must Cover Expected Claim Costs

  • To cover claim costs, on average, premiums must equal $500.
  • if premium = $480, the insurer will lose money, on average (and will have to raise prices or will eventually go out of business)
  • if premium = $640, the insurer will make profits, on average
  • But competition will eventually compete away this profit
  • Conclusion
  • Actuarially Fair Premium must cover expected claim costs (cannot undercharge or overcharge in a perfectly competitive market)

Implications of Heterogeneous Buyers

  • What if there are two groups of buyers with different risk characteristics? (Assume a simple loss distribution where losses are either $0 or $10,000)
  • One Group (Sunday Drivers)
  • Possible Loss Probability
  • $0 0.95
  • $10,000 0.05
  • Another Group (Speedy Drivers)
  • $0 0.90
  • $10,000 0.10

Expected Loss Components

  • Group: Sunday Driver · Probability of Loss: 0.05 · Probability of NO Loss: 0.95 · Severity of Loss: $10,000 · Expected Value (Loss): $500
  • Group: Speedy Driver · Probability of Loss: 0.10 · Probability of NO Loss: 0.90 · Severity of Loss: $10,000 · Expected Value (Loss): $1,000

Our initial assumptions are that

  • Equal number of each type (Sunday and Speedy—i.e., low-risk and high-risk)
  • Losses are Independent
  • Full Insurance is mandatory (i.e., nobody can opt out or purchase less insurance)
  • Costless to distinguish Sunday and Speedy drivers

Heterogeneous Buyers

  • Initial Scenario
  • Equal Treatment Insurance Company is the only insurer in the market
  • Premium for everyone = $750
  • Does Equal Treatment cover its costs? YES
  • $750 is the EV of all drivers if we combine both groups ($1000+ $500)/2
  • BUT: the Speedy Drivers pay less than their expected cost, and the Sunday Drivers pay more than theirs

Should we treat these two distributions of drivers differently? Should we charge them different premiums?

Equal-Risk Treatment:Company Perspective

  • Charges $750 to all policyholders
  • Makes a $250 profit on each Sunday driver
  • Loses $250 on each Speedy driver
  • Overall, the company breaks even.

New Scenario: Allow Competition

  • If Super Selective Insurance enters the market and assumes Equal Treatment will continue to charge $750, suppose Selective sets price with the objective of earning $50 profit per policy as follows
  • Premium to Sunday Drivers = $550 (profit = $550-$500)
  • Premium to Speedy Drivers = $1050 (profit = $1050-$1000)
  • Note: Profit per policyholder = $50
  • (it’s charging both groups $50 above expected losses)

What Happens to Equal Treatment?

  • BUT: Equal Treatment would experience adverse selection
  • Adverse Selection: Adverse selection is the tendency of persons with higher-than-average chances of loss to seek insurance at standard (average) rates.
  • If not controlled by underwriting (screening of applicants), adverse selection results in higher-than-expected loss levels.
  • Equal would obtain an adverse selection of policyholders -- only the Speedy Drivers will purchase from Equal Treatment
  • True cost of insuring Speedy Drivers is $1,000. Since Equal Treatment is only charging $750 and Selective is charging $1050.
  • Thus, Equal Treatment will have to classify or lose money.

Key Points

  • Profit Maximization
  • + ==> Risk Classification
  • Competition
  • Lack of Classification
  • + ==> Adverse Selection

Implications of Heterogeneous Buyers

  • Key Points
  • Profit Maximization
  • + ==> Risk Classification
  • Risk Management Alternatives
  • to Insurance
  • Lack of Classification
  • + ==> Adverse Selection

Adverse Selection—Recall

  • Adverse selection is the tendency of persons with higher-than-average chances of loss to seek insurance at standard (average) rates.
  • If not controlled by underwriting, adverse selection results in higher-than-expected loss levels.

Adverse Selection: Examples

  • High-risk drivers tend to seek insurance at preferred or standard rates.
  • Persons with serious health problems tend to seek health or life insurance at standard rates.
  • Businesses with a long record of burglary or robbery tend to seek property insurance at standard rates.
  • NOTE: In all of these cases, insurers have mechanisms in place to determine the true riskiness of potential policyholders
  • Underwriting is the primary tool to control adverse selection

Implications of Heterogeneous Buyers

  • What if it is costly to identify Speedy Drivers?
  • Go back to initial situation, but suppose that it costs $50 for each Speedy Driver identified (e.g., costs $50 to run DMV records)
  • Assuming Equal Treatment continues to charge $750, what does Selective charge?
  • Sunday Drivers? ______
  • Speedy Drivers? ______
  • Redo the analysis assuming the cost per Speedy Drivers identified = $300 (i.e., Selective would have to charge Sunday drivers $850)
  • At some point the marginal cost of gathering and analyzing such information with exceed its benefit, and insurer won’t adopt
  • TODAY: Costs of identifying and analyzing distinguishing risk characteristics are falling due to better technology (ability to analyze and store more information, more inexpensively, than ever before; Emphasis placed on data analytics, “Big Data,” AI, etc.)
  • $600
  • $1100
  • = $500+$50+$50
  • = $1000+$50+$50

Implications of Heterogeneous Buyers

  • Key Point
  • Profit Maximization Risk Classification
  • + ==> if it is
  • Competition Cost Effective
  • It may be too expensive to identify some factors necessary for risk classification. “Big Data” and “data analytics” can help with this by making more data that is helpful for underwriting purposes available—in a timely and cost-efficient manner.

Is Classification Good for Society?

  • Public Policy Issue
  • From a societal perspective, is risk classification desirable?
  • Some argue that risk classification should be restricted when
  • insurance is mandatory (e.g., auto liability)
  • classification is based on inherited traits (e.g., gender, genes)
  • classification is based on location of residence (e.g., auto, property)
  • classification is based on subjective criteria (e.g., “poor moral risks”)
  • When link between risk factor and risk isn’t immediately or intuitively obvious (e.g., use of credit information, education, occupation)
  • What do you think? Should life and health insurers be able to use genetic information to determine premiums?

Is Classification Good for Society?

  • Restricting classification will alter insurance prices to certain groups and therefore change behavior
  • Types of behavior
  • amount of insurance purchased
  • amount of risky activity undertaken
  • loss control activities
  • Some changes in behavior may be desirable and some undesirable
  • Examples
  • Should the unvaccinated be charged more for health insurance?
  • amount of liability insurance purchased by poor people
  • smoking
  • amount of life insurance purchased by people with HIV
  • What about obesity—should overweight people be charged more for health insurance?
  • Should people who regularly go to health clubs or maintain a low BMI receive discounts?

Is Classification Good for Society?

  • Restricting classification may decrease classification costs
  • Insurers incur costs in classifying, which increases prices on average. We might be better off not incurring these costs and just charge everyone the same price
  • BUT…Many of the controversial issues (gender, age, location) have low classification costs

Is Classification Good for Society?

  • 4. Limits on classification may increase regulatory costs
  • Monitoring of insurers to enforce restrictions
  • Need to impose other costly restrictions on insurers
  • marketing activities
  • underwriting activities
  • Restrictions lead insurers to not offer coverage (at all or less coverage)
  • Leads to residual market (involuntary market) mechanisms
  • Leads to additional costs

Risk Classification Practices

  • Consumers are classified by various criteria
  • Class Rate: rate that applies to all consumers in a given classification
  • Underwriter decides whether a particular consumer will be offered coverage at the class rate
  • Actuaries determine price within each class based on risk characteristics
  • Schedule Rating: modification of the rate by the underwriter based on specific characteristics of the consumer (applies mostly to commercial insurance)
  • Underwriter has flexibility to adjust rate (+/-) to reflect unique characteristics that may affect losses
  • Loss control programs in a business, lack of fence around a pool at a home, presence of a burglar alarm system, condition of the insured property; Many factors will influence rate.

Risk Classification Practices (cont’d)

  • Consumers are classified by various criteria
  • Experience Rating: refers to the practice of basing rates on past experience
  • Retrospective Experience Rating (Retro Rating): Adjusting premium based on loss experience during policy period (most commonly used in medium/large businesses to incentivize loss control)
  • Particularly common in workers compensation insurance (incentive is to reduce workplace injuries and associated costs)

Recouping versus Updating Rates

  • Basing rates on past experience is sometimes viewed as controversial
  • Are insurers
  • Recouping past losses?
  • Updating expected losses on future business?
  • Competition and low switching costs limit the ability of insurers to recoup

Return to Determinants of Fair Premiums

  • Summary
  • Ignoring
  • administrative costs
  • investment income
  • profits
  • Fair Premium = Expected Claim Costs = Pure Premium

4 Determinants of Fair Premiums

  • 4 Determinants of the Actuarially Fair Premium
  • Expected Claim Costs
  • Administrative Costs
  • Investment Income
  • Fair Profit Loading

Investment Income

  • Key Point
  • Fair premium is reduced to reflect investment income on premiums
  • Equivalently
  • Fair Premium = Present Value of Expected Costs
  • (i.e., we discount the amounts to be paid out in the future by a rate that reflects what can be earned on premiums in the interim)

Example to Illustrate Effect of Investment Income

  • Assume
  • No administrative costs
  • One year policies, premium received at beginning of year
  • Certain claim costs = $100 paid at year-end
  • Undiscounted Claim Cost = $100
  • To calculate discounted claim cost (i.e., assumes claim paid at end of year), must take into account time value of money
  • If interest rate is
  • 5%: Fair Premium = $100/(1+r) = $100/(1+0.05) = $100/(1.05) = $95.24
  • 10%: Fair Premium = $100/(1+r) = $100/(1+0.1) = $100/(1.1) = $90.91
  • What if instead $50 is paid out at end of Yr. 1 and $50 at end of Yr. 2?
  • 5%: $50/(1.05) + $50/(1.05)2 = $47.62 + $45.35 = $92.97

Example to Illustrate Effect of Investment Income

  • Assume
  • no administrative costs
  • one year policies, premium received at beginning
  • certain claim costs = $100 paid according to table below (at end of each period)
  • Fair Premium
  • Note that the PVs are very sensitive to the timing of claim payments and to interest rates
  • The cumulative percentage of losses paid over time is known as the Loss Payout Pattern
  • 50/(1.1) + 25/(1.1)2 + 25/(1.1)3
  • = $45.45 + $20.66 + 18.78 = $84.90

Loss Payout Patterns for Select Lines of Business

  • Homeowners claims are paid out relatively quickly, meaning that investment income is less of an influence in so-called “short-tailed” lines
  • WC and Other Liability (e.g. Products Liability) claims are paid out relatively slowly, meaning that investment income has a larger influence in so-called “long-tailed” lines

Long vs. Short-Tail Lines

  • Short-Tailed Line: Line (type) of insurance where losses are paid out relative quickly
  • Investment income will have less of an influence
  • Examples: homeowners, auto comprehensive coverage (covers weather-related claims to vehicles, theft, vandalism)
  • Long-Tailed Line: Line (type) of insurance where losses are paid out relative slowly
  • Investment income will have more of an influence
  • Some claims may not be “closed” for decades
  • Examples: workers comp, medical malpractice, products liability, directors and officers, employment practices liability
  • Bottom Line: All else equal, lower interest raise the price of insurance (esp. for LT lines) (and vice versa)

Administrative Expenses

  • Fair Premium must cover administrative costs, such as
  • marketing
  • underwriting
  • loss adjustment
  • premium taxes
  • underwriting income taxes
  • etc.

Expense Loadings as a Percentage of Premium

4 Determinants of Fair Premiums

  • 4 Determinants of the Actuarially Fair Premium
  • Expected Claim Costs
  • Administrative Costs
  • Investment Income
  • Fair Profit Loading

Summary of Determinants of Fair Premiums

  • Ignoring profit loading
  • Fair Premium = PV of Expected Costs
  • Fair Premium = PV of Pure Premium + PV of Expenses
  • Note: Analysis to this point has been based on expected values
  • Now take into consideration the uncertainty associated with operations
  • Owners of capital need to be compensated for the risk they are taking

Effect of Uncertainty: Profit Loading

  • Rationale for Profit Loading
  • UncertaintyClaim costs could exceed premiums
  • And investment income could fall short of expectations
  • Therefore, insolvency is possible
  • Insurers hold capital not only to pay claims but to reduce the likelihood of insolvency
  • Thus, capital providers bear the risk associated with insurance operations
  • Capital providers require compensation for the capital they put at risk

Fair Profit Loadings

  • Other Terms for Profit Loading
  • Risk Loading
  • Capital Costs
  • Determinants of Fair Profit Loading
  • Amount of capital needed to support the coverage
  • Cost of capital (based on relative risk)
  • Takes into account how risky the business is relative to the “risk-free” rate of return (typically a US Treasury yield)
  • The more volatile the business, the higher the cost of capital (e.g., lines of business written on property in areas prone to hurricanes will have a higher profit loading than in areas that are less disaster prone)

Summary of Fair Premium Calculation

  • Fair Premium
  • = PV of Expected Claim Costs
  • + PV of Expected Loss Adjustment Expenses
  • + Underwriting Expenses
  • + Fair Profit Loading
  • Note fair premiums depend on both notions of risk highlighted in Chapter 1
  • Expected losses AND
  • Unpredictability of losses

Numerical Example

  • $100,000 with prob. 0.02
  • Loss Dist. = $20,000 with prob. 0.08
  • $ 0 with prob. 0.90
  • Find Fair Premium if
  • policy provides full coverage
  • Underwriting costs = 20% of pure premium
  • LAE = 10% of pure premium
  • fair profit = 5% of pure premium
  • Premium paid at beginning of the year
  • claims are paid at end of year
  • interest rate = 8%

Numerical Example

  • Solution
  • Pure Premium = $3,600 = (.02)($100,000) + (.08)$20,000 + (.9)($0)
  • PV of Expected Claims = $3600/1.08 = $3,333
  • LAE = (0.1)($3,600) = $360
  • PV of LAE = $360/1.08 = $333 (discount because LAE is part of claim)
  • Underwriting Costs = 0.2($3,600) = $720 (don’t discount because underwriting expenses are incurred immediately)
  • Fair Profit: 0.05($3,600) = $180
  • Fair Premium = (PV of Expected Claims+ PV of LAE) + UW Costs + Fair Profit
  • Fair Premium = ($3,333 + $333) + $720 + $180 = $4,566

Capital shocks and underwriting cycles

  • Both Can Impact Pricing

Capital Shocks and Underwriting Cycles

  • Cycles and price increases following capital shocks are difficult to explain with fair premium model
  • Fair premium implies that prices are forward looking
  • prices depend on expected future costs
  • prices depend on uncertainty about future costs
  • What happened in past is irrelevant

Premium Increases Following Capital Shocks

  • One explanation
  • Capital shock depletes insurer capital (Supply of insurance falls)
  • Costly to raise new capital
  • Capital becomes scarce ==> its required return increases
  • That is, the risk load increases

Underwriting Cycles

  • Possible Explanations
  • Insurers naively extrapolate from recent past
  • high (low) losses in the past cause insurers to predict that future losses will be high (low)
  • Problem: Sometimes the future is different from the past
  • Capital shocks, followed by excessive competition
  • capital shock reduces capital
  • premiums increase, which replenishes capital
  • excess capital develops
  • premiums decrease

Change in Commercial Rate Renewals, by Account Size: 1999:Q4 to 2016:Q4

  • Note: CIAB data cited here are based on a survey. Rate changes earned by individual insurers can and do vary, potentially substantially.
  • Percentage Change (%)
  • Peak = 2001:Q4 +28.5%
  • Pricing Turned Negative in Early 2004 and Remained that way for 7 ½ years
  • Pricing turned positive in Q3:2011, the first increase in nearly 8 years; Q1:2015 renewals were down 2.8%; Some insurers posted stronger numbers.
  • Trough = 2007:Q3 -13.6%
  • KRW : No Lasting Impact

Cumulative Qtrly. Commercial Rate Changes, by Account Size: 1999:Q4 to 2016:Q4

  • Note: CIAB data cited here are based on a survey. Rate changes earned by individual insurers can and do vary, potentially substantially.
  • 1999:Q4 = 100
  • Pricing for smaller accounts has been more stable than for larger accounts
  • Suggests that pricing is particularly weak for large accounts

The Role of Capital in Insurance

The role of capital in insurance enterprises

  • Insurance is a Capital Intensive Industry

Benefits of a Strong Capital Position

  • Capital is the life’s blood of insurance companies
  • The more capital a company has, the less likely it is to become insolvent, all else equal
  • Benefits of Increasing Capital
  • Consumer Preference for Strength
  • Consumers prefer to insure with insurers that are financially strong
  • Effect is particularly true for commercial (corporate) insurance customers
  • Risk managers and brokers investigate strength before purchase
  • Usually rely on rating from ratings agencies like A.M. Best
  • All else equal, a strong insurer can charge more for its products
  • Reduce Likelihood of Regulatory Action
  • If regulators have to take action to stave off a capital shortfall or insolvency, that could be the end of the insurer.

Insurer ownership, financial and operational structure

Types of Ownership and Structure of Private Insurance Companies

  • Stock Insurers
  • Mutual Insurers
  • Lloyd’s of London
  • Reciprocal Exchanges
  • Blue Cross/Blue Shield Plans
  • Health Maintenance Organizations (HMOs)
  • Other Types of Private Insurers

Types of Ownership and Structure of Private Insurance Companies

  • Stock Insurers: Incorporated insurance companies that are owned by investors who have purchased stock (equity) of the company.
  • Stockholders elect a board of directors who appoint executive officers to manage the company
  • If profitable, board can decide to pay dividends to shareholders
  • Examples: Travelers, Allstate, AIG, Chubb, Hartford Financial, MetLife, Aflac, Unum
  • Mutual Insurer: An insurer that is owned by its policyholders. There are no stockholders.
  • Policyholders vote to elect a board of directors who appoint executive officers to manage the company
  • If profitable, board can decide to pay dividends to policyholders or instead may decide to reduce prices (or limit increases); Dividends are not guaranteed (since performance is uncertain) and in practice relatively few pay dividends
  • Advance Premium Mutual: Policyholders pay premium in advance (Common)
  • Assessment Mutual: Have right to assess policyholders if financial results unfavorable (Rare)
  • Fraternal Insurer: Life/Heath insurer that provides coverage to a social or religious organization (Knights of Columbus, Thrivent Financial)
  • Mutual Examples: Liberty Mutual, State Farm, New York Life, Nationwide, Amica

Types of Ownership and Structure of Private Insurance Companies

  • Lloyd’s of London: Lloyd’s is not itself an insurer. Lloyd’s is actually composed of many “syndicates” which are specialist insurers that are members of Lloyd’s.
  • Lloyd’s is composed of approximately 95 syndicates run by a “managing agent”
  • Syndicates tend to specialize in marine, aviation, catastrophe and professional liability coverages but also write many unusual types of risks (equine, hole-in-one, terrorism)
  • Major player in global reinsurance markets
  • Syndicate Examples: Catlin, Amlin, Beazley, Brit, Hiscox
  • Reciprocal Exchange: An unincorporated organization in which insurance is exchanged among its members (sometimes called subscribers). In effect, insurance is “exchanged” among the members; each member of the reciprocal insures the other members and is likewise insured by them.
  • Most write only a few property/casualty lines of insurance and are small, but are some exceptions
  • Reciprocal Example: USAA

Types of Ownership and Structure of Private Insurance Companies

  • Blue Cross/Blue Shield: BC/BS plans were historically organized as nonprofit, community-oriented hospital pre-payment plans. Today, they are a mixture of non-profit and for-profit health care providers. The non-profits receive some favorable tax treatment. Some of the for-profit BC/BS companies are publically traded.
  • Health Maintenance Organizations (HMO): HMOs are organized plans of healthcare that provide comprehensive healthcare to members.
  • Heavy emphasis on cost controls; restrictions of providers, treatments, pharmaceuticals, etc.

Types of Ownership and Structure of Private Insurance Companies

  • Captives: An insurer owned by a company (whose business is not insurance) for the purposes of insuring its parent company’s loss exposures. Several types of captives, e.g.
  • Single-Parent (or Pure) Captive: owned by one parent (e.g., often a corporation)
  • Association Captive: Multiple parents

Agents and Brokers

  • Agents and Brokers: Agents and brokers are not insurance companies. Agents and brokers sell insurance through insurance agencies and brokers. They bear no insurance risk.
  • Agent: Legally represents the insurer.
  • More commonly works with individuals and small businesses
  • Broker: Legally represents the insured (i.e., the buyer of the insurance)
  • More commonly represents modest-to-large businesses

Costs of Increasing Capital

  • If having more capital is always better than less, why don’t insurers contain to raise/build capital until the risk of insolvency is infinitesimally small (near zero)?
  • Because capital is costly. Investors want a return a return on their capital
  • There is a cost of capital and therefore an opportunity cost from the perspective investors (i.e., they could invest their money elsewhere)
  • Cost of capital for mutual insurers lower than it is for stock companies
  • Benefits of Increasing Capital
  • Consumer Preference for Strength
  • Consumers prefer to insure with insurers that are financially strong
  • Effect is particularly true for commercial (corporate) insurance customers
  • Risk managers and brokers investigate strength before purchase
  • Usually rely on rating from ratings agencies like A.M. Best
  • All else equal, a strong insurer can charge more for its products
  • Reduce Likelihood of Regulatory Action
  • If regulators have to take action to stave off a capital shortfall or insolvency, that could be the end of the insurer.

The insurability of risk

  • Many Factors Impact the Insurability of Risk

Effect of Large Losses on Industry Supply

Six Requirements for Insurability of a Risk

  • Large number of exposure units
  • to predict average loss
  • Accidental and unintentional loss
  • to control moral hazard
  • to assure randomness
  • Determinable and measurable loss
  • to facilitate loss adjustment
  • No exceedingly catastrophic loss (i.e., ruinous)
  • to allow the pooling technique to work
  • Calculable chance of loss
  • to determine the premium need
  • Economically feasible premium
  • so people can afford to buy

War - An Insurable Risk?

  • Requirements of an Insurable Risk — Yes/No
  • Large number of exposure units to predict average loss
  • Accidental and unintentional loss to control moral hazard to assure randomness
  • Determinable and measurable loss to facilitate loss adjustment
  • No (ruinous) catastrophic loss to allow the pooling technique to work
  • Calculable chance of loss to determine the premium need
  • Economically feasible premium so people can afford to buy

Nuclear Contamination - An Insurable Risk?

  • Requirements of an Insurable Risk — Yes/No
  • Large number of exposure units to predict average loss
  • Accidental and unintentional loss to control moral hazard to assure randomness
  • Determinable and measurable loss to facilitate loss adjustment
  • No (ruinous) catastrophic loss to allow the pooling technique to work
  • Calculable chance of loss to determine the premium need
  • Economically feasible premium so people can afford to buy

Terrorism Violates Traditional Requirements for Insurability

  • Requirement: Estimable Frequency · Definition: Insurance requires large number of observations to develop predictive rate-making models (an actuarial concept known as credibility) · Violation: Very few data points Terror modeling still in infancy, untested. Inconsistent assessment of threat
  • Requirement: Estimable Severity · Definition: Maximum possible/ probable loss must be at least estimable in order to minimize “risk of ruin” (insurer cannot run an unreasonable risk of insolvency though assumption of the risk) · Violation: Potential loss is virtually unbounded. Losses can easily exceed insurer capital resources for paying claims. Extreme risk in workers compensation and statute forbids exclusions.

Requirement: Diversifiable Risk · Definition: Must be able to spread/distribute risk across large number of risks “Law of Large Numbers” helps makes losses manageable and less volatile · Violation: Losses likely highly concentrated geographically or by industry (e.g., WTC, power plants)

  • Requirement: Random Loss Distribution/Fortuity · Definition: Probability of loss occurring must be purely random and fortuitous Events are individually unpredictable in terms of time, location and magnitude · Violation: Terrorism attacks are planned, coordinated and deliberate acts of destruction Dynamic target shifting from “hardened targets” to “soft targets” Terrorist adjust tactics to circumvent new security measures Actions of US and foreign govts. may affect likelihood, nature and timing of attack
  • Information Institute
  • Terrorism Violates Traditional Requirements for Insurability (cont’d)

Losses by Type of Insurancefrom Sept. 11, 2001 Terrorist Attack

  • Total Insured Losses Estimate: $42.9B**
  • *Loss total does not include March 2010 New York City settlement of up to $657.5 million to compensate approximately 10,000 Ground Zero workers or any subsequent settlements.
  • **$32.5 billion in 2001 dollars.
  • (Billions of 2013 Dollars)

3 Factors Impacting Insurability of Risk

  • Premium Loadings (e.g., expenses, profits)
  • Moral Hazard
  • Adverse Selection

Factors Limiting the Insurability of Risk

  • Figure 10.1 from text (p. 138)

The Effect of Premium Loadings

  • Premium Loadings: Addition costs added to the pure premium to account for the insurers
  • Administrative costs
  • Capital costs (risk load)
  • Higher Loading lower demand for coverage
  • Why is demand for coverage lower after loadings are accounted for?
  • Because the loadings increase the price of coverage
  • Econ 101: If the price for something rises, all else equal the quantity demanded will fall
  • RESULT: Buyers will often NOT fully insure
  • i.e.: they purchase less than full insurance

The Effect of Premium Loadings

  • Recall: If an insurance contract’s premium equals the value of expected claim costs, risk-averse person (business) will likely demand full insurance coverage
  • But because premiums (almost) always have a positive loading (loading > $0), then demand will be less than full coverage
  • Bottom Line: Any factor (e.g., administrative costs, profit loadings/capital costs) that increases the price of coverage above the expected value of claims (pure premium) will limit the amount of private insurance coverage.
  • This is one reason people are chronically underinsured

The Effect of Premium Loadings

  • Premium loadings can be high enough as to make insurance for certain types of exposures extremely limited or even not available at all
  • Exposures with Low Severity (i.e., low cost claims)
  • Exposures with High Frequency
  • Correlated Exposures
  • Exposures with Uncertain Expected Losses (aka “Parameter Uncertainty”)

Exposures with Low Severity

  • Administrative Costs are “Fixed”
  • The administrative costs of insuring a $200 bicycle are the same as insuring a $2,000 bicycle.
  • Exposures with low value will not be insured on an individual basis
  • Basically not worth it—amount of coverage you receive for the price paid is not a good value
  • Might be done when low value items are grouped under a larger limit (e.g., under the “contents” limits in home insurance policies where by all “contents” in the home are bundled together thus spreading the fixed costs across many items which collectively have a greater value).
  • Beware of high prices for warranties on inexpensive products
  • According to Consumer Reports, the average car repair is $180 but an extended warranty costs ~$1,500 (and must be purchased when car is new; Doesn’t kick in until regular warranty expires—e.g., 5-yr, 50K miles)

Supply Side Impact of Premium Loadings

  • In addition to reducing the demand for coverage, higher loadings affect supply.
  • Insurance for some types of exposures (with a high loading) is likely to be extremely limited (or nonexistent)
  • The administrative costs of underwriting could be so large (e.g., small employers with a history of injuring many workers) or the required risk loading so high that insurers may try to limit exposure to certain markets

Exposures with High Frequency

  • When the probability of loss is high then this implies that expected claim costs (i.e., EVs) are high
  • And if there are many claims then administrative costs high (e.g., claims adjuster has to make frequent trips)
  • This is one reason why insurers will non-renew a policy that has many claims filed on it
  • Implication: Fair Premium is Close to Potential Loss
  • If administrative costs are high enough the premium can actually exceed the value of the potential loss (insurance would make no sense in the context)
  • Demand for insurance in these situations is low.
  • Premiums are high relative to the potential loss

Exposures with High Frequency

  • High FrequencyPremium will be close to the potential loss
  • Example: Hi-end laptop worth $10,000/popular with thieves
  • Claim = $10,000 with probability 0.7
  • $0 with probability 0.3
  • Expected Loss = $7,000 = 0.7($10000) + 0.3($0)
  • Loading = 25% of expected loss = .25($7,000) = $1,750
  • Premium = $7,000 + $1,750 = $8,750
  • Little demand for such insurance
  • Premium is 87.5% of potential claim

Exposures with Correlated Losses

  • (Highly) Correlated Losses
  • Insurer needs to hold a large amount of capital to make promise to pay claims credible
  • Therefore, profit loading is high
  • Thus, less insurance coverage for highly correlated losses
  • Situations that give rise to highly correlated losses are sometimes referred to as “clash losses.”

Exposures with Parameter Uncertainty

  • Parameter Uncertainty (aka Uncertain Expected Losses or Ambiguity): Insurer does not know or is unsure of the true expected loss
  • If estimate of expected loss is too low (or too high) for one policyholder, then estimate is too low (or too high) for many policyholders (insurer’s errors in predicted losses are correlated)
  • Parameter uncertainty causes the distribution of avg. losses around the insurer’s estimate of expected losses to have a great variance, same effect as with correlated losses
  • Insurer needs to hold a large amount of capital
  • Profit loading is high
  • Less insurance for exposures with significant parameter uncertainty

Exposures with Parameter Uncertainty

  • Example
  • Property valued at $50,000, large number of policyholders
  • Probability of loss = 0.02 or 0.04
  • Insurer does not which probability is the true one
  • Insurer views each probability as equally likely
  • Insurer’s expected claim costs = $1,500 = (1000+2000)/2
  • But insurer knows that claim costs could be much higher
  • Insurer needs a lot of capital
  • BOTTOM LINE: The costs of holding larger amounts of capital imply that when it is more difficult to estimate expected losses (i.e., there is a high level of parameter uncertainty), insurance coverage will be limited
  • New types of riskReflects parameter uncertainty.

Moral Hazard: An Important Limiting Factor in the Effectiveness of Insurance Markets

  • Controlling Moral Hazard Is Critical to the Viability of Insurance Markets

Moral Hazard

  • Moral Hazard: Refers to the effect of insurance on the insured’s incentives to reduce losses
  • Examples
  • Drive less carefully, don’t lock car door when insured
  • Less concerned about buying a home in a flood-prone area
  • Consume more health care when insured
  • Less diligent about workplace safety
  • Main Point
  • Insurers understand insurance gives rise to moral hazard
  • Insurance market responds to this in several ways
  • But central point is that as a result of Moral Hazard, insurance contracts will generally offer less than full coverage (deductibles, co-pays) causes less than full insurance coverage
  • Insured will therefore have to bear some risk

Conditions for Moral Hazard

  • Two Conditions Required for Moral Hazard
  • Expected losses depend on insured’s behavior
  • Effect of behavior on expected losses is costly to observe and measure (therefore difficult to do anything about)
  • Examples
  • Example 1: Claim costs increase with driving speed
  • Costly for insurers to monitor driving speed
  • Less so today (E. g., Usage based insurance rating – “Snapshot”)
  • Example 2: Health costs rise with poor diet
  • Costly for health insurer to monitor diet

Implications of Moral Hazard

  • As consumers, we actually want contracts that reduce moral hazard
  • Otherwise, we must pay higher premiums
  • So, contracts are written to place some risk on the insureds to help control (disincentivize) moral hazard
  • This is done through the inclusion of
  • Deductibles (limits coverage)
  • Coinsurance (limits coverage)
  • Experience rating (penalizes incaution)

Implications of Moral Hazard

  • Alternative: Make premium or coverage contingent on consumer’s behavior during the policy period
  • Progressive’s “Snapshot” device is one example of how to do this in auto insurance
  • John Hancock’s new interactive life insurance program
  • Allows you to earn premium discounts and other perks (e.g., gift cards) for those willing to use a fitness track or app that documents healthy lifestyle
  • There are now smartphone apps that do much the same
  • Retrospectively rated policies in workers compensation
  • IMPORTANT: Technology is helping to reduce moral hazard by rapidly reducing monitoring costs
  • World is become loaded with sensor devices all connected though the “Internet of Things”

Aviation

  • Rail & Public
  • Transport
  • Trucking & Fleet Vehicles
  • Marine Transport
  • Private Motor Vehicles
  • The Cloud
  • The Insurance Industry’s Future Is in the Cloud…
  • Human Beings

Wearables Show Significant Potential to Reduce Workplace Injury, Death

  • Wearables Today Can Monitor
  • Location
  • Heart rate
  • Temperature
  • Steps/Exertion
  • Sweat
  • Sleep
  • In the Near Future Could Monitor
  • Glucose level
  • Oxygen levels
  • Pain
  • Nausea

Beyond Wearables: Ingestibles and Implantables, VR Could Have Big Impacts Too

  • Ingestibles
  • Body chemistry
  • View malignancies
  • Detect diseases
  • Medication adherence
  • Implantables
  • Smart Fabrics
  • Virtual Reality
  • Computer simulated reality
  • Augmented Reality
  • Real world environment supplemented by computer generated inputs

Adverse Selection: Can Also Limit Insurability

  • Adverse selection occurs when
  • policyholders have different expected losses
  • insurers cannot classifysame price charged to all
  • At a given price
  • higher risk consumers will buy more coverage
  • lower risk consumers will buy less coverage
  • Thus adverse selectionlow risk people obtain less coverage (because they have to pay too much)
  • Insurers recognize that risks are heterogeneous and therefore use classification
  • But classification is costly and only do so to the extent it is cost-efficient; Those some adverse selection persists.

Contractual Provisions that Limit Coverage

  • Summary of Factors Limiting Demand
  • administrative costs
  • capital costs
  • profit loadings
  • moral hazard
  • adverse selection
  • Now: Contractual Provisions that Limit Coverage
  • deductibles
  • coinsurance
  • policy limits
  • exclusions

Deductibles

  • Deductibles: Share of loss for which the policyholder is responsible. Insurer pays nothing if claim is less than equal to the deductible.
  • Example
  • policy with a $500 deductible
  • then policyholder pays first $500 of losses
  • E.g.: I’m in an auto accident and the cost to repair my car is $3,000. I pay the first $500, insurer pays $2,500
  • Types of deductibles
  • per occurrence (i.e., deductible paid on each claim)
  • Aggregate (i.e., total of claims must reach a specific deductible amount before coverage kicks in)

Deductibles and Claim Processing Costs

  • Deductibles reduce cost of processing small claims
  • Example: Fixed claim processing cost of $200
  • $2,000 with probability 0.01
  • Loss = 100 with probability 0.10
  • 0 with probability 0.89
  • Expected claim cost + claim processing cost w/o a deductible = .01(2000) + .1(100) + .01(200) + .1(200) = $30 + $22 = $52
  • Expected claim cost + claim processing cost w/ $100 deductible = .01($1,900) + .01($200) = $19 + $2 = $21
  • NOTE: now there’s a 0% chance of a $100 loss for the insurer since that amount is equal to deductible and will be paid by policyholder
  • Marginal cost of insuring the $100 loss equals $31 = $52-$21 (very expensive)

Deductibles and Moral Hazard

  • Deductibles reduce moral hazard
  • Insureds take greater care if they have to pay part of the loss
  • The higher the deductible, the lower the premium
  • People should take the highest deductible they can afford
  • Many people mistakenly believe that insurers simply institute deductibles to avoid paying claims
  • Deductibles actually make insurance more affordable
  • Allow insurers to offer higher limits of coverage
  • Low value claims are more akin to maintenance issues and are often high frequency in nature

Deductibles and Adverse Selection

  • Deductibles might be used to reduce adverse selection
  • Recall, adverse selection occurs when the insurer cannot classify, but the policyholders know their risk
  • At a given price
  • high risk people will buy more coverage
  • low risk will buy less coverage

Deductibles and Adverse Selection

  • Insurer offers multiple policies with different deductibles and different prices per dollar of coverage
  • Lower deductible (higher coverage) policies have a higher price per dollar of coverage
  • Higher risk people might choose the lower deductible (higher priced) policies
  • Lower risk people might choose the higher deductible (lower priced) policies
  • Thus policyholders separate themselves into different policy groups

Coinsurance

  • With coinsurance, insured pays a proportion (the coinsurance rate) of any loss
  • Example: Insured pays 20% of all medical costs
  • Coinsurance likely causes fewer services to be consumed (fewer claims) because the marginal cost of consumption >0

Policy Limits

  • A policy limit is the maximum amount that the insurer will pay
  • In property insurance, policy limits prevent people from paying for coverage in excess of losses they could sustain (i.e., don’t want to be “overinsured”)
  • Limits also place an upward bound on what an insurer will need to pay. Therefore insurers can estimate maximum possible losses

Purposes of Policy Limits

  • In addition to placing an upward bound on what an insurance allowing insurers to estimate maximum possible losses…
  • Reduce classification costs when consumers have information that is costly for insurers to obtain
  • Example: Homeowners policy limits coverage for jewelry losses to $2,500
  • Those with more expensive jewelry buy special coverage
  • Insurer does not have to investigate the value of each policyholder’s jewelry

Exclusions

  • Policies exclude coverage for some types of losses
  • Why?
  • Reduce administrative costs
  • e.g., high frequency/low severity claims
  • Reduce capital costs
  • Such as for highly correlated events: war, nuclear cont.
  • Reduce moral hazard
  • e.g., suicide excluded for first 2 years of life ins. policies
  • Reduce adverse selection
  • Non-fortuitous losses
  • e.g., normal wear and tear (maintenance issues)
  • Tailor coverage to buyers’ needs
  • Not all coverages in policy needed, so why pay for them
  • Any many exclusions can be restored for a price

Class 16Risk Management And Insurance

  • MATERIAL ENDS HERE
Legal Principles

Fundamental Legal Principles of Insurance

Class 17Risk Management And Insurance

  • MATERIAL BEGINS HERE

Legal doctrines of insurance

  • Many Important Legal Principles Govern Insurance Markets

Why Have Legal Doctrines?

  • Legal Doctrines of Insurance: Fundamental rules that apply to all insurance contracts (i.e., policies)
  • Why not allow free contracting?
  • Legal doctrines reduce contracting costs
  • Difficult to anticipate every possible contingency and to write very detailed contracts
  • Therefore it is useful to have principles that will be applied in a consistent manner to unforeseen circumstances
  • Applicable especially to the problems of
  • moral hazard
  • information disclosure
  • Legal system also helps resolve disputes and enforce contracts

Resolving Coverage Disputes

  • Insurance Contracts Are Contracts of Adhesion
  • Ambiguities are interpreted in favor of policyholder, who is generally viewed to be the less sophisticated party
  • Applied less often to commercial insurance (both parties viewed as sophisticated)
  • Doctrine of Reasonable Expectations
  • Courts interpret contracts as would a reasonable person not trained in the law
  • Bad Faith Suits
  • Law suits alleging that insurer has acted in manner inconsistent with what reasonable policyholder would have expected and therefore failed to act in good faith.
  • If successful, such suits sometimes lead to awards far in excess of actual damages

Legal Doctrines and Moral Hazard

  • Legal doctrines that mitigate moral hazard
  • Indemnity Principle
  • Insurable Interest
  • Subrogation

Principle of Indemnity

  • The insurer agrees to pay no more than the actual amount of the loss (i.e., insured should not profit from the loss)
  • Purpose
  • To prevent the insured from profiting from a loss
  • To reduce moral hazard
  • Example: If the insured has their home insured for $200,000 and sustains a partial loss of $50,000 when the home is damaged in a fire, then they should receive only $50,000 to compensate for actual damages even if policy limits are $200,000.
  • If the policyholder could collect more, they would be profiting from insurance
  • Moral hazard would potentially come into play if the policyholder could collect $200K from her $50K loss, incentivizing dishonest behavior

How Important Is the Principle of Indemnity?

  • So important that one of Hollywood’s greatest film classics (1944) was dedicated to the topic!
  • Love, fraud and MURDER!!!
  • Famous line from Fred MacMurray’s
  • character, Walter Neff, the insurance
  • Salesman…
  • Double Indemnity: is a clause or provision in a life insurance or accident policy whereby the company agrees to pay the stated multiple (e.g., double, triple) of the face amount in the contract in cases of death caused by accidental means. This includes murder by a person other than, and not in collusion with, the beneficiary of the insurance policy, and most accidental deaths. It excludes suicide, and deaths caused by the insured person's own gross negligence, as well as natural causes.

Actual Cash Value (ACV): Property Insurance

  • In property insurance, indemnification is often based on the actual cash value (ACV) of the property at the time of loss
  • 3 Main Methods to Determine ACV
  • Replacement cost less depreciation
  • Fair market value: The price a willing buyer would pay a willing seller in a free market
  • Broad evidence rule: Means that the determination of ACV should include all relevant factors an expert would use to determine the value of the property

Actual Cash Value Sample Question

  • ACV = (Replacement Cost) – (Depreciation)
  • Example: Jerry purchased a dining room set for $5000 and insured the furniture on an actual cash value basis.
  • Three years later the entire set was destroyed by fire.
  • At the time of loss, the property had depreciated in value by 50%
  • The replacement cost of a new dining room set at the time of loss was $6000.
  • Ignoring any deductible, how much will Jerry collect from the insurer? Show your calculations.
  • ANSWER: ACV = $6000 – 0.5(6000) = $3000
  • If Jerry were paid the full replacement cost of $6000 then the principle of Indemnity would be violated because Jerry would receive the value of a new furniture even if the original was 3 years old

Principle of Indemnity (Continued)

  • There are some exceptions to the principle of indemnity
  • A valued policy pays the face amount of insurance if a total loss occurs (i.e., pays the stated value of the policy)
  • Some states have a valued policy law that requires payment of the face amount of insurance to the insured if a total loss to real property occurs from a peril specified in the law
  • Replacement cost insurance means there is no deduction for depreciation in determining the amount paid for a loss
  • A life insurance contract is a valued policy that pays a stated sum to the beneficiary upon the insured’s death

Exceptions to Principle of Indemnity

  • A valued policy pays the face amount of insurance if a total loss occurs (not too common today except for life insurance, and rare/valuable items (e.g., Picasso))
  • Some states have a valued policy law that requires payment of the face amount of insurance to the insured if a total loss to real property occurs from a peril specified in the law
  • Replacement cost insurance means there is no deduction for depreciation in determining the amount paid for a loss (becoming increasingly common)
  • A life insurance contract is a valued policy that pays a stated sum to the beneficiary upon the insured’s death

Exceptions to the Principle of Indemnity

  • Valued policies include
  • Antiques
  • Fine arts
  • Rare paintings (e.g., the Mona Lisa doesn’t depreciate)
  • Family heirlooms
  • Valued policy laws
  • Original purpose to protect insured from over-insurance
  • Less important now since over-insurance is rare

Collectibles: Insured Under Valued Policies

  • Valued policies include

Exceptions to the Principle of Indemnity (cont.)

  • Life Insurance
  • Not a contract of indemnity
  • Valued policy that pays a stated sum at the Insured’s death, no matter how much your life is “worth” at time of death
  • Usually concerned less about moral hazard in life insurance (i.e., presence of life insurance doesn’t usually contribute to behaviors that increase likelihood of death
  • Exception: Suicide—2-year suicide exclusion in life policies

Exceptions to the Principle of Indemnity (cont.)

  • Replacement Cost Insurance
  • No deduction taken for depreciation in determining the amount paid for a loss
  • Recognizes that few people budget for depreciation.
  • Under ACV, Insured could be required to come up with a substantial cash sum to restore the property to its original condition.
  • Example: Assume roof on home is 5 years old and has a useful life of 20 years. Roof is damaged by a tornado. If replacement cost of roof is $10,000, under ACV rule receive only $7,500 = ($10,000 - $2,500). But with a replacement cost policy, receive the full $10,000 (less any applicable deductible).

Principle of Insurable Interest

  • The insured must stand to lose financially if a loss occurs
  • Purpose
  • To prevent gambling (in the context of insurance)
  • To reduce moral hazard
  • To measure the amount of loss
  • Recovery shouldn’t exceed insurance interest
  • Example 1: Who can take a life insurance policy out on ?
  • Example 2: Who can take out a property insurance policy on your parents’ home?

Importance of Insurable Interest

  • To be legally enforceable, all insurance contracts must be supported by insurable interest.
  • A policy can be voided if insurable interest is found to be non-existent.

When Must An Insurable Interest Exist?

  • Property Insurance: at the time of the loss
  • Life Insurance: only at inception of the policy

Examples of Insurable Interest (P/L)

  • Ownership of Property
  • Homeowner, vehicle owner, business owner
  • Potential Legal Liability
  • Dry cleaner has insurable interest in customers’ property
  • Auto repair shop has insurable interest in my car while on their property
  • Secured Creditors
  • Bank granting a mortgage has an insurable interest in your home
  • Contractual Right
  • Example: Business contracting to purchase goods from abroad
  • Could suffer loss if goods to arrive (or arrive on time)

Examples of Insurable Interest (Life Ins.)

  • Insurable Interest: Not an issue when you buy life insurance on your own life
  • Law automatically presumes insurance interest exists when a person voluntarily purchases life insurance on their own life
  • Can designate anyone, any entity I want as my beneficiary.
  • No requirement that beneficiary demonstrate that an insurable interest exists (e.g., you could designate as your beneficiary)
  • Life Insurance Purchased on Another Person’s Life
  • Requires demonstration of insurance interest
  • Example 1: Familial Interest: Close family ties (husband on wife)
  • Example 2: Pecuniary (Financial): Corporation may insure life of CEO, top salesperson or business partner because of the negative impact this person’s death could have on the company (known as “key man” insurance)

Choice of Contract Type Explained By Two Factors

  • The costs of assessing value
  • Moral hazard

Principle of Subrogation

  • Substitution of the insurer in place of the insured for the purpose of claiming indemnity from a third person for a loss covered by insurance. [Insurer, after paying a claim, has the right to recover against the third party responsible for that loss (i.e., “go after” the negligent party]
  • Purpose
  • To prevent the insured from collecting twice for the same loss
  • To hold the negligent party responsible for the loss
  • To hold down insurance rates

Principle of Subrogation (cont.)

  • The insurer is entitled only to the amount it has paid under the policy
  • The insured cannot impair the insurer’s subrogation rights
  • Subrogation does not apply to life insurance and to most individual health insurance contracts
  • The insurer cannot subrogate against its own insureds

Subrogation Example

  • Assume a negligent motorist
  • Fails to stop at a red light
  • Smashes into the Insured’s car
  • Causing damages to the Insured’s car of $5,000.
  • Insured has purchased collision insurance for her car.
  • The Insured’s Insurance Company will
  • Pay the physical damage loss (less any deductible)
  • Then attempt to collect from (“go after”) the negligent motorist

Subrogation Example (cont.)

  • Alternatively, the Insured could attempt to collect directly from the negligent motorist.
  • Subrogation does not apply, if a loss payment is not made by the Insured’s insurance company.
  • To the extent that a loss payment is made, the Insurer receives the legal right to collect damages from the negligent third party.

Subrogation’s Effects

  • Prevents the Insured from collecting twice for the same loss (only the insurer can collect if the Insured has been indemnified by their insurer)
  • Holds the guilty party responsible
  • Holds down insurance rates
  • Generally, subrogation recoveries are factored into the rate-making process

Subrogation Caveats

  • The Insured cannot do anything after the loss that impairs the Insurer’s subrogation rights.
  • Subrogation does not apply to life insurance and most health insurance contacts.
  • Effect: If Joe is killed by a drunk driver and he had a $100K life insurance policy in force, his life insurance policy would pay the full $100K. However, his wife would still be free to sue the negligent driver for damages because of his unlawful death.
  • Insurer cannot recover against its own Insureds.
  • Would defeat the purpose of insurance

Case Application—Insurable Interest

  • Background
  • Jeff is a book dealer who purchased a building from Richard. Jeff obtained a loan from the Gateway Bank, which held a mortgage on the building.
  • Jeff planned to store his inventory of books in the building. He also planned to use part of the building for a fast food restaurant.
  • When Jeff applied for property insurance on the building, he did not tell the agent about the fast food restaurant because premiums would be substantially higher.
  • Eight months after the policy was issued, a fire occurred in the restaurant that caused substantial damage to the entire building

Case Application – Insurable Interest (cont.)

  • Do any of the following parties have an insurable interest in the building at the time of loss?
  • Jeff
  • Richard
  • Gateway Bank
  • Richard told Jeff he could save money by taking over Richard’s policy instead of purchasing a new policy. Can Richard validly “assign” his existing property insurance policy to Jeff without notifying the insurer?

Case Application

  • Could Jeff’s insurer deny coverage for the fire loss based on material concealment?
  • Investigation of the fire revealed that an electrician wired an electrical outlet in the restaurant, which caused the fire. Explain how subrogation might apply in this case.

Legal Doctrines & Information Disclosure

Legal Doctrines & Information Disclosure

  • Legal doctrines about inducing truthful information disclosure
  • Utmost good faith
  • Misrepresentations
  • Concealment

Principle of Utmost Good Faith

  • A higher degree of honesty is imposed on both parties to an insurance contract than is imposed on parties to other contracts
  • Insurance contracts are contracts uberrimae fidei, or of the utmost good faith.
  • Neither party is to take advantage of the other’s lack of information.
  • Note: This is important because one party often has more information than the other. Many provisions in insurance seek to remedy these informational asymmetries.

Historical Roots: Utmost Good Faith

  • Legal foundation rests on principles established long ago for insurance on ocean vessels and cargoes
  • Marine underwriter had to place great faith in the statements made by the applicant for insurance
  • The cargo may not have been able to be visually inspected
  • Captain/crew unknown to insurer
  • The contract may have been issued in a location far removed from the cargo or ship
  • Thus, a high degree of honesty was imposed on the applicant for insurance

Principle of Utmost Good Faith (cont.)

  • Supported by three legal doctrines
  • Representations are statements made by the applicant for insurance
  • A contract is voidable if the representation is material, false, and relied upon by the insurer
  • An innocent misrepresentation of a material fact, if relied on by the insurer, makes the contract voidable

Principle of Utmost Good Faith Supporting Legal Doctrines

  • A concealment is intentional failure of the applicant for insurance to reveal a material fact to the insurer
  • Concealment is the same thing as a nondisclosure
  • A warranty is a statement that becomes part of the insurance contract and is guaranteed by the maker to be true in all respects
  • Statements made by applicants are considered representations, not warranties
  • A statement achieves the status of a warranty if it becomes a condition of the insurer’s promise (e.g., if the applicant states none of the cargo vessels they want to insure contains toxic substances and the insurance contract then states coverage applies only if losses do not involve toxic substancewarranty)

Breach of Warranty

  • If a statement is construed as a warranty, the court may allow the insurer to deny coverage, if there has not been literal compliance with the statement
  • Effect can be very harsh.
  • To deny coverage, all the insurer may need to show was that the warranty was breached, even if the breach were unintentional and not related to the loss.

Elements of Misrepresentation

  • Material means that
  • If the Insurer knew the true facts, the policy would not have been issued or would have been issued on different terms.
  • False means that
  • The statement is not true or is misleading.
  • Reliance means that
  • The Insurer relies on the misrepresentation in issuing the policy at a specified premium

Concealment: Legal Significance

  • Concealment is the same as non-disclosure.
  • The applicant for insurance deliberately withholds material information from the insurer
  • Legal significance is the same as misrepresentation
  • The contract is voidable at the Insurer’s option.

Concealment Generally Has Two Elements

  • The concealed fact was known by the Insured to be material
  • The Insured intended to defraud the Insurer

Requirements of an Insurance Contract

  • To be legally enforceable, an insurance contract must meet 4 requirements
  • Offer and acceptance of the terms of the contract
  • Exchange of Consideration – the value that each party gives to the other
  • Competent parties, with legal capacity to enter into a binding contract
  • The contract must exist for a legal purpose

Distinct Legal Characteristics of Insurance Contracts

  • An insurance contract is
  • Aleatory: values exchanged are not equal
  • Unilateral: only the insurer makes a legally enforceable promise (the policyholder can walk away at any time)
  • Conditional: policyowner must comply with all policy provisions to collect for a covered loss (e.g., provide documentation of loss)
  • Personal: property insurance policy cannot be validly assigned to another party without the insurer's consent
  • A contract of adhesion: the insured must accept the entire contract with all of its terms and conditions

Distinct Legal Characteristics of Insurance Contracts (Continued)

  • Courts have ruled that any ambiguities or uncertainties in the contract are construed against the insurer (policy of adhesion).
  • The principle of reasonable expectations states that an insured is entitled to coverage under a policy that he or she reasonably expects it to provide, regardless of policy provisions.

Law and the Insurance Agent

  • An agent is someone who has the authority to act on behalf of a principal (the insurer)
  • Several laws govern the actions of agents and their relationship to insureds
  • There is no presumption of an agency relationship
  • An agent must be authorized to represent the principal
  • A principal is responsible for the acts of agents acting within the scope of their authority
  • Limitations can be placed on the powers of agents

Law and the Insurance Agent (Continued)

  • An agent’s authority comes from three sources
  • Express authority
  • Implied authority
  • Apparent authority
  • Knowledge of the agent is presumed to be knowledge of the principal with respect to matters within the scope of the agency relationship
  • Insurers can place limitations on the power of agents by adding a nonwaiver clause to the application or policy

Law and the Insurance Agent (Continued)

  • The doctrines of waiver and estoppel may require an insurer to pay a claim that it ordinarily would not have to pay
  • Waiver is defined as the voluntary relinquishment of a known legal right
  • Estoppel is the loss of a legal defense because of previous actions that are now inconsistent with that defense
  • Example: If an applicant for health insurance discloses a health problem and the agent assures the applicant that the condition need not be disclosed—then the insurer could be “estopped” from denying benefits based on the assertion that the information was not included on the application.

Basic Parts of an Insurance Contract

  • Declarations are statements that provide information about the particular property or activity to be insured
  • Usually on the first page of the policy
  • In property insurance, it contains name of the insured, location of property, period of protection, amount of insurance, premium and deductible information
  • Insurance contracts typically contain a page or section of definitions
  • For example, the insured is referred to as “you,” insurer as “we”…

Basic Parts of an Insurance Contract: Sample Declarations Page—Auto Policy

Basic Parts of an Insurance Contract: Sample Declarations Page—Auto Policy II

Basic Parts of an Insurance Contract (Continued)

  • Insuring Agreement summarizes the major promises of the insurer
  • The 2 basic forms of an insuring agreement in property insurance are
  • Named Perils Coverage: where only those perils specifically named in the policy are covered. If the peril is not named, it’s not covered
  • Open-Perils, All-Perils, All Risks or Special Coverage: where all losses are covered except those losses specifically excluded

Basic Parts of an Insurance Contract (Continued): II

  • Insurance contracts contain 3 major types of exclusions
  • Excluded Perils: e.g., in home insurance: flood, war, nuclear contamination, intentional acts (e.g., arson) [Need to buy specific coverage separately (e.g., flood insurance)]
  • Excluded Losses: e.g., a professional liability loss is excluded in the homeowners policy (i.e., if you operate a business out of your home, your HO policy will likely exclude any losses that arise from the operation of that business; If you’re an Uber driver, that activity is excluded under your personal auto policy)
  • Excluded Property: e.g., pets are not covered as personal property in the homeowners policy, neither are trees and shrubs, automobiles or aircraft…

Basic Parts of an Insurance Contract: III

  • Conditions are provisions in the policy that qualify or place limitations on the insurer’s promise to perform
  • If policy conditions are not met, the insurer can refuse to pay the claim (e.g., for a life insurance claim, need to furnish proof of death; for business interruption claim, need to demonstrate that there was actual physical loss or damage from a covered peril that gave rise to the interruption; for auto liability need to submit to physical exam if claiming injury)
  • Insurance policies contain a variety of miscellaneous provisions
  • e.g., cancellation, subrogation, grace period, misstatement of age

Definition of “Insured”

  • An insurance contract must identify the persons or parties who are insured under the policy
  • The named insured is the person or persons named in the declarations section of the policy
  • The first named insured has certain additional rights and responsibilities that do not apply to other named insureds (e.g., responsible for payment of premium but right to receive premium refund)
  • Other Insureds: A policy may cover other parties even though they are not specifically named (e.g., in an HO policy, resident relatives under the age of 21 under the care of the insured; resident relatives of the insured under age 24 who are FT students away from home)
  • Additional insureds may be added using an endorsement (e.g., if I lease my land to a tenant farmer, I might want to be listed on tenant’s farm liability policy as an additional insured

Endorsements and Riders

  • If coverage is excluded in a policy, it can sometimes be restored through and endorsement or rider
  • Endorsement: In property and liability insurance, an endorsement is a written provision that adds to, deletes from, or modifies the provisions in the original contract
  • e.g., an earthquake endorsement to a homeowners policy
  • A livery driver endorsement is necessary to use personal vehicle to be insured for use of your personal auto as an Uber driver
  • Rider: In life and health insurance, a rider is a provision that amends or changes the original policy
  • e.g., a waiver-of-premium rider on a life insurance policy

Deductibles

  • A deductible is a provision by which a specified amount is subtracted from the total loss payment that otherwise would be payable
  • The purpose of a deductible is to
  • Eliminate small claims that are expensive to handle and process
  • Reduce premiums paid by the insured
  • Reduce moral hazard (puts policyholder’s “skin in the game”)

Deductibles (Continued)

  • Straight Deductible: the insured must pay a certain number of dollars of loss before the insurer is required to make a payment (usually takes the form of a fixed dollar amount, such as $1,000, but could also be a share of the insured value of the property, such as 2%)
  • e.g., an auto insurance deductible usually has a fixed dollar deductible of $500 to $1,000
  • Aggregate Deductible: means that all losses that occur during a specified time period, usually a year, are accumulated to satisfy the deductible amount

Deductibles in Health Insurance

  • A calendar-year deductible is a type of aggregate deductible that is found in basic medical expense and major medical insurance contracts
  • An elimination (waiting) period is a stated period of time at the beginning of a loss during which no insurance benefits are paid
