  • e.g., disability income contracts that replace part of a disabled worker’s earnings typically have elimination periods of 30, 60, or 90 days, or longer periods.

Coinsurance

  • A coinsurance clause in a property insurance contract encourages the insured to insure the property to a stated percentage of its insurable value
  • If the coinsurance requirement is not met at the time of the loss, the insured must share in the loss as a coinsurer

Coinsurance (Continued)

  • The fundamental purpose of coinsurance is to achieve equity in rating
  • A property owner wishing to insure for a total loss would pay an inequitable premium if other property owners only insure for partial losses
  • If the coinsurance requirement is met, the insured receives a rate discount, and the policyowner who is underinsured is penalized through application of the coinsurance formula

Coinsurance in PROPERTY INSURANCE

  • A coinsurance clause in a property insurance contract encourages the insured to insure the property to a stated percentage of its insurable value (e.g. at least 80%)
  • Insurance-to-Value (ITV) is the goal
  • If the coinsurance requirement is not met at the time of the loss, the insured must share in the loss as a coinsurer
  • In other words, there is a penalty for not insuring to the appropriate property value. The penalty is assessed per this formula: (DID/SHOULD) x Loss = Amt. of Recovery, or stated differently

Coinsurance in Property Insurance II

  • The purpose of coinsurance is to achieve equity in rating
  • A property owner wishing to insure for a total loss would pay an inequitable premium if other property owners only insure for partial losses
  • If the coinsurance requirement is met, the insured receives a rate discount, and the policyowner who is underinsured is penalized through application of the coinsurance formula
  • Also helps reduce likelihood of underinsurance (i.e., policyholder receives payout from insurer insufficient to fully recover).
  • Underinsurance has sometimes led to litigation, with policyholders accusing insurers of not selling a policy with adequate limits

Property Coinsurance: Example

  • Commercial Building
  • ACV = $500,000
  • 80% coinsurance clause
  • Required Amount of Insurance = $400,000 = 0.8($500,000)
  • Insurance Amount Actually Purchased = $300,000
  • Loss = $10,000
  • 300,000 x $10,000 = $7,500 = Amount of Recovery
  • 400,000
  • If this were a “replacement cost” policy, you would simply use the replacement cost figure instead of ACV

Insurance-to-Value and Coinsurance

  • C = amount of coverage purchased (policy limit) = “Did”
  • L = the amount of the loss = “Loss”
  • V = value of property at the time of the loss
  • r = coinsurance rate
  • Value x coinsurance rate = “Should”
  • Insurer will pay the lesser of C , L
  • Example: C = $150,000, V = $200,000, r = 80% ==> C /(V x r) = 93.75%
  • Loss Insurer pays
  • $100,000 $93,750
  • $150,000 $140,625

Remember

  • Purpose of Coinsurance in Property Insurance
  • To achieve equity in rating
  • Why? Because, most property losses are partial
  • “Insurance-to-Value” (ITV)
  • Another name for “Coinsurance” in Property Insurance

Coinsurance in Health Insurance

  • Health insurance policies frequently contain a coinsurance clause (Note: very different from property coinsurance)
  • The clause requires the insured to pay a specified percentage of covered medical expenses in excess of the deductible
  • The purposes of coinsurance in health insurance are to reduce premiums and prevent overutilization of policy benefits
  • Example: Bob’s health plan requires him to pay a deductible of $50 for each visit to the doctor and contains a coinsurance clause requiring him to pay 20% of all charges above that amount. If Bob has a procedure that costs $500, how much is paid by Bob and how much by his insurer?
  • ANSWER: Bob pays his $50 deductible + 20% of the remaining $450, or $90. So Bob pays a total of $140 and his insurer pays $360.

Deeper Look At Coinsurance in HEALTH INSURANCE

  • With coinsurance, insured pays a proportion (the coinsurance rate) of any loss
  • Example: Insured pays 20% of all medical costs (usually after a deductible applies as well)
  • Bob feels sick and goes to see the doctor. The charges from the doctor are $200. Bob has a $50 per visit deductible. How much does Bob pay? $50 + .2($150) = $50 + $30 = $80
  • Reason for coinsurance provisions
  • Insureds demand less than full insurance when the policy has a loading
  • If Bob had a 5% coinsurance provision he would only pay $57.50 and might be tempted to visit the doctor sooner or more often.
  • Reduce moral hazard

Other-Insurance Provisions

  • The purpose of other-insurance provisions is to prevent profiting from insurance and violating the principle of indemnity. This is necessary because it is not uncommon for more than one insurance policy to apply to a given claim (because no single insurer wants to be overexposed to the risk). The question is therefore how should the loss be divided up among the various insurers in a way that is consistent with the principle of indemnity. 2 Methods
  • Under a pro rata liability provision, each insurer’s share of the loss is based on the proportion that its insurance bears to the total amount of insurance on the property
  • Under contribution by equal shares, each insurer shares equally in the loss until the share paid by each insurer equals the lowest limit of liability under any policy, or until the full amount of the loss is paid

Exhibit 10.3 Pro Rata Liability Example

  • Note: It’s very common for multiple insurers to jointly insure a single risk (e.g., building), so they need to agree in advance how to apportion any losses that occur.
  • Example: Suppose Bob owns a building valued at $500K. Insurance Company A agrees to write $300K of coverage, B writes 100K and C writes $100K. How much is paid by each company if a $100K loss occurs?

Exhibit 10.4 Contribution by Equal Shares (Example 1)

  • Example: Suppose Bob owns a building valued at $500K. Insurance Company A agrees to write $100K of coverage, B writes 200K and C writes $300K. How much is paid by each company if a $150K loss occurs?

Exhibit 10.5 Contribution by Equal Shares (Example 2)

  • Example: Suppose Bob owns a building valued at $600K. Insurance Company A agrees to write $100K of coverage, B writes 200K and C writes $300K. How much is paid by each company if a $500K loss occurs?
  • Each insurer pays equal amounts until policy limits are exhausted

Other-Insurance Provisions

Other-insurance Provisions II

  • Under a primary and excess insurance provision, the primary insurer pays first, and the excess insurer pays only after the policy limits under the primary policy are exhausted
  • Example 1: Once coverage under one of a business’s standard liability policy is exhausted, it may have a separate excess policy that kicks in
  • Example 2: If you’re driving a friend’s car (with their permission) and you’re in an accident and found to be at fault, the insurance on the vehicle is primary (insurance follows the vehicle). If the limits on the friend’s car are exhausted, your insurance kicks in as “excess”
  • The coordination of benefits provision in group health insurance is designed to prevent overinsurance and the duplication of benefits if one person is covered under more than one group health insurance plan

Other-insurance Provisions (Continued)

  • Many states have adopted part or all of the coordination of benefits provisions developed by the NAIC, which include a number of rules, including
  • Coverage as an employee is usually primary to coverage as a dependent
  • For dependents in families where the parents are married or are not separated, the plan of the parent whose birthday occurs first during the year is primary

Pro Rata and Excess Coverage Clauses

  • Issue: How is coverage divided when multiple policies apply to the same loss
  • Pro rata clause: divide in proportion to amount of coverage
  • Excess clause: one policy pays losses in excess of the other policy’s limit
  • Why have these clauses?
  • Protects the principle of indemnity (can’t be doubly indemnified)
  • Prevents coverage in excess of loss, which would cause moral hazard

Indemnity vs. Valued Contracts

  • Indemnity Contract: Insurer pays based on the amount of loss that occurred (i.e., amount is determined after loss occurs)
  • Example: auto physical damage (adjuster examines damage and determines repair costs)
  • Valued contract: Insurer pays a pre-determined amount
  • Example: life insurance (value of death benefit is determined when policy is purchased)

Choice of Indemnity vs. Valued Contract

  • Type of contract is largely explained by
  • The costs of assessing value
  • Moral hazard
  • When the amount of loss can be assessed at low cost following the loss, more likely to have indemnity contracts
  • When moral hazard (i.e., insured’s behavior is not greatly influenced by the existence of insurance) is less likely to be a problem, fixing the insurance payment (in dollar terms) before a loss can avoid costly haggling following a loss (e.g., life insurance)

DEEP DIVES into policy limitations

  • Coinsurance in Health Insurance
  • Coinsurance in Property Insurance
  • Exclusions
  • Deductibles
  • Pro-Rata Provisions

Coinsurance in Health Insurance

  • Technically called a percentage participation clause
  • Twofold Purpose of Coinsurance in Health Insurance
  • To Reduce Premiums
  • To Prevent Over-Utilization of Policy Benefits.

Coinsurance in Health Insurance (cont.)

  • Specifically, Major Medical policies typically require the Insured to pay a percentage of covered expenses in excess of the deductible
  • Typical percentage: 20-25% in excess of deductible

Major Medical Coinsurance:Example for a Hospital Visit

  • Covered expenses: $50,500
  • $500 Deductible
  • 80-20 coinsurance clause.
  • $50,500 – 500 = $50,000
  • $50,000 x .80 = $40,000
  • How much does Insured pay?
  • $10,000 coinsurance + $500 deductible = $10,500

Coinsurance in PROPERTY INSURANCE

  • A coinsurance clause in a property insurance contract encourages the insured to insure the property to a stated percentage of its insurable value (e.g. at least 80%)
  • Insurance-to-Value (ITV) is the goal
  • If the coinsurance requirement is not met at the time of the loss, the insured must share in the loss as a coinsurer
  • In other words, there is a penalty for not insuring to the appropriate property value. The penalty is assessed per this formula: (DID/SHOULD) x Loss = Amt. of Recovery, or stated differently

Coinsurance in Property Insurance II

  • The purpose of coinsurance is to achieve equity in rating
  • A property owner wishing to insure for a total loss would pay an inequitable premium if other property owners only insure for partial losses
  • If the coinsurance requirement is met, the insured receives a rate discount, and the policyowner who is underinsured is penalized through application of the coinsurance formula

Property Coinsurance: Example

  • Commercial Building
  • ACV = $500,000
  • 80% coinsurance clause
  • Required Amount of Insurance = $400,000 = 0.8($500,000)
  • Insurance Amount Actually Purchased = $300,000
  • Loss = $10,000
  • 300,000 x $10,000 = $7,500 = Amount of Recovery
  • 400,000
  • If this were a “replacement cost” policy, you would simply use the replacement cost figure instead of ACV

Insurance-to-Value and Coinsurance

  • C = amount of coverage purchased (policy limit) = “Did”
  • L = the amount of the loss = “Loss”
  • V = value of property at the time of the loss
  • r = coinsurance rate
  • Value x coinsurance rate = “Should”
  • Insurer will pay the lesser of C , L
  • Example: C = $150,000, V = $200,000, r = 80% ==> C /(V x r) = 93.75%
  • Loss Insurer pays
  • $100,000 $93,750
  • $150,000 $140,625

Remember

  • Purpose of Coinsurance in Property Insurance
  • To achieve equity in rating
  • Why? Because, most property losses are partial
  • “Insurance-to-Value” (ITV)
  • Another name for “Coinsurance” in Property Insurance

Exclusions

  • Policies exclude coverage for some types of losses
  • Why?
  • reduce administrative costs
  • reduce capital costs
  • reduce moral hazard
  • reduce adverse selection

Exclusions

  • Insurance contracts contain three major types of exclusions
  • Excluded perils, e.g., flood, intentional act (arson)
  • Excluded losses, e.g., a professional liability loss is excluded in the homeowners policy
  • Excluded property, e.g., pets, trees and shrubs are not covered as personal property in the homeowners policy

Exclusions Are Necessary

  • Because
  • Some perils are not commercially insurable
  • e.g., catastrophic losses due to war
  • Extraordinary hazards are present
  • Using your personal automobile as a taxi service (such as Ubermaterially increases risk)
  • Regularly renting our your home through Airbnb (turns your home into a hotelmaterially increases risk)
  • Coverage is provided by other contracts
  • e.g., use of auto excluded on homeowners policy

Exclusions Are Necessary (cont’d)

  • Because
  • Moral hazard is present or it would be difficult to measure the amount of loss
  • e.g., coverage of money (i.e., cash) limited to $200 in homeowners policy
  • Coverage not needed by typical insureds
  • e.g., homeowners policy does not cover aircraft, watercraft

Coverage Not Needed by Typical Insureds

  • Homeowners example
  • Most Homeowners policies do not cover aircraft as personal property
  • To cover aircraft would be unfair to most insureds, who do not own aircraft
  • Because premiums would have to be substantially higher

Examples: Perils Not Commercially Insurable

  • The peril may depart substantially from the requirements of an insurable risk
  • Property and liability contracts
  • Exposure to nuclear radiation
  • Health Insurance
  • Losses within the direct control of the insured
  • For example, intentional, self-inflicted injury
  • Property Insurance
  • Wear and tear (ordinary maintenance issue)
  • Inherent vice (damage without external force, e. g., fruit to rot, diamonds to crack)

Moral Hazard Present

  • Homeowners example
  • Coverage for money typically limited to $200
  • If unlimited amounts were provided, fraudulent claims would increase.
  • Also, there would be a problem determining the exact amount of loss.

Extraordinary Hazards Involved

  • Taxi Cab Example - Personal auto policy
  • Personal Auto policy assumes vehicle used for personal or recreational purposes
  • If car is used as a taxicab, the chance of an accident and resulting lawsuit is much higher
  • Could result in inadequate premium, or
  • Unfair rate discrimination against other insureds who are not using the policy as a taxicab

Coverage Provided by Other Contracts

  • Homeowners policy example
  • Excludes coverage for autos because should be covered under Personal Auto Policy
  • Commercial General Liability policy example
  • Excludes claims covered under Workers Compensation policy

Percent of Americans Who Have Engaged in the “Gig/Sharing Economy” by Transaction

  • Drivers have significant WC exposures
  • About 22% of Americans have offered services in the sharing economy.

On-Demand/Sharing/Peer-to-Peer Economy Impacts Many Lines of Insurance

  • The “On-Demand” Economy is or will impact many segments of the economy important to P/C insurers
  • Auto (personal and commercial)
  • Homeowners/Renters
  • Many Liability Coverages
  • Professional Liability
  • Workers Comp
  • Many unanswered insurance questions
  • Insurance solutions are increasingly available to fill the many insurance gaps that arise

Homesharing Arrangements: Potential Host Exposure Concerns (Receives Rental Income)

Homesharing Arrangements: Potential Traveler Exposure Concerns

Homesharing: Insurance Industry Proposed Changes to Dwelling Policies *

  • *As of Oct. 6, 2015.

Endorsements and Riders

  • If coverage is excluded in a policy, it can sometimes be restored through and endorsement or rider
  • Endorsement: In property and liability insurance, an endorsement is a written provision that adds to, deletes from, or modifies the provisions in the original contract
  • e.g., an earthquake endorsement to a homeowners policy
  • A livery driver endorsement is necessary to use personal vehicle to be insured for use of your personal auto as an Uber driver
  • Rider: In life and health insurance, a rider is a provision that amends or changes the original policy
  • e.g., a waiver-of-premium rider on a life insurance policy

Impact of /Restrictions on Endorsements

  • Endorsement generally takes precedence over the conflicting terms of the contract
  • However, if a law or regulation requires that a standard policy contain certain provisions
  • An endorsement cannot be used to circumvent the purpose of legislation
  • If an endorsement is contrary to a law or regulation, the policy is read as if the endorsement did not exist

“Other” Insurance Provisions

  • Typically present in Property & Liability and Health Insurance policies
  • Apply when more than one contract covers the same loss.
  • Purpose: To prevent profiting from insurance and violation of the principle indemnity

Types of Other Insurance Provisions

  • Pro Rata Liability: Each insurer’s share of the loss is based on the proportion that its insurance bears of the total amount of insurance on the property
  • Contribution by Equal Shares: Each insurer shares equally in the loss until the share paid by each insurer equals the lowest limit of liability under any policy, or until the full amount of the loss is paid
  • Primary and Excess Insurance: The “primary” insurer pays first, and the excess insurer pays only after the policy limits of the primary policy are exhausted.

Pro Rata Liability: Example

  • Pro Rata Liability: Each insurer’s share of the loss is based on the proportion that its insurance bears of the total amount of insurance on the property.
  • Example: Because many insurers limit the amount of coverage they write on any given policy, three insurers cooperate to insurance a property insured for $500,000. Company A agrees to write $300,000; Company B $100,000; Company C $100,000K

Contribution by Equal Shares: (Example 1)

  • Contribution by Equal Shares: Each insurer shares equally in the loss until the share paid by each insurer equals the lowest limit of liability under any policy, or until the full amount of the loss is paid.
  • Example: Suppose the loss is $150,000Each insurer would pay an equal share of $50,000 because that share is below the amount of insurance underwritten by all 3 insurers

Contribution by Equal Shares: (Example 2)

  • Contribution by Equal Shares: Each insurer shares equally in the loss until the share paid by each insurer equals the lowest limit of liability under any policy, or until the full amount of the loss is paid.
  • Example: Suppose the loss is $500,000Insurer A’s insurance (limit) is exhausted at $100,000. Insurer B and C will need to pay the remaining loss so that the full loss is paid (hence $200,000 each)

Primary and Excess Provisions

  • Under a primary and excess insurance provision, the primary insurer pays first.
  • The excess insurer pays only after the policy limits under the primary policy are exhausted.

Primary and Excess Insurance Personal Auto Example

  • Bob borrows Jill’s car and is the at-fault driver in an accident
  • Limits of Liability
  • Bob: $100,000 per person for Bodily Injury (BI)
  • Jill: $50,000 per person for BI
  • Normal rule: Liability insurance on borrowed vehicle is primary; any other insurance is considered excess
  • Court judgment of $75,000
  • Jill’s policy is primary; pays $50,000
  • Bob’s policy is excess; pays $25,000

Resolving Coverage Disputes

  • Contracts of Adhesion
  • Ambiguities are interpreted in favor of policyholder
  • Applied less often to commercial insurance
  • Doctrine of Reasonable Expectations
  • Courts interpret contracts as would a reasonable person not trained in the law
  • Mediation/Arbitration
  • May be required before litigation can be filed
  • Bad Faith Suits

Liability System in the United States

  • From a risk management standpoint, the U.S. liability system poses a significant threat.
  • US liability system is by far the costliest in the world
  • The extent and size of liability exposures has increased dramatically since the post World War II years.
  • Understanding the legal liability system has become an essential competency.

Adapting to an Ever-Changing and Risky World of Tort Liability

  • American Bar Association
  • Magna Carta 800 Conference
  • London, UK
  • 12 June 2015
  • Download at
  • Insurance Information Institute  110 William Street  New York, NY 10038
  • Tel: 212.346.5520  Cell: 917.453.1885  bobh@iii.org 

How Big Are Liability Insurance Markets in Major Economies?

  • The US and UK have the highest shares of liability premiums relative to GDP
  • Liability premiums in China account for a much smaller share of GDP
  • Liability insurance premiums totaled $160 billion in 2013, accounting for 0.26% of global GDP. This also equates to 10% of global non-life premiums and 23% of global commercial lines premiums

General Liability Claims Incurred as a Percent of GDP

  • General liability claims incurred as a % of GDP
  • Traditionally, liability claims grow faster than GDP.
  • The decline in claims began in 2004 and was revealed by a turn in the reserves cycle. Improved safety reduced the frequency of claims.
  • After 2008, underlying claims trends slowed down due to the global recession.
  • The question is whether economic drivers of claims costs have begun to accelerate in the US and some other countries.
  • If historical trends re-emerge, then claims trends are likely to rise with stronger economic growth and at a pace greater than that of overall GDP
  • Question as to what will happen when the current period of reserve releases runs its course

Frivolous Litigation Impacts the Economy, Businesses, Products, and Households

  • Reduces economic opportunities, including jobs and government revenues. In 2021
  • 4.24 million lost jobs
  • $429.35 billion in lost output annually
  • $110 billion in lost government (federal, state, and local) revenues annually
  • (Source: Perryman Group)
  • Increases costs of products and services, like groceries and gas.
  • $3,600 “tort tax” paid by the average American household annually
  • (Source: U.S. Chamber Institute for Legal Reform)

Legal System Abuse: Many Interrelated Causes, Difficult to Manage

  • Source:Risk and Uncertainty Management Center, Univ. of South Carolina, adapted from Verisk “Social Inflation” presentation (2020).
  • INSURANCE CLAIM COSTS
  • Increasing Propensity to Sue
  • Size of Jury Awards
  • Courts/ Juries Favoring Plaintiffs
  • Growing Distrust of Large Corps.
  • Litigation Financing
  • Aggressive Plaintiff Bar Ads
  • Changes in Regulatory and Legal Environment Bar Ads
  • Applied to a seemingly limitless number of issues, these drivers are pushing tort costs (and therefore claim costs upward)

US Tort Cost Trends

  • 1933-Current Era
  • Examination of Long-Term Escalation of Tort Costs and Evidence of a “Bending” in the Cost Curve

Sources of Law & Tort Liability

Branches of the Law

  • Criminal Law – Usually the result of statutes—is law that is against the state (i.e., federal, state, local government)
  • Civil Law – Applies to acts against other individuals or entities
  • Contract Law – Applies to when a contract governs the relationship between the parties in a dispute
  • Tort Law – Applies to when a contract does NOT govern the relationship between the parties in a dispute

Basis of Legal Liability

  • A legal wrong is a violation of a person’s legal rights, or a failure to perform a legal duty owed to a certain person or to society as a whole
  • Could have physical impact on person/property, monetary impact, reputational impact, etc.
  • Legal wrongs include
  • Crime
  • Breach of contract
  • Tort

Basic Tort Liability Rules

  • No Liability – Defendant cannot be held liable
  • Liability for Negligence – Defendant is liable if found negligent, but might be able to avoid liability using certain defenses
  • Strict Liability – Defendant is liable even if not negligent, but might be able to avoid liability using certain defenses
  • Absolute Liability – Defendant is always liable; no defenses are available

Three Types of Torts

  • Negligence
  • Intentional tort
  • Absolute liability

Negligence

  • Failure to exercise the standard of care required by law to protect others from unreasonable risk.
  • The standard of care is not the same for each wrongful act.
  • Very important concept when it comes to risk management and insurance
  • In the US, a defendant is responsible for paying their own defense costs, even if found by a court to have zero negligence, no matter how frivolous the case brought against them.
  • Some countries (like the UK) have “loser pays” laws, that require an unsuccessful plaintiff to pay the legal costs of the defendant. The idea is that this discourages frivolous lawsuits.

Frivolous Litigation Is One Factor that Supports High Liability Costs in the US

  • George Sink board in Columbia, SC

Components/Elements of Negligence

  • The following 4 elements are required to prove negligence
  • Legal duty to the plaintiff to use due care
  • Breach of duty: Failure to conform to the standard of care
  • Causal connection: Defendant’s negligent behavior is the proximate cause of the plaintiff’s injury or damages
  • Damages: Bodily injury or property damage to the Plaintiff (could also refer to damages to reputation, emotional distress, invasion of privacy, financial loss, etc.)

Statutes

  • Statutes: Impose legal liability on persons regardless of whether they committed a tort or assumed a contract.

Insurance and Legal Liability

  • The same conduct could constitute both a civil wrong and a crime.
  • Example
  • Suppose a driver causes the death of a pedestrian
  • Government authorities may charge the driver with vehicular homicide, a criminal act.
  • The driver may also be subject to a civil action by the estate of the deceased pedestrian for medical bills, funeral expenses, loss of support and other damages
  • Insurance would not respond to the criminal charges
  • It could, however provide payment for the civil claims (damages as well as defense costs).

Standard of Care

  • The standard of care is based on the care required of a reasonably prudent person
  • The person’s actions are compared with the actions of a reasonably prudent person under the same circumstances.
  • If the conduct and behavior are below the standard of care required of a reasonably prudent person, the person may be found negligent.
  • NOTE: Standard of Care is a very subjective concept

Joint and Several Liability

  • What does it mean?
  • Answer: If multiple parties are liable and one cannot pay, then the others are responsible
  • Example: If several companies produced products containing the same carcinogen that resulted in customers developing cancer, they could all be sued. If some go bankrupt as a result of the suits, the others may be obliged to pick up the whole tab.
  • For insurers, joint and several liability related to issues such as asbestos and environmental claims has been extremely expensive of the past ~40 years

Types of Damages Awards

  • The law allows for the following types of damages
  • Compensatory damages compensate the victim for losses actually incurred. They include
  • Special damages, e.g., medical expenses, loss of income
  • General damages, e.g., pain and suffering
  • Punitive damages are designed to punish people and organizations so that others are deterred from committing the same wrongful act

Defenses to Negligence

  • Assumption of Risk
  • A person who understands and recognizes the danger inherent in a particular activity
  • Cannot recover in the event of injury
  • Contributory Negligence
  • If the injured person’s conduct falls below the standard of care for their own protection, the injured party cannot collect damages
  • Old steps down to the Riverwalk area of Savannah, GA

Defenses to Negligence

  • Signs at the Grand Canyon

Defenses to Negligence

  • Cliffs of Moher, West Coast of Ireland: The rest of the world is less litigious than the US

Defenses to Negligence (cont.)

  • Comparative Negligence
  • Allows the injured person to recover (at least some) damages even though he or she has contributed to their injury.
  • Because of the harshness of contributory negligence, most states have enacted some form of comparative negligence.
  • These laws are not uniform among the states

Contributory Negligence

  • Under a contributory negligence law, the injured person cannot collect damages if his or her care falls below the standard of care required for his or her protection (e.g., they were intoxicated)
  • Under strict application of common law, the injured cannot collect damages if his or her conduct contributed in any way to the injury (i.e., even if the injured party were just 1% negligent, they would collect $0)
  • This interpretation was historically commonplace in many situations
  • Example: Until the advent of modern workers compensation systems (early 1900s), workers could be denied benefits for their occupational injuries even if they were just 1% negligent

Comparative Negligence Law

  • Under a comparative negligence law, the financial burden of the injury is shared by both parties according to their respective degrees of fault.
  • Comparative Negligence Rules
  • Pure Rule
  • 50 Percent Rule
  • 51 Percent Rule

Comparative Negligence

  • Pure Rule: (12 States)
  • Injured person can collect damages even if they were negligent, but award is reduced proportionally (e.g., if 15% negligent, award reduced 15%)
  • 50 Percent Rule: (11 States)
  • Cannot recover if you are 50% or more at fault. However, if you are 49% at fault or less, you can recover from the other party but the award will be reduced.
  • Examples: You are in a car accident and your damages are $10,000
  • 50% or more at FaultRecover $0
  • 49% at FaultDamage award reduced by 49% so recovery is $5100
  • 51 Percent Rule: (22 States)
  • Cannot recover if you are 51% or more at fault. However, if you are 50% at fault or less, you can recover from the other party but the award will be reduced.
  • 51% at FaultRecover $0
  • 50% at FaultDamage award reduced by 50% so recovery is $5000

Applications of Negligence Law

  • A trespasser is a person who enters or remains on the owner’s property without the owner’s consent
  • The duty to refrain from injuring a trespasser is sometimes referred to as the duty of slight care
  • A licensee is a person who enters the premises with the occupant’s expressed or implied permission
  • The property owner must warn the licensee of unsafe conditions or activities which are apparent
  • E.g., a door-to-door salesperson
  • An invitee is a person who is invited onto the premises for the benefit of the occupant
  • The occupant has an obligation to inspect the premises and eliminate any dangerous conditions

Applications of Negligence Law

  • Example
  • An attractive nuisance is a hazardous condition that can attract and injure children
  • The occupants of land are liable for the injuries of children who may be attracted by some dangerous condition, feature or article
  • e.g., a building contractor leaves the keys in a tractor, and a child is injured while driving it
  • Unfenced swimming pool (even if it’s in your own yard and the child who drowned was uninvited)
  • Railroad property
  • Construction sites
  • High voltage lines
  • Even refrigerators were once considered attractive nuisances

Imputed Negligence

  • Imputed Negligence: Means that under certain situations the negligence of one person can be attributed to another
  • Examples
  • Employee acting on behalf of an employer. If the
  • employee acts negligently, then the employer may be
  • held liable (e.g., Tracey Morgan vs. Walmart)
  • Vicarious Liability Laws: a driver’s negligence is imputed to the vehicle owner. If John borrows Mary’s car, then Mary could be found negligent if John is involved in an accident.
  • Family Purpose Doctrine: Owner of vehicle held liable for the negligent acts committed by immediate family member operating the vehicle (e.g., you injure someone driving a family vehicle)
  • Joint Business Ventures: Acts of a business partner
  • Dram Shop Liability Laws: Bar/restaurant could be held responsible if intoxicated patron is involved in an accident

Some legal defenses can defeat a claim for damages

  • The last clear chance rule states that a plaintiff who is endangered by his or her own negligence can still recover damages from the defendant if the defendant has a last clear chance to avoid the accident but fails to do so
  • Under the assumption of risk doctrine, a person who understands and recognizes the danger inherent in a particular activity cannot recover damages in the event of an injury

Strict Liability

  • Liability does not depend on actual negligence or the intent to harm.
  • Rather, it is based on an absolute duty to make something safe.
  • Often applies in products liability
  • Design, production or warnings of the product
  • Were defective or unreasonably dangerous
  • Not necessary to show that defect was due to negligence
  • VACCINES (with the exception of some childhood vaccines)

Absolute Liability

  • An older legal doctrine than strict liability.
  • Imposes liability without regard to fault due to the ultra- hazardous nature of certain activities.
  • No defenses are available if the defendant caused the injury.

Examples of Absolute Liability

  • Occupational injury/disease of employees under a workers compensation law
  • Blasting operations that injure another person
  • Manufacturing of medicines, food products, and explosives
  • Owning of wild or dangerous animals
  • Crop spraying by airplanes

Intentional Tort: Definition

  • Intentional Tort represents a legal liability
  • that arises from an intentional act or omission
  • that results in harm or injury to another person
  • or damage to the person’s property.

Categories of Intentional Tort

  • Those involving interference with personal rights
  • Those involving interference with property rights
  • Business or competitive torts

Examples of Intentional Torts

  • Assault and battery
  • Trespass
  • False imprisonment
  • Fraud
  • Libel and slander (including online)
  • Intentional infliction of emotional distress
  • Copyright infringement
  • Invasion of Privacy (big issue now with online risks)

Battery

  • Battery is the intentional, harmful or offensive touching of another without their consent.
  • A contact is harmful if it produces bodily injury
  • However, battery also includes non-harmful contacts that are offensive
  • Calculated to offend a reasonable sense of personal dignity

Assault

  • Assault is the intentional attempt or offer to cause harmful or offensive contact
  • where the attempt or offer
  • causes a reasonable apprehension of imminent battery in the other person’s mind.
  • In an assault case, it is irrelevant whether the threatened contact actually occurs

Trespass to Land

  • Definition: Any unauthorized intentional intrusion upon on another’s real property.
  • These might include
  • Physically entering the other’s land
  • Causing another to do so (chasing onto land)
  • Remaining after one’s right to remain has ceased
  • Failing to remove form the land anything one has a duty to remove
  • Invading the airspace above or the subsurface beneath

Intent Required for Trespass

  • The intent required for trespass liability is
  • Simply the intent to be on the land or cause it to be invaded
  • A person can be held liable for trespass
  • Even though the trespass resulted from mistaken belief that the person’s entry was legally justified

False Imprisonment

  • Definition: Intentional confinement of another person for an appreciable time
  • A few minutes could be enough
  • Confinement must be complete
  • Blocking one means of exit, where others exist, does not constitute false imprisonment
  • Provided that the other party could reasonably been expected to know of the other other exits
  • And, using the escape route does not involve unreasonable risk of harm

Fraud

  • The tort a claim of knowing misrepresentation; also known as deceit.
  • Liability for fraud usually requires
  • A false statement of material fact
  • Knowingly or recklessly made
  • With the intent to induce reliance by the other
  • Actual reliance by the other
  • Harm to the other due to his reliance

Tort of Defamation

  • Protects the individual’s interest in his reputation.
  • Defamation is defined as
  • Unprivileged publication of
  • False and defamatory statements concerning another
  • Truth is a complete defense in a defamation case

Libel

  • Libel refers to
  • Written or printed defamation
  • Or, to other defamations that have a permanent (more or less) physical form
  • Picture
  • Sign
  • Statue

Slander

  • Slander refers to
  • All other defamatory statements
  • Mainly oral defamation
  • However, courts generally treat defamatory statements made on radio or television as libel

Law of Negligence (Continued)

  • Some legal defenses can defeat a claim for damages
  • The last clear chance rule states that a plaintiff who is endangered by his or her own negligence can still recover damages from the defendant if the defendant has a last clear chance to avoid the accident but fails to do so
  • Under the assumption of risk doctrine, a person who understands and recognizes the danger inherent in a particular activity cannot recover damages in the event of an injury

Imputed Negligence

  • Under certain conditions, the negligence of one person can be attributed to another
  • Under a vicarious liability law, a motorist’s negligence is imputed to the vehicle’s owner
  • Under the family purpose doctrine, the owner of an auto can be held liable for negligent acts committed by family members
  • Negligence may arise out of a joint business venture
  • Under a dram shop law, a business that sells liquor can be held liable for damages that may result from the sale of liquor

Res Ipsa Loquitur – “the thing speaks for itself”

  • Under this doctrine, the very fact that the injury or damage occurs establishes a presumption of negligence
  • Three requirements must be met for res ipsa loquitur to apply
  • The event is one that normally does not occur in the absence of negligence
  • The defendant has exclusive control over the instrumentality causing the accident
  • The injured party has not contributed to the accident in any way

Specific Applications of the Law of Negligence

  • The standard of care owed to others depends upon the situation
  • A trespasser is a person who enters or remains on the owner’s property without the owner’s consent
  • The duty to refrain from injuring a trespasser is sometimes referred to as the duty of slight care

Specific Applications of the Law of Negligence (Continued)

  • A licensee is a person who enters the premises with the occupant’s expressed or implied permission
  • The property owner must warn the licensee of unsafe conditions which are apparent
  • An invitee is a person who is invited onto the premises for the benefit of the occupant
  • The occupant has an obligation to inspect the premises and eliminate any dangerous conditions

Specific Applications of the Law of Negligence (Continued)

  • An attractive nuisance is a hazardous condition that can attract and injure children
  • The occupants of land are liable for the injuries of children who may be attracted by some dangerous condition, feature, or article
  • For Example, a building contractor leaves the keys in a tractor, and a child is injured while driving it

Specific Applications of the Law of Negligence (Continued)

  • Owners and operators of automobiles who drive in a careless manner can be held liable for property damage or bodily injury sustained by another person
  • An owner who is not the operator can be held liable for the acts of operators if an agency relationship exists
  • Charitable institutions are no longer immune from lawsuits, especially with respect to commercial activities

Specific Applications of the Law of Negligence (Continued)

  • Governmental entities can be sued in almost every aspect of governmental activity
  • The doctrine of sovereign immunity has been modified over time
  • A governmental unit can be held liable if it is negligent in the performance of a proprietary function, e.g., the operation of water plants
  • Immunity from lawsuits for governmental functions, such as the planning of a sewer system, has eroded over time

Specific Applications of the Law of Negligence (Continued)

  • Under the doctrine of respondeat superior, an employer can be held liable for the negligent acts of employees while they are acting on the employer’s behalf
  • The worker must be an employee
  • The employee must be acting within the scope of employment when the negligent act occurred
  • Parents can be held liable for the actions of a child if
  • The child uses a dangerous weapon to injure someone
  • The child is acting as an agent for the parents
  • A minor child is operating a family car

Specific Applications of the Law of Negligence (Continued)

  • Most states have laws that hold parents liable for willful and malicious acts of children that result in property damage to others
  • Owners of wild animals are held strictly liable for injuries to others
  • Strict liability may also be imposed on the owners of ordinary pets, such as dogs

Current Tort Liability Problems

  • Critics claim the present tort system has numerous defects that reduce its effectiveness in compensating injured victims, including
  • Rising tort liability costs
  • Inefficiency in compensating injured victims
  • Uncertainty of legal outcomes
  • High jury awards
  • Long delays in settling lawsuits

Rising Tort Liability Costs

  • Several factors help explain the substantial increase in tort costs over time, including
  • Juries and judges desensitized to the value of the dollar when damages are awarded
  • Aggressive and creative litigation strategies
  • Rising medical costs
  • Abuses in class action lawsuits
  • States striking down portions of tort reform
  • An increase in lawsuits against company officials
  • Deep pocket syndrome
  • Exploitation of high-verdict cases by the media

Exhibit 19.2 Tort Costs Relative to GDP ($billions)

Exhibit 19.3 Median1 and Average Personal Injury Jury Awards, 2000 and 2009 (000 omitted)

Tort Reform in the States

  • State tort reforms include
  • Capping noneconomic damages, such as pain and suffering (usually to a specific dollar limit or as a multiple of economic damages)
  • Reinstating the state-of-the-art defense for product liability cases
  • Restricting punitive damages awards
  • Restricting eligibility to file class action suits in state courts
  • Modifying the collateral source rule
  • Modifying the joint and several liability rule
  • Alternative dispute resolution (ADR), a technique for resolving a legal dispute using arbitration or mediation
  • Reforming the judiciary

Medical Sector Claims

  • Medical malpractice occurs when a negligent act or omission by a physician or other healthcare professional results in injury or harm to the patient
  • The injured patient must show that the doctor deviated from the generally accepted standards of practice in this particular case.
  • Many malpractice suits are due to medical errors by healthcare providers, especially errors in hospitals that result in the death of patients

Medical Sector Claims (Continued)

  • Other reasons patients sue physicians include
  • The long-term relationship between patients and physicians that existed in the past has been lost
  • People are more litigious than in the past
  • Physicians and other medical experts will now testify against physicians in malpractice cases
  • The media has made more people aware of the vulnerability of physicians to malpractice suits
  • Physicians accuse attorneys of filing malpractice suits because of the high fees that attorneys may collect if they win
  • There is a growing resentment against large for-profit healthcare firms and managed care plans

Reducing Medical Malpractice Costs

  • Methods to reduce medical malpractice costs include
  • Not charging for “never events”
  • Laws allowing physicians to apologize without allowing the admission to be used against them
  • Prompt disclosure of medical errors
  • Remedial action against problem physicians
  • Emphasis on risk management principles

Reducing Medical Malpractice Costs (Continued)

  • The major impact of the ACA on medical sector liability claims is the sheer number of new prospective plaintiffs
  • Law firms are advertising for potential plaintiffs to join class action lawsuits
  • Medically related class actions include defective replacement joints, asbestos-related mesothelioma and ineffective drugs
  • Class actions are costly to defend and can result in significant legal liability

Liability Arising from Technological Change

  • Cyber Liability refers to the risk that an unauthorized party gains access to an organization’s data
  • Individuals who have their personal information exposed through a cyberattack may seek damages
  • Unauthorized use of data can create liability as well (e.g., Facebook’s issue with Cambridge Analytics in 2018)
  • Hydraulic fracturing (“fracking”) can create liability exposures by causing tremors in areas not subject to seismic activity, and contaminating groundwater
  • Organizations using unmanned aircraft systems, or drones, are liable for damages caused by the drone and improper use (e.g., invasion of privacy)
  • Autonomous Vehicles: 94% of vehicle accidents are due to driver error today—how will that change in the future?

Which Company Popular with Young Stock Traders Revealed a Big Hack 2 Years Ago?

  • 5 million email address exposed (out of 22.4M accounts)
  • Full names for a different group of 2 million users exposed
  • More extensive info for a subset of ~300 exposed
  • Cause: “Vishing Attack”: Intruder was able to gain access to Robinhood systems by impersonating an authorized party to a customer-service employee on the phone
  • A ransom was demanded after attack was contained

Cyberinsurance: What Does it Cover?

  • Cyberinsurance policies are intended to cover a variety of both liability and property losses that may result when a business engages in various electronic activities, such as selling on the Internet or collecting and storing data within its internal electronic network. Cyber cover a business' liability for a data breach in which the firm's customers' personal information, such as Social Security or credit card numbers, is exposed or stolen
  • The policies cover a variety of expenses associated with data breaches, including: notification costs, credit monitoring, costs to defend claims by state regulators, fines and penalties, and loss resulting from identity theft.

Cyberinsurance: What Does it Cover? (cont’d)

  • In addition, the policies cover liability arising from website media content, as well as property exposures from
  • (a) business interruption (e.g. denial of service attack)
  • (b) data loss/destruction (e.g., theft of data, malware damages data)
  • (c) computer fraud
  • (d) funds transfer loss (e.g. phishing), and
  • (e) cyber extortion (e.g., randsomware)

Liability Arising from Societal/Economic/ Technological Change

  • The “sharing economy” is creating new exposures
  • Automobile and homeowners insurance policies are evolving to address new liabilities arising from
  • Car sharing
  • Ride sharing (Uber)
  • Home sharing (Airbnb)

The Sharing Economy Has Spawned Exclusions and Clarifications—But Also New Coverages

Percent of Americans Who Have Engaged in the “Gig/Sharing Economy” by Transaction

  • About 22% of Americans have offered services in the sharing economy.
  • Drivers have significant WC exposures

On-Demand/Sharing/Peer-to-Peer Economy Impacts Many Lines of Insurance

  • The “On-Demand” Economy is or will impact many segments of the economy important to P/C insurers
  • Auto (personal and commercial)
  • Homeowners/Renters
  • Many Liability Coverages
  • Professional Liability
  • Workers Comp
  • Initially, many unanswered insurance questions
  • Insurance solutions are increasingly available to fill the many insurance gaps that arise

Economic Role of the Tort Liability System

  • From an economic perspective, tort system has 2 objectives
  • Provide the right incentives to achieve optimal safety
  • optimal loss control (i.e., want tort system to provide incentives for individuals and firms to optimally invest in loss control)
  • Provide the right amount (optimal) compensation to victims
  • optimal insurance coverage (i.e., want tort system to provide optimal amount of protection (insurance coverage) to people
  • Rarely possible to achieve both simultaneously
  • Objective of Tort System: The tort system should attempt to minimize the cost of risk to society, where the cost of risk includes expected accident losses, the cost of loss control, the cost of compensating victims (loss financing) and the cost of residual uncertainty

Economic Role of the Tort Liability System (cont.)

  • To determine if the tort system is working toward those objectives, we must also consider transaction costs, both of
  • the tort system, and
  • the alternative methods for achieving these objectives
  • Recall, that the objective of risk management is to minimize the cost of risk.
  • This view leads to the following conclusion: It is rarely optimal to minimize the probability of loss or injury.
  • Rather than have a zero-risk objective, the tort system should provide incentives for people to invest in additional safety only if the marginal benefits exceed the marginal costs.

Economic Role of the Tort Liability System

  • Objectives can conflict
  • Optimal safety might imply too much compensation to victims
  • Optimal compensation might imply too high legal costs
  • Framework ignores certain issues
  • Distribution of wealth
  • Social justice

Identifying Costs and Benefits in Practice

  • Benefits of loss control can be difficult to estimate
  • Can use historical data on your own firm
  • Use industry data
  • Hire consultants, brokers
  • Get estimates of insurance premium reductions
  • Brokers and insurers
  • Key danger: Settle for the information at hand. Give way to the availability bias.

Identifying Costs and Benefits in Practice

  • Many firms and governments routinely conduct cost/benefit analyses: Examples
  • Auto Safety
  • Should collision avoidance systems be required?
  • Homes
  • Should all homes in hurricane prone areas be required to have wind-resistant windows?
  • Child-Resistant Packaging for Nonprescription Drugs
  • Very expensive but may save the lives of children
  • Cyber Security
  • How much to invest in anti-malware software?
  • Gun Safety
  • Should all new handguns be required to have Touch ID technology so that only a registered owner can use it

Government Safety Programs

  • Examples
  • Occupational Safety and Health Administration (OSHA)
  • National Highway Transportation Administration (NHTSA)
  • Environmental Protection Agency (EPA)
  • Food & Drug Administration (FDA)
  • Consumer Protection Safety Commission (CPSC)
  • Why have safety regulations?
  • Two reasons
  • Firms may not consider all benefits of loss control if workers or customers are not fully informed
  • Avoids duplication of expenditures on safety research

Valuing Life

  • Often loss control decisions involve changing the probability of death. How do you value a life?
  • One Approach: Use wage differentials for jobs with different probabilities of death (actual studies are more complex)
  • Example
  • Job 1 has .0002 higher probability of death on the job per year
  • Job 1 has $1,000 wage premium per year, holding all else equal (earn more to compensate for risk)
  • Employees willing to receive $1,000 for a .0002 increased chance of dying.
  • $1,000 = .0002 x (Value of Life)
  •  Value of Life = 1,000/.0002 = $5 million
  • Interpretation: When an employee is willing to work for $1000 more per year in exchange for a .0002 increases chance of dying, the employee is implicitly valuing their life at $5 million

Homeowners insurance

  • An Essential Coverage Carried by 95% of All Homeowners

Home Insurance: The Basics

  • Large Market: ~$100 Billion in 2021
  • 95% of homeowners carry coverage
  • Most condo owners carry coverage as well, as do ~25% of renters
  • Not Compulsory: But banks will require you buy coverage at least large enough to protect the outstanding balance on a mortgage
  • Risks to homes covered under Homeowners Multi-Peril Policies, so named because the policies cover a large number of “perils” (i.e., potential causes of loss). They also provide liability coverage.
  • Policies originally (pre-1950s) coverage fire only because fire was the principal cause of loss to property (people typically heated and cooked with wood, coal or kerosene). Damage due to many other perils (with the notable exception of flood and earthquake) were added to policies in the 1950s as home construction and ownership surged after WW II.

Home Insurance: The Basics

  • Homeowners insurance is a package policy, providing both property and personal liability insurance.
  • The typical policy covers the house, garage and other structures on the property—as well as personal property inside the house—against a wide variety of perils, such as fire, windstorm, vandalism and accidental water damage.
  • The typical homeowners policy includes theft coverage on personal property anywhere in the world and liability coverage for accidental harm caused to others. It also reimburses the policyholder for the additional cost of living elsewhere while his or her house is being repaired or rebuilt after a fire or other disaster.
  • Policies with very similar coverages are available for renters and condo owners

Home Insurance: The Basics

  • There are several common Homeowners Forms
  • Designed for specialized uses: homes, condos/co-ops, rentals, older homes
  • We will focus on the HO-3 Form
  • Most common form—used to insure traditional homes (but not rentals, condos/co-ops)
  • Insures the dwelling and other structures against physical loss to property
  • Means that all direct physical losses to the dwelling and other structures unless otherwise specifically excluded
  • Damage to dwelling and other structures are typically covered on a “replacement cost” (not actual cash value) basis
  • Damage/loss to contents will be ACV

Home Insurance: What’s Covered

  • What Does the HO-3 Form Cover?
  • Fire or Lightning
  • Windstorm or Hail
  • Explosion
  • Riot or Civil Commotion
  • Smoke
  • Vandalism or Malicious Mischief
  • Theft
  • Volcanic Eruption
  • Falling Objects
  • Weight of Ice, Snow, or Sleet
  • Accidental Discharge or Overflow of Water or Steam
  • Sudden & Accidental Tearing Apart, Cracking, Burning, or Bulging
  • Freezing
  • Sudden & Accidental Damage from Artificially Generated Electric Current
  • The HO-3 policy is an “All Perils” policy—it covers everything that’s not excluded. After the exclusions, this is what’s covered.

Home Insurance: What’s Not Covered

  • What’s Does the HO-3 Form Exclude?
  • Earth Movement (earthquake coverage can be endorsed on) (Earthquake Exclusion)
  • Ordinance or Law (some coverage may be provided in your policy)
  • Water Damage (Sudden & Accidental Water Damage is automatically included; others can be endorsed onto the policy) (Flood Exclusion)
  • Power Failure
  • Neglect
  • Nuclear Hazard
  • Intentional Loss (e.g., arson by you)
  • Government Action
  • Collapse (some coverage may be provided in your policy)
  • Theft to a Dwelling Under Construction
  • Vandalism or Malicious Mischief if Structure is Vacant 60+ Days
  • Mold, Fungus, or Wet Rot (some coverage may be provided in your policy)
  • Wear & Tear, Deterioration
  • Mechanical Breakdown
  • Smog, Rust & Corrosion
  • Smoke from Agricultural Smudging & Industrial Operations
  • Discharge, Dispersal, Seepage of Pollutants
  • Settling, Shrinking, Bulging, or Expanding
  • Birds, Vermin, Rodents, Insects
  • Animals Owned by Insured
  • The HO-3 policy covers everything that’s not excluded. After the exclusions, this is what’s covered.
  • Also Generally Excluded
  • 1. Damage from Home-Based Businesses
  • 2. Damage from Rental Activity

The HO-3 Form

  • The HO-3 Form has two sections
  • Section I: Covers dwelling, other structures and personal property (i.e., contents of dwelling)
  • Section I has 4 basic coverages, Coverages A, B, C, D (discussed below)
  • Section II: Provides personal liability insurance
  • Section I Coverages
  • Coverage A: Covers the dwelling as well as any structure attached to the dwelling (e.g., garage)
  • Specifically excludes land (and anything growing on it—likes trees or shrubs)
  • Coverage B: Covers other structures that are separated from the dwelling by a clear space (e.g., detached garage, tool shed; also includes such things as fences, utility lines, outdoor pool)
  • Amount of coverage available is typically 10% of the insured value of the dwelling under Coverage A
  • Example: If the dwelling is insured for $100,000, then the total amount available to pay for damage to other structures is limited to $10,000.
  • Excludes land and other structures used for business purposes

The HO-3 Form

  • Section I Coverages (cont’d)
  • Coverage C: Covers personal property
  • Defined as property owned or used by an insured
  • Coverage extends to anywhere in the world
  • Amount of coverage is typically 50% of Coverage A on dwelling (higher % is available)
  • Example: If the dwelling is insured for $100,000, then the total amount available to pay for damage to personal property is limited to $50,000 (unless higher % selected)
  • There are special limits of liability that apply to certain categories of personal property such as cash ($200), collectibles, jewelry $1,500), firearms ($2,500), silverware/goldware ($2,500)
  • Exclusions Include: Animals/Pets, motor vehicles, property of boarders/renters

The HO-3 Form

  • Section I Coverages (cont’d)
  • Coverage D: Covers “Loss of Use” which has 3 main benefits
  • Additional Living Expense (ALE): Covers the increase in living expenses necessary to maintain the family’s standard of living while the insured premises is being repaired
  • Example: My insured home suffers a fire and repairs take 3 months. I rent an apartment for $1,500/month. The ALE coverage would pay $4,500.
  • Fair Rental Value: Covers loss of rent if part of home is rented to others
  • Prohibited Use: Civil authorities may prohibit owner from living in the home
  • Example: Home next door to me suffers a fire and is deemed unstable. Authorities prohibit me from inhabiting my home because of the fear that the adjacent home might collapse. (Typically have 2 weeks of coverage)
  • Example: Additional Coverages: Are in addition to Coverages A-D
  • Debris Removal
  • Reasonable Repairs
  • Ordinance & Law

The HO-3 Form

  • Section II: Liability ($100,000 limits most common)
  • Coverage E: Coverage in the event a suit is brought against the insured because of bodily injury and/or property damage resulting from the acts or non-acts of the insured. (Example: Someone slips and falls on your icy steps and injures themselves; They sue you.)
  • Legal defense costs are “outside the policy limits”
  • Coverage F: Medical payments to others (Example: A guest needs stitches after cutting themselves on a sharp object in your home)
  • Coverage G: Damage to others’ property (Example: Your child accidentally throws a ball through your neighbor’s expensive window from your backyard)

Dog Bites: Second Costliest Type of Claim for Home Insurers

Dog Bites: Second Costliest Type of Claim for Home Insurers

Auto Insurance

Auto Insurance Fundamentals

Auto insurance

  • Auto Insurance is the Largest of All Lines of Insurance—By Far!

Personal Auto Policy (PAP) Coverages:4 Main Coverage Types

  • Part A: Liability Coverage
  • Pays damages for “bodily injury” or “property damage” for which any “insured” becomes legally responsible because of an auto accident.
  • Part B: Medical Payments Coverage
  • Pays any reasonable expenses for necessary medical and funeral expenses because of “bodily injury” caused by accident and sustained by an “insured”.

Personal Auto Policy Coverages:4 Main Coverage Types (cont’d)

  • Part C: Uninsured/Underinsured Motorists Coverage
  • Pays compensatory damages which an “insured” is legally entitled to recover from the owner or operator of an “uninsured motor vehicle because of “bodily injury” sustained by an “insured” and caused by an accident involving someone with no insurance or insufficient insurance
  • Part D: Coverage for Damage to Your Auto
  • Pays for direct and accidental loss to “your covered auto” or any “non-owned auto”…caused by (1) Other than “collision” only if the declarations indicate that Other Than Collision is provided for that auto. (2) “Collision” only if the declarations indicate…

Part A: Liability Coverage

  • Liability Coverage (Part A) is the Most Important Part of the PAP
  • It protects a covered person against a suit or claim arising out of the ownership or operation of a covered vehicle
  • The coverage is usually written in split limits, where the amounts of insurance for bodily injury liability and property damage liability are stated separately
  • Split limits of 250/500/100 mean that you have bodily injury coverage of $250,000 for each person, a maximum of $500,000 of bodily injury coverage per accident, and a maximum of $100,000 for property damage liability
  • Every state has minimum limits you must carry to legally drive (25/50/10 in SC)

Part A: Liability Coverage

  • Example Using SC Minimum Required Limits of 25/50/10
  • You’re at fault in an accident, you’re liable for the injuries of 1 person and damage to their property
  • Your insurance will pay up to $25K for their injuries and up to $10K for damage to their property (e.g., vehicle, home)
  • Assume 3 people injured and property is damaged.
  • Your insurance will pay no more than $25K for any single individual and no more than $50K for all 3. It will also pay no more than $10K for property damage. (You are personally responsible for all costs beyond your insurance policy’s limits!!! Hence, having sufficient limits is important!)

Eligible Vehicles

  • A four-wheeled motor vehicle owned or leased by the insured for at least six consecutive months
  • A pick-up or van with a gross vehicle weight rating of 10,000 pounds or less
  • Cannot be used for deliveries, with some exceptions

Definition of Covered Auto

  • Any auto shown in the declarations
  • A newly acquired auto
  • Coverage depends on whether it is an additional vehicle or a replacement vehicle and whether the declarations indicates at least one auto for collision coverage
  • A trailer owned by the named insured
  • A temporary substitute vehicle
  • which is a non-owned auto or trailer used temporarily because of mechanical breakdown, repair, servicing, loss, or destruction of a covered vehicle

Discussion - Part A: Liability Coverage

  • Most important part of the PAP
  • Two-fold promise
  • Protect a covered person against a claim or lawsuit arising out of the ownership or operation of a covered vehicle
  • Provide defense and pay all legal defense costs for claims covered by the policy
  • Defense costs are paid for in addition to the policy limits (i.e., “outside” policy limits)
  • Example: Assume you have SC min limits of 25/50/10 and $50K is used to pay for injuries. If you also have $10K in legal costs, that will still be paid (in addition to the $50K)

Definition of Insured – Part A: Liability

  • “You” or any resident family member for the ownership, maintenance and use of any auto or trailer
  • Any person using the named insured’s covered auto
  • Coverage follows the vehicle
  • Any person or organization, but only for liability arising out of an insured person’s use of a covered auto on behalf of that person or organization.
  • Any person or organization legally responsible for the named insured’s or family members’ use of any auto or trailer (other than a covered auto or one owned by that person or organization)

Definition of “You” and “Your”

  • The “named insured(s)” shown in the declarations.
  • The spouse if a resident of the same household.
  • If the spouse ceases to be a resident of the same household during the policy period or prior to the inception of this policy, the spouse will be considered “you” and “your” under this policy but only the earlier of
  • The end of 90 days following the residency
  • The effective date of another policy listing the spouse as the named insured
  • The end of the policy period

Limits of Liability

  • The coverage is usually written in split limits
  • Amounts of insurance for bodily injury liability and property damage liability are stated separately
  • Example: split limits of 250/500/100 mean that you have
  • bodily injury coverage of $250,000 for each person
  • maximum of $500,000 of bodily injury coverage per accident
  • maximum of $100,000 for property damage liability
  • Reminder: Defense costs are covered in addition to the policy limits

Liability Coverage – Supplementary Payments

  • In addition to the policy limits and legal defense, certain supplementary payments can be paid, including
  • The cost of a bail bond
  • Premiums on appeals bonds
  • Interest accruing after a judgment
  • Loss of earnings ($200/day)
  • Other reasonable expenses

Part A: Liability Coverage Exclusions

  • Intentional injury or damage (e.g., commission of a crime)
  • Property owned or transported (e.g., valuables in car)
  • Property rented, used, or in the insured’s care
  • Bodily injury to an employee (falls under WC)
  • Use as a public livery or conveyance (e.g., Uber)
  • Vehicles used in the auto business (dealer car)
  • Other business vehicles* (defined on next slide)
  • Using a vehicle without a reasonable belief of permission
  • Vehicles with fewer than four wheels (e.g., motorcycles)
  • Exclusion does not apply if the vehicle is being used in a medical emergency or to any non owned golf cart
  • Vehicle furnished for the insured’s regular use (needs to be declared)

Other Business Vehicles Exclusion

  • Maintaining or using any vehicle while that “insured” is employed or otherwise engaged in any “business” (other than farming or ranching) not described in Exclusion 6 (“used in the auto business”).
  • This exclusion does not apply to the maintenance or use of a
  • private passenger auto
  • pickup or van that
  • You own; or
  • You do not own while used as a temporary substitute for “your covered auto”
  • “trailer” used with a vehicle described above.

Part A: Out of State Coverage

  • If an accident occurs in another state, and the financial responsibility law in that state has higher liability limits than shown in the declarations, the PAP automatically provides the higher limits
  • That is, PAP policy automatically adjusts to comply with
  • Financial responsibility law, or
  • No-fault law of another state.

Part A: Other Insurance

  • If two auto policies cover a loss to an owned automobile, each company pays its pro rata share of the loss.
  • If the insured is driving someone else’s car and has an accident, the insured’s policy is excess and the insurance on the borrowed car is primary.

Exhibit 22.1 Primary and Excess Insurance

Checkpoint Question: Liability Coverage

  • Who Is an Insured?
  • The named insured and any resident family member
  • Any person using the named insured’s covered auto
  • Any person or organization legally responsible for any insured’s use of a covered auto on behalf of that person or organization
  • Any person or organization legally responsible for the named insured’s or family members’ use of any auto or trailer (other than a covered auto or one owned by the person or organization)

Physical Damage Coverage

  • Collision
  • Covers damage from collisions and rollovers
  • Other-than-collision (Comprehensive)
  • Covers damage from
  • falling objects, explosions, glass breakage
  • earthquake, windstorms, hail
  • contact with an animal
  • theft, vandalism
  • Deductibles generally used for both

Part B: Medical Payments

  • Medical payments coverage covers all reasonable medical and funeral expenses incurred by an insured in an accident
  • Covers medical services rendered within three years from the date of the accident
  • Coverage is not based on fault

Medical Payments Coverage

  • In tort liability states
  • Optional
  • Limits are generally low (e.g., $1,000 - $10,000)
  • Payments regardless of fault
  • Payments not coordinated with other medical expense insurance
  • This means insured could collect twice

Medical Payments Coverage

  • In “no-fault” states
  • Often compulsory
  • Provided by “Personal injury protection (PIP)” coverage
  • PIP also provides limited loss of income coverage

Definition of Insured: Part B, Medical Payments

  • Two groups are eligible for coverage under Part B
  • The named insured and family members are covered
  • While occupying any motor vehicle, or
  • As pedestrians when struck by a motor vehicle
  • Other persons occupying a covered auto are covered
  • But not covered in a non-owned vehicle

Part B: Medical Payments Exclusions

  • Injuries sustained
  • While occupying a vehicle with fewer than four wheels
  • While operating the vehicle as a public livery or conveyance (e.g., as an Uber driver)
  • When the vehicle is used as a residence
  • When the vehicle is used without a reasonable belief of permission
  • When the vehicle is competing in a race
  • When vehicle is being used for business purposes
  • Example: you sell pies online and deliver them to people’s homes
  • Pizza delivery

Part B: Medical Payments – Other Insurance

  • If more than one auto policy covers a loss
  • The insurer pays its pro rata share of the loss for an owned vehicle
  • The insurance coverage is excess over any other insurance for a non-owned vehicle

Part C: Uninsured Motorists Coverage

  • Pays for bodily injury (and property damage in some states) caused by
  • Coverage for all damages that otherwise would have been paid
  • medical expenses
  • lost income
  • pain and suffering
  • Compulsory in many states
  • Situations when Uninsured Motorist Coverage Applies
  • An uninsured motorist
  • Hit and run driver
  • Negligent driver whose insurance company is insolvent
  • Negligent driver who has insurance, but the amount is less than required by the state’s financial responsibility law

Part C: Uninsured Motorists Coverage – Definition of Insured

  • The coverage applies to
  • The named insured and family members
  • Any other person while occupying a covered auto
  • Any person legally entitled to recover damages (e.g., a surviving spouse)
  • The uninsured motorist must be legally liable
  • NOTE: ~14% of drivers in 2022 (about 1-in-7) were driving without insurance (Insurance Research Council), ranging from 5.9% of drivers in WY to 24.9% in NM (12.3% in SC). Insured drivers in the US pay more than $14 billion each year in uninsured/underinsurance motorist insurance premiums to cover this risk!

Part C: Uninsured/Underinsured Motorists Coverage

  • Coverage does not apply when
  • An insured is injured in, or by, a vehicle owned by the named insured, but not insured under the policy
  • There is primary coverage under another policy
  • The vehicle is used as a public livery or conveyance (e.g., taxi, Uber)
  • Does not apply to a carpool
  • When workers compensation benefits are applicable (i.e., accident was work-related)

Part C: Uninsured Motorists Coverage

  • There are several limitations when more than one uninsured motorist coverage provision applies to a loss
  • For example, if an insurer provides coverage on a vehicle not owned by the named insured, the insurance provided is excess over any collectible insurance provided on a primary basis

Part C: Uninsured Motorists Coverage

  • Underinsured motorists coverage can be added to the PAP to provide more complete protection
  • In general, the maximum amount paid is the underinsured motorist’s coverage limit stated in the policy less the amount paid by the negligent driver’s insurer
  • Coverage is typically added as an endorsement
  • Some states make it mandatory, while others make it optional

Part D: Coverage for Damage to Your Auto

  • Under the coverage for damage to your auto, the insurer agrees to pay for any direct and accidental loss to a covered auto or any non-owned auto
  • 2 Coverages
  • Collision
  • Comprehensive (aka “other than collision”)

Part D: Insuring Agreement

  • Coverage has two parts
  • Collision: as defined in the contract
  • Comprehensive (Other-than-Collision): for certain listed perils (e.g., weather damage), including theft
  • These are optional coverages
  • Coverage is in force only if the declarations page indicates that the coverage is provided for in the policy
  • Coverage is not required by law, but if you have a loan or lease on the car you will generally be compelled to purchase it (protects the interests of the bank/leasing company)
  • Even if no loan/lease, makes sense to keep in force until vehicle is older (~10+ years old)

Part D: Covered Vehicles

  • Any vehicle listed in the declarations
  • Newly acquired vehicles provided coverage is requested within 14 days of acquisition.
  • In addition, the Part D coverages apply to a non-owned auto and temporary substitute auto.

Collision Definition

  • The upset (i.e., damage) of your covered auto or non-owned auto or its impact with another vehicle or object.
  • Examples
  • Car overturns on an icy street
  • Car hits another car, telephone pole, tree or a building
  • Car is parked and you find the rear fender dented when you return (can’t identify negligent party)
  • You open your door in a parking lot and damage your door by hitting another car’s door
  • Pothole damage

Collision Losses – Paid Regardless of Fault

  • If you cause the loss, your insurer will pay for the damage, less any deductible (typically $500-$1000)
  • If the other driver is at fault, you can either
  • Collect from the negligent driver or his insurer, or
  • Submit the claim to your insurer who will pay the claim (in which case your insurer may subrogate against the negligent party)

Collision Losses – Paid Regardless of Fault (cont.)

  • If your insurer pays the loss, they will assume your subrogation rights.
  • Your insurer will attempt to collect from the negligent party.
  • If they collect the entire amount, they will refund your deductible.

Comprehensive (Other Than Collision Coverage)

  • Comprehensive (Other-than-collision coverage): an other-than-collision loss is a loss due to the following perils (typically)
  • Missiles or falling objects
  • Fire
  • Theft or larceny
  • Explosion or earthquake
  • Windstorm
  • Hail, tornado, water or flood
  • Malicious mischief or vandalism
  • Riot or civil commotion
  • Contact with a bird or animal
  • Glass breakage

Comprehensive Coverage (cont.)

  • Theft includes theft of equipment permanently installed
  • Hub caps
  • Tires
  • Stereo set, Navigation system
  • Air bag
  • If glass breakage is caused by a collision, you can elect to have it covered as a collision loss.
  • In this way, you avoid paying two deductibles
  • Reminder: Colliding with a bird or animal is a comprehensive loss
  • Top 5 States for Colliding with a Deer, Moose or Elk
  • 1—in164 drivers annually collides with one of these (1.5 million collisions/year).
  • Avg. claim costs $4,000.

Part D: Coverage for Damage to Your Auto

  • A non-owned auto is also covered under the Part D coverages
  • A non-owned auto is a private passenger auto, pickup, van, or trailer not owned by or furnished or made available for regular use of the named insured or family member, while it is in the custody of or being operated by the named insured or family member
  • The coverage also applies to a temporary substitute vehicle
  • The policy offers the broadest coverage applicable to any covered auto shown in the declarations

Part D: Coverage for Damage to Your Auto

  • A collision damage waiver (CDW) may be unnecessary on a rental car if you carry collision and comprehensive coverage on your own car (i.e., if you carry collision and comprehensive on your personal vehicle you can generally decline the expensive coverage sold to you by rental car companies)

Part D: Transportation Expenses

  • Part D also pays for temporary transportation expenses, e.g., for train, bus, taxi expenses
  • The expense must be the result of a covered loss
  • Coverage is subject to a daily and total limit
  • Includes charges from a rental car company for loss of daily rental
  • Typically $50 a day for “Loss of Use”
  • Train
  • Taxi
  • Rental car
  • Loss of rental car income if the car is rented

’s Auto Policy

  • Coverage D
  • Transportation and Other

Part D: Exclusions

  • Exclusions to the coverage include
  • Use as a public livery or conveyance
  • Damage from wear and tear, freezing, and mechanical or electrical breakdown
  • Radioactive contamination or war
  • Certain electronic equipment
  • Permanently installed equipment is covered
  • Tapes, records, and disks
  • Government destruction or confiscation
  • Loss to a trailer, camper body, or motor home not shown in the declarations

Part D: Exclusions (cont.)

  • Using a non-owned auto without a reasonable belief of permission
  • Radar detection equipment
  • Custom furnishings or equipment
  • Non-owned auto used in the automobile business
  • Using the vehicle in a racing facility
  • Loss to a rental car if a state law or rental agreement precludes the rental company from recovering from the insured.
  • Note: If you have a heavily customized or antique vehicle, you’ll need to get in touch with your insurer to have the vehicle appraised

Background: Application Questions

  • Fred has a PAP with the following coverages
  • Part A: Liability coverage: $100,000/$300,000/$50,000
  • Part B: Medical payments coverage: $5,000 each person
  • Part C: Uninsured motorists coverage: $25,000 each person
  • Part D: Coverage for Damage to Your Auto
  • Collision loss: $250 deductible
  • Other-than-collision (Comprehensive) loss: $100 deductible
  • With respect to each of the following situations, indicate whether the loss is covered and the amount payable, if any under the policy.
  • Assume that each situation is a separate event.

Application Questions

  • Fred’s son, age 16, is driving a family car, runs a red light and kills a pedestrian. The family of the deceased pedestrian sues and damages are awarded in the amount of $500,000.
