  • Fred borrows a friend’s car to go to the supermarket with permission. He fails to stop at a red light and negligently smashes into another motorist. The other driver’s car, valued at $15,000, is totally destroyed. In addition, repairs to the friend’s car are $5,000.
  • Fred’s daughter, Heather, attends college in another state and drives a family auto. Heather lets her boyfriend drive the car, and he negligently injures another motorist. The boyfriend is sued for $50,000.
  • Fred’s wife is driving the family car in a snowstorm. She loses control of the car on an icy street and smashes into the foundation of a neighbor’s house. The property damage to the house is $30,000. The damage to the family car is $8,000. Fred’s wife has medical expenses of $5,000.

Application Questions (cont.)

  • Fred is walking across the street and is struck by a motorist who fails to stop. He has bodily injuries in the amount of $15,000.
  • Fred’s car is being repaired for faulty brakes. While road testing the car, a mechanic injures another motorist and is sued for $50,000.
  • Fred’s car hits a deer crossing a highway. The cost of repairing the car is $2,500.
  • A thief breaks a car window and steals a camera and golf clubs locked in the car. It will cost $400 to replace the damaged window. The stolen property is valued at $500.
  • Fred’s wife goes shopping at a supermarket. When she returns, she finds that the left rear fender has been damaged by another car who did not leave a name. The cost of repairing the car is $2,000.

Application Questions (cont.)

  • Fred works for a construction company. While driving a large cement truck, he negligently injures another motorist. The injured motorist sues Fred for $25,000.
  • Fred’s son drives a family car on a date. He gets drunk, and his girlfriend drives him home. The girlfriend negligently injures another motorist, who has bodily injuries in the amount of $200,000.
  • A laptop computer valued at $1,000 is stolen from Fred’s car. The car was locked when the theft occurred. [What if the car was unlocked?]
  • While driving a rental golf cart, Fred accidentally injures another golfer with the cart
  • Fred drives his motorcycle and crashes into his neighbor’s house.
  • Fred’s car is stolen from the parking lot where he works.
  • Fred’s son challenges his friends to a race, loses control of the vehicle and crashes the car into a tree, causing $10,000 of damage to the vehicle.

Personal Auto Policy — Coverages & Loss Settlement

Coverage for Towing and Labor Costs

  • Can be added by endorsement
  • Pays towing and labor costs if a covered auto or non-owned auto breaks down.
  • Various amounts available
  • Labor costs include only work done at the place of the breakdown

Part D: Loss Settlement

  • For a total loss, the policy pays the actual cash value less the deductible (though some insurers are now introducing replacement value if car is relatively new)
  • Car must be totaled in first year and <15,000 miles
  • For a partial loss, the policy pays only the amount necessary to repair or replace the damaged property of like kind and quality
  • The car can be repaired with original equipment manufacturer (OEM) or generic parts (though OEM-only options are available at additional cost)
  • The policy includes an appraisal provision for handling disputes over the amount of physical damage loss
  • Either party can demand an appraisal of the loss

Tariffs, Auto Insurance & Politics

  • On Nov. 25, 2024 on Truth Social, President-elect Trump posted
  • “On January 20th...I will sign all necessary documents to charge Mexico and Canada a 25% Tariff on ALL products coming into the United States, and its ridiculous Open Borders." He then added that tariffs against China will be increased by an additional 10%.
  • NOTE: In September 2024, then candidate Donald Trump posted to X the following
  • “Your Automobile Insurance is up 73% — VOTE FOR TRUMP, I’LL CUT THAT NUMBER IN HALF!”
  • Imports of Motor Vehicles and Parts into the US, 2021*
  • Sharp increases in tariffs focused on Mexico, Canada and China will exert pressure on auto insurance premiums
  • *Source:

Part D: Other Insurance

  • If more than one auto policy covers a physical damage loss
  • For a covered auto: Each company pays in proportion to what its limit of liability bears to the total of all applicable limits.
  • For a non-owned auto, any physical damage insurance on the borrowed car is primary and your physical damage insurance is excess.

Part E: Duties After an Accident or Loss

  • After an accident, the insured is required to perform certain duties, such as
  • Promptly notify the insurance company or agent
  • Cooperate with the insurer in the investigation and settlement of a claim
  • Send the insurer copies of any legal notices received in connection with an accident
  • Take a physical exam, if required (IME=Independent Medical Exam)
  • Requiring an IME is often an indication that the insurer expects fraud in the claiming and/or delivery of medical benefits

Part E: Duties After an Accident or Loss (cont.)

  • The police must be notified if a hit-and-run driver is involved
  • The insurer must be allowed to inspect your vehicle if you are seeking coverage under Part D (Collision/Comprehensive)
  • The insurer can deny coverage only if failure to comply is prejudicial to the insurer
  • NOTE: This could include situations in which you deliberately conceal the accident from the insurer (hoping to avoid higher rates) but are subsequently sued by another party. Failure to report the accident promptly is potentially prejudicial to the insurer (i.e., puts them at a legal disadvantage as they had no chance to settle the case before a suit was filed)

Part F: General Provisions – Policy Territory

  • The policy territory for PAP is
  • The United States
  • U.S. territories and possessions
  • Puerto Rico, or
  • Canada
  • PAP policy also covers losses or accidents involving the covered auto while being transported between ports
  • All states restrict the insurer’s right to cancel or non-renew coverage

Part F: Termination Provisions

  • Insured may cancel at any time.
  • The insurer also has the right of cancellation under certain conditions
  • when the policy has been in force for less than 60 days, the insurer must give 10 days’ notice of cancellation, and
  • when the policy has been in force for more than 60 days, the insurer may cancel only if
  • the premium is not paid, or
  • the insured’s driver’s license has been suspended or revoked, or
  • the policy was obtained by a material misrepresentation.

Part F: General Provisions – Policy Period

  • PAP applies only to accidents and losses which occur
  • During the policy period
  • Within the policy territory

Personal Auto – Case Application

  • Kim, age 20, is a college student who recently purchased her first car from a friend. The vehicle is a high mileage, 2010 Toyota Corolla with current market value of $2,000.
  • Assume you are an insurance agent and Kim asks your advice concerning the various coverages in the PAP.
  • Briefly describe the major coverages that are available in the PAP.
  • Which of the coverages should Kim purchase? Justify your answer.
  • Which of the coverages should Kim not purchase? Justify your answer.

Suggested Answers – a

  • The major coverages under the PAP consist of the following
  • Part A—Liability Coverage
  • Part B—Medical Payments Coverage
  • Part C—Uninsured Motorists Coverage
  • Part D—Coverage for Damage to Your Auto

Suggested Answers – b

  • Liability coverage (Part A) is the most important coverage that Kim should buy since it would cover her for legal liability arising out of the negligent operation of an automobile.
  • Since many motorists drive without liability insurance (~14%), the uninsured motorists coverage should also be purchased.
  • Medical payments coverage could be purchased if she consistently drives with other passengers in her car, or she has no individual or group health insurance that would cover her in an accident.

Suggested Answers – c

  • Coverage for damage to your auto (Part D) should not be purchased. Kim is driving an older automobile with a limited market value, which should not be insured for physical damage losses. She can retain the entire exposure.

Case Application - Question d

  • Assume that Kim purchases the PAP coverages that you recommend. To what extent, if any, would Kim’s insurer cover the following situations?
  • Danielle, Kim’s roommate, borrows Kim’s car with her permission and injures another motorist. Danielle is at fault.
  • Kim is texting while driving and is involved in an accident where another motorist is seriously injured.
  • During the football season, Kim charges a fee to transport fans to the football stadium. Several passengers are injured when Kim suddenly changes lanes without signaling and hits another car.
  • Kim drives her boyfriend’s car on a regular basis. While driving her boyfriends’ car, she is involved in an accident in which another motorist is injured.

Case Application - Question d & e

  • Kim rents a car in England where she is a participating in a summer study program. The car is stolen from her dormitory parking lot.
  • Kim also owns a motorcycle. To what extent, if any is the motorcycle covered?

Suggested answers – question d, 1- 4

  • Danielle is covered under Kim’s policy. Kim’s PAP will pay the liability claim up to the policy limits.
  • The loss is covered. However, because texting while driving is a serious offense (illegal in many jurisdictions), Kim’s insurer may refuse to renew her policy or will substantially increase the premium.
  • The loss is not covered. Using a vehicle as a taxi or public livery conveyance is specifically excluded.
  • The loss is not covered under Kim’s policy. The PAP does not cover non-owned vehicles driven on a regular basis or made regularly available for use.

Suggested answers, questions d(5)and e

  • The loss is not covered. The PAP provides coverage only in the United States, its territories and possessions, Puerto Rico, and Canada (note: not Mexico).
  • The PAP does not cover motorcycles. However, coverage can be obtained by adding the miscellaneous-type vehicle endorsement to the policy

Insured – Liability Section - Personal Auto Policy

  • Which of the following situations would be covered under the liability section of the PAP?
  • An attendant at a parking lot is sued by a pedestrian who is injured when the attendant has an accident while parking the covered auto.
  • The daughter of the named insured is sued after she has an accident when a new friend she just met at a campus hangout lets her drive his car.
  • I only
  • II only
  • both I and II
  • neither I nor II
  • Answer
  • B—The car was in the care, custody and control of the parking attendant, hence the liability falls to the attendant’s employer (e.g., restaurant, hotel). Same situation would hold for a valet.

Personal Auto Policy – Coverage D question (Collision/Comprehensive)

  • Bill has a Personal Auto Policy which has both collision and other-than-collision coverage. Which of the following statement(s) is (are) true with regard to his Part D coverage under the PAP?
  • If Bill’s car is stolen on Thursday morning, his cab fare to work on Monday morning of $15 will be covered by the policy.
  • If Bill is at fault in an accident, his insurer will not pay for damage to his car.
  • Collision losses will be settled on a replacement cost basis.
  • Collision coverage and other-than-collision coverage are the most important coverages that Bill can purchase under the PAP.
  • Answer
  • A: Will reimburse for alternative transportation expenses.

Other Insurance – Personal Auto Policy

  • Joyce was injured by an uninsured drunk driver while she was riding in a friend’s car. Joyce and her friend each have a PAP with an uninsured motorists limit of $50,000. How much will be paid by each policy if it is determined that Joyce has $70,000 of bodily injuries?
  • Each policy will pay $35,000.
  • Joyce’s policy will pay $50,000, and the friend’s policy will pay nothing.
  • Joyce’s policy will pay $50,000, and the friend’s policy will pay $20,000.
  • The friend’s policy will pay $50,000, and Joyce’s policy will pay $20,000.
  • Answer
  • D—Remember: Insurance follows the car! And the car involved in the accident is the primary coverage!

Covered Autos – Liability – Personal Auto Policy

  • All of the following are covered autos under the liability section of the PAP EXCEPT
  • a non-owned van which is driven by the insured on a regular basis.
  • a trailer owned by the named insured.
  • a borrowed or rented auto used by the insured as a substitute for a stolen or damaged covered auto.
  • a newly acquired auto which replaces a vehicle previously described in the policy.
  • Answer
  • A—Remember: Any vehicle that is regularly driven by the insured must be included on the policy!

Personal Auto Policy Claims Question

  • Donna has a PAP with liability limits of $25/50/10, medical payments coverage of $5,000 per person, and other-than-collision (comprehensive) coverage with a $100 deductible. Donna fell asleep while driving late at night. She crossed the center line and hit a car approaching from the other direction. The following losses occurred
  • The driver of the other car suffered $30,000 in bodily injuries.
  • Donna’s car sustained $5,000 in damages.
  • Donna incurred $10,000 in medical expenses.
  • The car that Donna hit was a total loss. The claims adjustment value was $25,000.
  • Because of her injury, Donna was unable to work and lost $5,000 in wages.
  • How much will Donna’s insurer pay?
  • Answer
  • $25,000 (BI per person) + 0 (collision) + $5,000 (Med pay) + $10,000 (PD Liability limit) + 0 (lost wages) = $40,000

Personal Auto Policy Claims Question

  • Donna has a PAP with liability limits of $12.5/25/7.5, uninsured motorist coverage of $12,500, medical payments coverage of $5,000 per person, and other-than-collision coverage with a $100 deductible. Donna was driving her covered auto. She negligently crossed the center line and hit another car approaching from the other direction.
  • The following losses occurred
  • The other car was a total loss. The claims adjustment value of the other car was $25,000.
  • The driver of the other car suffered $15,000 in bodily injuries.
  • Two passengers in the other car who were also injured. One suffered $20,000 in bodily injuries; the other passenger’s bodily injuries totaled $15,000.
  • Donna was uninjured. However, her passenger suffered bodily injuries which resulted in $10,000 in medical expenses.
  • Donna’s car was damaged in the collision. The cost to repair her car was $13,000.

Answer

  • How much will Donna’s insurer pay? Please provide your rationale and show your calculations.
  • Answer Hints
  • First, Donna is liable. She negligently crossed the center line.
  • Therefore, she is legally liable for
  • Property damage to the other car and
  • Bodily injuries to the drive and passengers of the other car
  • Assume you are the claims adjuster assigned to this claim. How much will your company pay out in total. Determine by ascertaining how much will be paid across each coverage.
  • Super Big Hint: The Personal Auto Policy will pay a total of $37,500.
  • Use the table on the next slide to help determine your answer.

Answer (cont.)

  • Type of Loss — Amount Paid by Insurance
  • Property damage to the other car — $
  • Bodily injury to Driver, Other Car — $
  • Bodily injury to Passenger A, Other Car — $
  • Bodily injury to Passenger B, Other Car — $
  • Donna’s passenger — $
  • Collision — $
  • Total — $37,500

Answer (cont.)

  • Type of Loss — Amount Paid by Insurance
  • Property damage to the other car — $7,500 (PD liability limit)
  • Bodily injury to Driver, Other Car — 12,500 (BI per person limit)
  • Bodily injury to Passenger A, Other Car — $12,500 (BI per person limit)
  • Bodily injury to Passenger B, Other Car — $0 (BI per accident limit- exhausted)
  • Donna’s passenger — $ 5,000 (Med pay limit)
  • Collision — $0 (No Collision coverage)
  • Total — $37,500

Financial Responsibility Laws in Auto Insurance

  • In general a driver demonstrates financial responsibility by
  • Having an insurance policy whose liability limits comply with the law
  • posting a bond, or
  • depositing collateral.

Compulsory Insurance Laws

  • A compulsory insurance law requires motorists to carry at least a minimum amount of liability insurance before the vehicle can be licensed or registered
  • Major advantage: An innocent accident victim has some protection against an irresponsible driver.

Compulsory Insurance Laws

  • Some argue that the law provides greater protection against uninsured drivers because motorists must provide evidence of financial responsibility before an accident occurs
  • Critics cite: mandatory insurance does not reduce the number of uninsured drivers
  • There is no correlation between compulsory insurance laws and the number of uninsured vehicles on the highway
  • Computer reporting systems to track uninsured motorists have not been effective

Compulsory Insurance Laws: Disadvantages

  • Such laws do not compel drivers to buy and keep insurance over the long term
  • many motorists continue to drive without insurance
  • claim frequency and loss costs associated with financially irresponsible drivers continue to increase in those states requiring such insurance; and
  • injury loss experience connected to drivers without liability insurance is on average worse in compulsory states than in noncompulsory states.

Economic Rationale for Compulsory Auto Insurance

  • Auto Liability Insurance Is Compulsory: WHY?
  • Innocent victims have means of recovery
  • Without it, accident costs will not be borne by those who cause accidents
  • Uninsured are not bearing the full cost of their driving and should therefore be compelled to do so.
  • Uninsured are not bearing the full cost of decisions to drive less safely
  • The uninsured would have a greater incentive to drive more safely if forced to purchase insurance with experience rating (recall what happens when an insurer rates based on risk)
  • Driving is not a right, it’s a privilege with commensurate responsibilities

Criticisms of Compulsory Insurance

  • It’s Regressive
  • i.e., it disproportionately hurts low income people (auto insurance accounts for a higher share of their income)
  • Forces them to buy insurance to protect other people
  • Weak Enforcement
  • Police often don’t enforce mandatory insurance laws
  • This is one reason why about 13% of drivers on the road are uninsured

Key Personal Rating Factors in Auto Insurance

  • Driving Experience
  • Gender
  • Marital Status
  • Territory
  • Miles Driven
  • Number of Drivers in Household
  • Garaged vs. Non-Garaged
  • Credit Standing (except in CA, HI and MA)
  • Education (where permitted)
  • Occupation (where permitted)
  • Increasingly, Telematics-based options are available that evaluate your driving in real-time

How Certain Rating Factors Are Related to Specific Driving Behaviors

  • Source:

How Certain Rating Factors Are Related to Specific Driving Behaviors

  • Source:

Key Rating Factors in Auto Insurance

  • What if Someone Cannot Obtain Insurance?
  • Residual Markets: All states have a “residual market” that allows drivers who might otherwise have difficulty obtaining insurance find coverage at a regulated price.
  • While some states’ residual markets were once large, most today are tiny and some have no drivers at all in them (i.e., they have been depopulated)
  • There was just 1 driver (out of 3,553,729) in SC’s residual market in 2013 (latest available)
  • National residual market share = 1% in 2013
  • Only NC has a significant share of its drivers in residual markets (23.5% in 2013).

Future Issues in Auto Insurance

  • Autonomous Vehicles – What will happen as autonomous features become more common on vehicles?
  • Today: 94% accidents are driver error
  • Future: Man vs. Machine—assigning and apportioning fault could actually become more difficult! (e.g., Tesla and Uber cases)
  • Telematics – Tracking your actual driving behavior (black boxes, apps) and assessing price accordingly
  • Opt in/out; Privacy concerns
  • Vehicle Sharing – Will people “subscribe” to auto-on-demand services?
  • Will insurance be purchased individually or as part of the subscription price
  • Tort Law – Applies to when a contract does NOT govern the relationship between the parties in a dispute

Background: Application Questions for Personal Auto Insurance

  • Fred has a PAP with the following coverages
  • Part A: Liability coverage: $100,000/$300,000/$50,000
  • Interpret: $100K per person Bodily Injury (BI); Up to $300K per accident for BI; $50K for Property Damage Liability
  • Part B: Medical payments coverage: $5,000 each person
  • Part C: Uninsured motorists coverage: $25,000 each person
  • Part D: Coverage for Damage to Your Auto
  • Collision loss: $250 deductible
  • Other-than-collision loss: $100 deductible
  • With respect to each of the following situations, indicate whether the loss is covered and the amount payable, if any under the policy.
  • Assume that each situation is a separate event.

Application Questions

  • Fred’s son, age 16, is driving a family car, runs a red light and kills a pedestrian. The family of the deceased pedestrian sues and damages are awarded in the amount of $500,000.
  • Fred borrows a friend’s car to go to the supermarket with permission. He fails to stop at a red light and negligently smashes into another motorist. The other driver’s car, valued at $15,000, is totally destroyed. In addition, repairs to the friend’s car are $5,000.
  • Fred’s daughter, Heather, attends college in another state and drives a family auto. Heather lets her boyfriend drive the car, and he negligently injures another motorist. The boyfriend is sued for $50,000.
  • Fred’s wife is driving the family car in a snowstorm. She loses control of the car on an icy street and smashes into the foundation of a neighbor’s house. The property damage to the house is $30,000. The damage to the family car is $8,000. Fred’s wife has medical expenses of $5,000.

Application Questions (cont.)

  • Fred is walking across the street and is struck by a motorist who fails to stop. He has bodily injuries in the amount of $15,000.
  • Fred’s car is being repaired for faulty brakes. While road testing the car, a mechanic injures another motorist and is sued for $50,000.
  • Fred’s car hits a deer crossing a highway. The cost of repairing the car is $2,500.
  • A thief breaks a car window and steals a camera and golf clubs locked in the car. It will cost $400 to replace the damaged window. The stolen property is valued at $500.
  • Fred’s wife goes shopping at a supermarket. When she returns, she finds that the left rear fender has been damaged by another car who did not leave a name. The cost of repairing the car is $2,000.

Application Questions (cont.)

  • Fred works for a construction company. While driving a large cement truck, he negligently injures another motorist. The injured motorist sues Fred for $25,000.
  • Fred’s son drives a family car on a date. He gets drunk, and his girlfriend drives him home. The girlfriend negligently injures another motorist, who has bodily injuries in the amount of $200,000.
  • A laptop computer valued at $1,000 is stolen from Fred’s car. The car was locked when the theft occurred. [What if the car was unlocked?]
  • While driving a rental golf cart, Fred accidentally injures another golfer with the cart
  • Fred drives his motorcycle and crashes into his neighbor’s house.
  • Fred’s car is stolen from the parking lot where he works.
  • Fred’s son challenges his friends to a race, loses control of the vehicle and crashes the car into a tree, causing $10,000 of damage to the vehicle.

Commercial InsuranceInsurance for Businesses(Corresponds to Chapter 26 in )

Major Types of Commercial Property and Casualty Insurance

  • First-party coverage for
  • Property Damage (e.g., commercial property insurance)
  • Loss of Income (e.g., business interruption coverage)
  • Liability and related coverage for injury to third-parties
  • E.g., Commercial liability insurance; Workers Compensation
  • Multiple peril contracts (cover property and liability exposures in a single contract)
  • Commercial Multi-Peril Insurance
  • Surety bonds and financial guarantees
  • Performance guarantees
  • Surety / guarantor has recourse against principal

Surety Bonds

  • A surety bond is a bond that usually provides monetary compensation if the bonded party fails to perform certain promised acts
  • The parties to a surety bond include
  • The principal agrees to perform certain acts or fulfill certain obligations
  • The obligee receives the proceeds of the bond if the principal fails to perform
  • The surety agrees to answer for the debt, default, or obligation of the principal
  • Surety bonds are similar to insurance contracts in that both provide protection against specified losses
  • Example: wants to build a new dormitory. It puts a contract out to bid but wants a guarantee the work will be completed. To bid, all contractors but be “bonded” meaning they’ve secured a surety bond from an insurer guaranteeing their performance. The winning contractor is the principal, is the obligee and the surety provides the guarantee (an insurer)

Policy Standardization

  • Insurance Services Office (ISO) (now known as Verisk)
  • Standardized Auto, Home, Commercial policies
  • National Council on Compensation Insurance (NCCI)
  • Workers Compensation
  • Standard forms are designed to meet the basic needs of a broad class of buyers
  • Customization through endorsements or “Manuscript” policies
  • Manuscript: Used for larger, complex insurance situations
  • Standardization facilitates price and service comparisons

Designing / Negotiating Commercial Insurance Programs

  • Risk managers, consultants, insurance brokers involved
  • Major selection criteria for choosing an insurer
  • Present value of expected cost
  • Risk
  • Specialized coverages and endorsements
  • Quality of loss control, claims settlement, other services
  • Insurer’s reputation and financial strength

General Liability Loss Exposures

  • General Liability: refers to legal liability arising out of business operations other than auto (falls under Commercial Auto) or aviation (falls under Aviation insurance) accidents and employee injuries (falls under Workers Comp)
  • A commercial firm typically purchases a commercial general liability (CGL) policy or a businessowners policy (BOP) to cover its general liability loss exposures

General Liability Loss Exposures (Continued)

  • Some important liability exposures include
  • Premises and operations liability: arising out of the ownership and maintenance of the premises where the firm does business
  • Products liability: arising out of the manufacturing and sale of products
  • Completed operations liability: arising out of faulty work performed away from the premises after the work or operation is completed
  • Contractual liability: arising out of the assumption of legal liability through a written or oral contract
  • Contingent liability: arising out of work done by independent contractors

Commercial General Liability Policy

  • The commercial general liability policy (CGL) is widely used by firms to cover their general liability loss exposures
  • An occurrence policy covers liability claims arising out of occurrences that take place during the policy period
  • A claims-made policy covers only claims that are first reported during the policy period or extended reporting period (grace period for reporting claims), provided that the event occurred after the retroactive date, if any, stated in the policy
  • The CGL policy can be written alone or included in a commercial package policy

Commercial General Liability Policy (Continued)

  • Section I includes Coverages A–C
  • Coverage A: Bodily Injury and Property Damage_Liability
  • The insurer agrees to pay on behalf of the insured all sums up to the policy limits that the insured is legally obligated to pay because of bodily injury or property damage
  • The bodily injury or property damage must be caused by an occurrence, i.e., an accident, including continuous or repeated exposure to substantially the same general harmful conditions

Commercial General Liability Policy (Continued)

  • Coverage A contains a lengthy list of exclusions, including
  • Expected or intended injuries or damages
  • Contractual liability
  • Liquor liability
  • Workers compensation
  • Employers liability
  • Pollution
  • Aircraft, auto, and watercraft
  • Mobile equipment
  • (There are separate policies that deal with most of these risks)

Commercial General Liability Policy (Continued)

  • Damage to property owned, rented or occupied by the insured
  • Damage to the insured’s product
  • Damage to the insured’s work
  • Damage to impaired property
  • Recall of products
  • Personal and advertising injury
  • Electronic data
  • Distribution of material in violation of statutes
  • (There are separate policies that deal with most of these risks)

Commercial General Liability Policy (Continued)

  • Coverage A includes defense costs, and the insurer has the right to investigate a claim or suit and settle it at its discretion
  • Coverage A includes fire legal liability coverage
  • Covers liability for fire damage to premises rented to the named insured or temporarily occupied by the named insured with the permission of the owner
  • A separate limit of coverage applies
  • Other damage to property rented by the insured is NOT covered

Commercial General Liability Policy (Continued)

  • Coverage B: Personal and Advertising Liability
  • The insurer agrees to pay those sums that the insured is legally liable to pay as damages because of personal and advertising injury
  • The policy covers legal liability resulting from
  • False arrest - Malicious prosecution
  • Slander - Wrongful eviction or entry
  • Violation of privacy - Copyright infringement

Commercial General Liability Policy (Continued)

  • Coverage C: Medical Payments
  • The insurer will cover the medical expenses of persons who are injured in an accident on the premises or on ways next to the premises (e.g., parking lot), or as a result of the insured’s operations
  • Expenses must be incurred within one year of the accident
  • Payments are made without regard to legal liability

Commercial General Liability Policy (Continued)

  • Certain supplementary payments are included under Coverages A and B
  • All expenses incurred by the insurer
  • Up to $250 for the cost of a bail bond
  • Up to $250/day for actual loss of earnings by the insured
  • Cost of bonds to release attachments
  • Actual loss of earnings by the insured up to $250/day
  • Court costs levied against the insured
  • Pre- and post-judgment interest

Commercial General Liability Policy (Continued)

  • Section II of the CGL defines the “insureds”
  • If designated in the declarations, insureds include
  • Owner and spouse if a sole proprietorship
  • Partners, members, and their spouses if a partnership or joint venture
  • Members and managers if a limited liability company (LLC)
  • Officers, directors, and stockholders if a corporation
  • A trust and trustees, but only with respect to their duties as trustees

Commercial General Liability Policy (Continued)

  • Insureds also include persons not named in the policy
  • Volunteer workers acting for the organization
  • Employees acting within the scope of employment
  • Any person or organization acting as a real estate manager
  • A legal representative if the named insured should die
  • Any newly acquired or formed organization, other than a partnership, joint venture or LLC

Commercial General Liability Policy (Continued)

  • Section III of the CGL contains the coverage limits
  • The general aggregate limit is the maximum amount that the insurer will pay for the sum of the following
  • Damages under Coverage A (except for amounts paid for products/completed operations hazard), damages under Coverage B, and medical payments under Coverage C
  • The policy contains a separate products/completed operations aggregate limit

Commercial General Liability Policy (Continued)

  • A personal and advertising injury limit is the maximum paid under Coverage B
  • An each-occurrence limit is the maximum amount the insurer will pay for the sum of damages under Coverage A and medical expenses under Coverage C arising out of any one occurrence
  • The damage to rented premises limit is the maximum amount paid for damages under Coverage A due to a single fire
  • The medical expense limit is the maximum amount paid under Coverage C because of a bodily injury sustained by any one person

Exhibit 26.1 Illustration of the CGL Limits of Insurance

Commercial General Liability Policy (Continued)

  • Section IV of the CGL states the various conditions that apply to the coverage form
  • For example, this section contains provisions for dealing with bankruptcy, and the duties in the event of an occurrence
  • Section V of the CGL contains the definitions of various terms used in the policy
  • Some terms defined in the policy include “advertisement,” “hostile fire,” and “volunteer worker”

Commercial General Liability Policy (Continued)

  • The ISO claims-made policy is similar to the occurrence policy with the major exceptions of
  • Payment of claims is on a claims-made basis
  • The policy contains an extended reporting period provision
  • Under a claims-made policy, premiums, losses, and loss reserves can be estimated with greater accuracy
  • A policy can include a retroactive date

Employment-Related Practices Liability Insurance

  • Under the ISO employment-related practices liability insurance coverage, the insurer agrees to pay damages resulting from a “wrongful act” arising out of
  • Demotion or failure to promote
  • Termination
  • Negligent hiring or supervision
  • Retaliatory action against employees
  • Coercing an employee to commit an unlawful act
  • Work-related harassment
  • Employment-related libel
  • Other work-related abuse

Employment-Related Practices Liability Insurance (Continued)

  • The ISO form provides for a legal defense
  • Exclusions include
  • criminal acts
  • contractual liability
  • workers compensation
  • violation of laws applicable to employers, such as the Age Discrimination in Employment Act
  • Strikes and lockouts of employees
  • Prior or pending litigation
  • Prior notice
  • Interest in this coverage is increasing due to an increase in suits against employers for sexual harassment

Workers Compensation Insurance

  • Workers Compensation Insurance provides medical care, cash benefits, survivor benefits, and rehabilitation services to workers who are injured or die from job-related accidents or disease
  • Benefits are paid on the principle of liability without fault: the employer is held absolutely liable for job-related accidents and diseases regardless of fault
  • Medical Benefits: Cost of treatments, rehab, etc.
  • Indemnity Benefits: Lost wages paid to injured worker

Workers Compensation Insurance (Continued)

  • Under Part One, the insurer agrees to pay all workers compensation benefits and other benefits that the employer must legally provide to covered employees who have a job-related injury or an occupational disease
  • There are no policy limits. The insurer pays all benefits required by state law
  • The employer must reimburse the insurer for any payments that exceed regular benefits in certain cases, e.g., willful misconduct by the employer

Workers Compensation Insurance (Continued)

  • Under Part Two, the insurer agrees to pay those sums that the insured is legally liable to pay as damages for worker injuries that are not compensable under Part One, such as
  • In some states, workers compensation is not required for smaller employers
  • An employee may sue for injury caused by an accident at the job site, but the injury is not considered work related
  • Some states allow spouses to sue for loss of consortium
  • Some employers are confronted with lawsuits because of third-party-over cases

Workers Compensation Insurance (Continued)

  • Some of the major exclusions in the employers liability section include
  • Liability assumed under contract
  • Punitive damages because of a bodily injury to an employee who is hired in violation of the law
  • Intentional bodily injury caused by the employers
  • Damages resulting from coercion, demotion, evaluation, reassignment, harassment, discrimination, or termination of any employee
  • Fines or penalties because of violation of federal or state law

Workers Compensation Insurance (Continued)

  • Part Three provides other-states insurance
  • The employer is covered for workers compensation claims arising in states not listed on the information page (declarations page)
  • Part One covers claims arising in any states listed on the information page
  • Other-states insurance applies only if one or more states are listed on the information page

Commercial Auto Insurance

  • The ISO business auto coverage form is used to insure commercial auto exposures
  • There are 10 numerical classifications or “symbols” for covered autos (e.g., cars, delivery vehicles, trucks of various sizes, etc.)
  • Coverage of newly acquired autos depends on the symbols selected
  • The form includes liability and physical damage coverage
  • It includes limited liability for pollution losses
  • Options for physical damage coverage include comprehensive, specified cause-of-loss, and collision
  • Coverage for towing and labor costs can be added

Commercial Auto Insurance (Continued)

  • The auto dealers coverage form is a specialized form for auto dealers
  • Under the liability coverage for “covered autos,” the insurer agrees to pay up to the limit of liability shown in the declarations for accidents resulting from the ownership, maintenance, or use of covered autos
  • Covered autos coverage includes garagekeepers coverage to cover accidents in the course of garage operations
  • The terms of coverage for general liability are similar to those used in the CGL
  • The policy protects auto dealers against a range of claims for any “act, error, or omission” arising out of their operations

Aircraft Insurance

  • Most states apply the common-law rules of negligence to aviation accidents
  • Some states have absolute or strict liability laws that hold the owners or operators of aircraft absolutely liable for aviation accidents
  • Absolute liability is imposed on commercial airlines for aviation accidents that occur during international flights
  • Aircraft insurance is highly specialized coverage and is underwritten by a small number of insurer organizations
  • Major airlines generally buy a minimum of $2 billion of liability coverage on their jets

Aircraft Insurance for Private Business and Pleasure Aircraft

  • Aircraft insurers offer policies for private business and pleasure aircraft
  • Policies include coverage for property damage and bodily injury arising out of the ownership or use of the insured aircraft, medical expense coverage, and physical damage coverage for damage to the aircraft
  • Liability coverages include
  • Bodily injury liability excluding passengers
  • Passenger bodily injury liability
  • Property damage liability

Aircraft Insurance for Private Business and Pleasure Aircraft (Continued)

  • Physical damage insurance provides coverage for direct damage to the aircraft
  • Three forms are available
  • “All-risks” basis
  • “All-risks” basis, not in flight
  • “All-risks” basis, not in motion
  • The medical expense coverage pays reasonable medical expenses for passenger injuries
  • Crew members are generally excluded

Commercial Umbrella Policy

  • A commercial umbrella policy can protect a business against catastrophic liability judgments
  • The ISO commercial umbrella policy pays the ultimate net loss in excess of the retained limit for bodily injury, property damage, and personal and advertising injury to which the insurance applies
  • The ultimate net loss is the total sum the insured is legally obligated to pay as damages
  • The retained limit refers to (1) the available limits of underlying insurance listed in the declarations, or (2) the self-insurance retention, whichever applies

Commercial Umbrella Policy (Continued)

  • Insureds are required to carry certain minimum amounts of liability coverage before the umbrella insurer will pay any claims
  • The form contains a lengthy list of exclusions for bodily injury and property damage liability, including
  • Expected or intentional injury
  • Liquor liability
  • Liability arising out of professional services
  • The form also contains a list of exclusions for personal injury and advertising liability

Cyber Liability Insurance

  • Cyber liability insurance covers damages arising from the failure of a data holder to protect private information from being accessed by an unauthorized party
  • Two coverage approaches are currently being used
  • A stand-alone cyber liability form
  • A cyber liability endorsement
  • Coverage can be purchased for liability arising from a data breach, the cost of responding to the breach, and fines and penalties owed as a result of the breach

Businessowners Policy

  • The ISO businessowners policy (BOP) includes liability coverage that is similar to the CGL form
  • The BOP coverage includes
  • Business liability
  • Medical expenses
  • Legal defense
  • Although the BOP excludes professional liability, some professional liability endorsements are available for certain businesses

Professional Liability Insurance

  • Professional liability insurance is available for certain business professionals to provide protection against a lawsuit involving a substantial error or omission
  • The physicians, surgeons, and dentists liability coverage form covers these professionals for acts of malpractice or omission resulting in harm or injury to patients
  • The injury must result from a medical incident
  • The policy also covers the physician for liability arising out of negligent acts of an employee
  • Current forms permit the insurer to settle a claim without the physician’s or surgeon’s consent

Professional Liability Insurance (Continued)

  • Errors and omissions (E&O) insurance provides protection against loss incurred by a client because of negligent acts, errors, or omissions by the insured
  • This coverage is purchased by professionals who provide advice to clients, such as
  • insurance agents and brokers
  • travel agents
  • real estate agents
  • stockbrokers
  • attorneys
  • consultants
  • engineers, and architects

Professional Liability Insurance (Continued)

  • E&O policies are generally issued on a claims-made basis, covering only claims made in the current policy period
  • Claims that result from dishonest, fraudulent, criminal or malicious acts by the insured are specifically excluded

Directors and Officers Insurance

  • A directors and officers (D&O) liability policy provides financial protection for the directors and officers and the corporation if they are sued for mismanagement of the company’s affairs
  • Typically, the policy agrees to pay damages on behalf of directors, officers, and employees because of a wrongful act
  • D&O policies are written on a claims-made basis

Directors and Officers Insurance (Continued)

  • D&O policies generally include
  • Payment of damages on behalf of directors and officers because of a wrongful act
  • Reimbursement for losses resulting from the company's obligation to reimburse directors and officers for suits alleging wrongful acts
  • Entity coverage, which covers the corporation if it is named as a defendant in a suit against the directors and officers
  • Common exclusions include bodily injury and property damage, libel and slander, personal profit, and illegal discrimination
Life & Health Insurance

Life & Health Insurance Overview

Life & Health InsuranceProtecting the Value of Human Life

Life/Health Insurance: 3 Types of Exposures

  • Mortality Risk – Possibility that one’s death causes negative financial consequences for others
  • Longevity Risk – Possibility of outliving one’s financial resources
  • Morbidity Risk – Possibility that injury, disease or incapacity creates unacceptable financial consequences
  • Health Insurance
  • Disability Income Insurance
  • Long-Term Care Insurance

Three Categories of Insurance within Our Broad Definition

  • Classical/Tradition Life Insurance: which pays on benefits to named beneficiaries on the death of the insured
  • Annuities & Pensions: Insurance which pays while an insured is still alive
  • Health & Disability: Pays in the event of loss of health, physical (or mental) disability or incapacity: Medical Expense Insurance, Disability Income, Long Term Care, Credit Life/Health, “Dread” Disease Coverage

Life Insurance Markets: How Markets Are Made

Life/Health Insurance Markets

  • In 2014, there were 895 life/annuity insurers in the US and 857 health insurers.
  • Life markets are therefore very competitive
  • Health insurance markets are much less competitive and consolidation is ongoing
  • Virtually all markets, including life insurance markets suffer from imperfections. In insurance, informational asymmetry problems are among the most consequential and must be constantly monitored and managed
  • Buyer ignorance
  • Adverse selection
  • Moral hazard

Managing Adverse Selection

  • Applicants for insurance have an incentive to conceal and misrepresent the true risk that they pose in order to secure lower rates. This is why insurers often ask many questions of insurance applicants and conduct other types of inquiries. (e.g., medical tests, background checks)
  • A central purpose of insurance underwriting is to deter and detect adverse selection. Life insurers may
  • Ask many questions about health status but still require some form of medical testing (e.g., BP, blood tests, cardio evaluation).
  • Life insurance policies typically exclude suicide for the first two years to deter suicidal individuals from purchasing life insurance
  • Law allows insurers to rescind policy if policyholder materially misrepresented themselves

Managing Moral Hazard

  • Moral Hazard: Occurs when the presence of insurance changes loss prevention behavior. In effect, the insured becomes less risk averse. Not as big of an issue in life insurance markets as in p/c markets. People usually don’t become more willing to die because they’ve purchased life insurance
  • Homeowners Insurance Example: Homeowner fails to spend money necessary to fix defective wiring, reasoning that if the home burns down then it’s covered by insurance
  • But…insurers do want some assurance that you’re worth more alive than dead
  • Require beneficiary to have some logical relationship to policyholder, known as an “insurable interest”
  • Spouses
  • Parents with dependent children
  • Businesses and key executives (“Key Man” insurance)

Determinants of Life Insurance Consumption: Drivers of Demand for Insurance

Factors Influencing Life Insurance Demand: Price

  • Price: As the price of any product or services rises, demand falls. The same is true for insurance.
  • Various studies estimate that demand for life insurance is at least somewhat inelastic

Factors Influencing Life insurance Demand: Income & Wealth, Inflation

  • Income & Wealth: The higher an economy’s income, the more it spends on all types of insurance (same at the level of the individual household)
  • Likewise for education
  • Inflation: Usually detrimental to insurance demand. During such times consumers favor shorter term, more liquid investments as consumers tend to perceive traditional cash value insurance products as long-term, fixed investments and so demand shrinks. On the supply side, inflation causes uncertainty for insurers in terms of long-term planning and investments

Factors Influencing Life insurance Demand: Demographic, Social Factors

  • Demographics: Many demographic factors influence demand for life insurance
  • Marriage (+)
  • Age of Marriage (-)
  • Aging Population (+)
  • Urbanization and Industrialization (+)
  • Education (+)
  • Social Factors: Sales are higher in countries where high personal savings rates are the cultural norm (many parts of Asia); Lower in many predominantly Muslim countries, where insurance can be viewed as violating Koran’s prohibition on payment of interest and on gambling.

Means of Dealing with Financial Consequences of Death

  • Untimely death can result in enormous financial distress for families (and sometimes businesses after death of key employee)
  • To avoid adverse financial consequences of death, resources must come from one or more of these sources
  • Relatives
  • Savings/Investments
  • Employer-provided death benefits
  • Government-provided death benefits
  • Individual life insurance

Mortality-Based Policies: 2 Classes

  • Life Insurance: Pays a death benefit on the death of the insured
  • Annuity Contracts: Policies that promise to make payments through the systematic liquidation of principal and interest for a fixed period or over the annuitant’s lifetime (possibly also a “survivor benefit”)
  • Focus now on Life Insurance

Life Insurance: 2 Categories

  • Term Life Insurance: Life insurance that provides a specific amount of coverage for a designated period of time
  • Example: $100,000 of coverage for 1,3,5,10 or 20 years (so long as you pay the premium each year)
  • Cash Value: Life insurance that provides coverage on the life of the insured individual and also accumulates cash value

Term Life Insurance MarketsQuick Overview

Cash Value Life Insurance TypesQuick Overview

3 Main Types of Cash Value Policies

  • Whole Life: Life insurance that provides guaranteed death benefits, guaranteed fixed premiums and guaranteed cash value accumulation throughout the life of the policy
  • Universal Life: A type of policy that offers the policyholder a flexible premium structure (can increase or decrease what you pay in premiums), current interest rates paid on cash values and a flexible death benefit
  • Variable Life: A type of policy that permits the policyholder to allocate how their premiums are invested
  • For this reason VL is regulated as a security

Cash Value Policies

  • Thousands of variations of cash value policies exist
  • Almost all fall into one of three categories
  • Whole Life
  • Limited Payment
  • Single Premium
  • Universal Life
  • Endowment Insurance

Whole Life Insurance

  • “Ordinary” Whole Life Insurance: is a cash value policy that provides lifetime protection
  • Typically requires payment of fixed premium or premium will terminate
  • A stated amount is paid to a designated beneficiary when the insured dies, regardless of when the death occurs
  • A share of the premium goes toward a death benefit, a share goes towards the cash value (savings component of the plan)

Initially the cash value of the policy is low and the death benefit (net amount at risk) is high

  • Over time, the cash value of the policy builds and the net amount at risk declines
  • The NAR is the death benefit that would have to be paid by the insurer to reach the face value of the policy
  • AGE

Universal Life Insurance

  • Universal Life Insurance: UL a flexible premium policy that provides lifetime protection
  • After the first premium, the policyholder decides the amount and frequency of payments
  • Most policies have a target premium
  • But, the policyowner is not obligated to pay it

Universal Life Cash Value

  • Universal Life cash values do not follow a fixed schedule
  • Cash values vary with
  • policyholder’s premium payments
  • insurer’s expense and mortality charges
  • rate insurer uses to credit interest to cash value
  • Minimum interest rate usually guaranteed
  • rate often linked to short term interest rates
  • can therefore change during policy period

Advantages of Universal Life Insurance

  • Universal life provides considerable flexibility
  • In addition to flexible premiums, cash withdrawals are permitted
  • Policies receive favorable federal income tax treatment
  • Interest/investment income earned accumulates on a tax-deferred basis (paid when withdrawals made)

Variable Life Insurance

  • Variable Life Insurance: is a fixed-premium policy in which the death benefit and cash values vary according to the investment experience of a separate account maintained by the insurer
  • The premium is level
  • Policyholder decides how funds are invested
  • If the investment experience is favorable, the face amount of insurance is increased
  • Although the insurer bears the risk of excessive mortality and expenses, the policyholder bears the risk of poor investment results (because they decide how funds are invested)

Variable Life Insurance:Investment Feature

  • Entire policy reserve is held in a separate account
  • Policy owner has the option to invest the cash value in a variety of investments, such as
  • Common stock fund
  • Bond fund
  • Balanced fund
  • Money market fund
  • International fund

Life Insurance Products & Annuities

Life Insurance Retirement ProductsFocus on Annuities

Annuity Basics

  • Annuity: A periodic payment that continues for a fixed period or for the duration of a designated life or lives.
  • Annuitant: person who receives annuity payments
  • In some sense, and annuity is the opposite of life insurance
  • Life insurance protects against mortality risk—risk of dying too soon
  • Annuity protects against longevity risk—risk of outliving retirement assets
  • What’s being insured and diversified away through pooling is the risk of living too long (rather than the risk of early death)
  • Fundamental Purpose of an Annuity: To provide lifetime income that cannot be outlived
  • Protects against the loss of income due to excessive longevity and the exhaustion of savings that results
  • Annuity Calculator:

Types of Annuities

  • Fixed Immediate Annuity
  • Deferred Annuity
  • Joint Life Annuity
  • Variable Annuity
  • Equity-Indexed Annuity
  • Longevity Annuity (Longevity Insurance)

Types of Annuities

  • Purchase Options for Fixed Deferred Annuities
  • Single Premium Deferred Annuity: Purchased with a lump sum, but income deferred until some later date
  • Flexible Premium Annuity: Annuity owner can vary premium payments; No requirement that a specified amount be deposited each year. Gives annuity owner considerable flexibility.
  • Longevity Annuity (Longevity Insurance): A single premium deferred annuity that begins paying at an advanced age (e.g., 85). Protects against risk of asset depletion at an advanced age

Types of Annuities

  • Fixed Immediate Annuity: Pays periodic payments (e.g., monthly or annually) that are guaranteed and fixed in amount
  • First payment to annuitant is made one period after purchase
  • Most Common Type: Single Premium Immediate Annuity—annuity is purchased with a single lump sum payment and payments begin one period later.
  • Immediate annuities most commonly purchased by people nearing retirement

Types of Annuities

  • Fixed Deferred Annuity: Provides a guaranteed periodic payment at some point in the future
  • Typically part of a plan to accumulate retirement assets on a tax-deferred basis
  • If annuitant dies during this “accumulation phase,” a death benefit is typically paid, equal to the greater of total premiums paid or cash value
  • At maturity, policyholder can have funds paid as a lump sum or could choose a variety of payout options

Annuities—Key Terms/Issues

  • Accumulation Phase of an Annuity: Period before annuity payments commence and during which premium payments are made and cash values accumulate.
  • Premiums and CVs accumulate during this period at a rate determined by the accumulation structure of the annuity contract
  • Liquidation Period of Annuity: Period during which annuity payments are made.
  • Tax Treatment: Interest credited to annuities accumulates on a tax deferred basis. On liquidation, interest is taxable as ordinary income
  • Tax-deferral investment earnings within annuities is among there most attractive features

Types of Annuities

  • Joint Life Annuity: Annuity written on the lives of two or more people (e.g., husband and wife). In a joint life annuity, income payments terminate with the death of the first covered person
  • Obvious drawback: survivor may still require income
  • Joint-and-Survivor Annuity: Annuity written on the lives of two or more people (e.g., husband and wife). Payments terminate with the death of the last surviving annuitant
  • Typically pay less to surviving annuitant (e.g., 2/3 or ½) since annuity will be paying out for a longer period of time

Types of Annuities

  • Variable Annuity (VA): Provides a lifetime income but the income payments vary depending on the investment experience of the accounts in which the premiums are invested (e.g., common stock fund).
  • The fundamental premise of a VA is to provide an inflation hedge based on the assumption of a positive correlation between the cost of living and common stock prices over the long run.

Tax Treatment of Annuities

  • Non-Qualified
  • Individual annuities purchased from a commercial insurer are non-qualified. This means that annuities do not meet IRS requirements that most employer-sponsored retirement benefits receive. Hence
  • Premiums are not tax deductible (paid with after-tax dollars)
  • However, investment earnings are tax deferred (meaning that earnings are taxable only when proceeds are distributed)
  • Taxable portion taxed as ordinary income
  • Taxable portion is subject to an IRS penalty of 10% if taken before age 59 ½
  • Cautions
  • Annuities, especially variable annuities, are not for everyone
  • Shouldn’t purchase unless funds are need after age 59 ½
  • Should be invested for at least 15 years
  • Should exhaust other retirement options such as 401(k)s, IRAs, first

Health Insurance(Medical Expense Insurance)A Problematic System

Health (Medical Expense) Insurance

  • Health insurance in the US is a major economic and political issue in the United States
  • Healthcare expenditures totaled about $4.3 trillion (as of 2021)*
  • This equates to about 18% or GDP or nearly $1 out ever $5 spent in the US economy today or about $10,000 per person, per year (compared to about $5,000 in other advanced economies)
  • The US healthcare system is the most expensive and least efficient among developed countries
  • Costs are higher with no end in sight
  • Many people are uninsured
  • Patient outcomes are no better than in most of these other countries
  • Health insurance markets are generally not very competitive
  • Legislative, regulatory fixes are not fully effective and are political mine fields
  • * Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services:

DEFECTS: Defects in the US Healthcare System1. Rising Healthcare Costs2. Large Number of Uninsured Persons3. Waste and Inefficiency

DEFECT 1: Rising Healthcare CostsA Multi-Decade Challenge

Employer Responses to Rising Healthcare Costs

  • Increased employee co-payments (e.g., deductibles, higher coinsurance percentages, etc.)
  • Introduction of flexible benefit plans that provide a variety of health, life and other benefits with a fixed employer contribution
  • Termination of benefits entirely
  • Redesigning benefits to create incentives for more efficient utilization of healthcare resources
  • Reducing administrative cost
  • Increased use of managed care providers such as HMO, PPO and POS plans

The Economics of Healthcare

  • Healthcare financing in the US is typically financed through
  • General tax revenue
  • Under a social insurance model
  • Through a private insurance systems
  • From and individual’s or family’s personal resources (out-of-pocket)
  • Some combination of 1-4.

Defect 3: Waste and Inefficiency

  • It is estimated that as much as 30% of healthcare spending is wasted or unnecessary. Examples include
  • Duplication of tests because records from other providers are unavailable
  • Preventable medical errors
  • Defensive medicine
  • High administrative costs and excessive paperwork
  • Readmissions to hospitals because of ineffective or inadequate initial treatment
  • Hospitalization for controllable conditions (e.g., diabetes)
  • Fraud and overbilling
  • Overuse and duplication of expensive medical technology (e.g., MRIs)
  • Overuse of ERs (e.g., for non-emergency conditions)

Other Problems in Healthcare Markets

  • Cost/Benefit Measurement Problems
  • Often very hard to measure benefit relative to cost
  • Clear that preventative care (e.g., vaccinations) provide more benefit than expensive technologically intensive interventions
  • But HC market is characterized by rapid and continuing advances with high costs, overuse of ERs (e.g., for non-emergency conditions)
  • Information Problems
  • Buyer Ignorance—HC providers know much more about the services they deliver, their effectiveness and cost, alternatives, etc.

Other Problems in Healthcare Markets

  • Information Problems (cont’d)
  • Adverse Selection—Individuals have much more information about their health status and likelihood to use medical services than do insurers. Thus those who are most likely to consume medical services will be mostly likely to buy insurance—since they are effectively buying the coverage below actual cost. Consequently, low-risk individuals end up subsidizing high-risk individuals Screening for health status can help but this is expensive.
  • Note that controlling/limited adverse selection underlies all insurance contract design and underwriting processes in private insurance markets.
  • Moral Hazard—3rd-party payment for medical services increases moral hazard in the sense that: i.e., the presence of the insurance changes loss prevention/minimization behavior of the insured. In other words, the insured becomes less risk-averse and will be inclined to consume more medical resources. Insureds will tend to think the price of medical care is zero or equal to their deductible or co-pay and will therefore overconsume medical care. People also have less of an incentive to maintain their health or shop around for best deals on care.

Other Problems in Healthcare Markets

  • Market Power: Factors Impacting Competition
  • Barriers to Entry—Very high barriers in the market for medical services. Healthcare is heavily regulated, facilities and equipment are very expensive, licensing and certifications require difficult and lengthy processes to obtain. Medical professionals require specialized degrees, licenses, etc.
  • These factors all limit supply of medical services.
  • Economies of Scale—Some believe that medical services can be provided most efficiently on a large scale. At the same time, most HC is consumed locally. These competing points are at the center of the debate as to whether the private market is the best way to deliver HC services in the US.

Basic Provisions of the ACA: Prohibitions on Certain Insurer Practices

  • Lifetime and Annual Limits on Coverage Prohibited
  • Dollar limits on coverage prohibited
  • Pre-Existing Condition Exclusions Prohibited
  • Insurers are prohibited from denying claims or excluding coverage based on pre-existing conditions
  • Rescission of Insurance Policies Prohibited
  • Prohibits retroactive rescission of policy due to unintentional error except in cases of fraud or material misrepresentation
  • Retention of Coverage Until Age 26
  • ACA allows young adults to remain on parents policy until age 26 (can enroll immediately after turning 26)

Basic Provisions of the ACA: Prohibitions on Certain Insurer Practices

  • Guaranteed Access to Health Insurance
  • Policies sold in the individual and small group market via the Health Insurance Market are sold on a guaranteed issue and guaranteed renewable basis. Applicants cannot be denied coverage or be required to pay highest rates because of health status. Variations in rating variables are allowed only for age (limited to 3:1 ratio), number of family members, geographical area and tobacco usage (limited to 1.5:1 ratio). Cannot charge females more.
  • Grandfathered Plans
  • Certain types of plans that were in existence prior to March 23, 2010 can remain the same so long as they conform to certain new ACA requirements (e.g., no lifetime caps on coverage, no rescissions, extension of coverage to children up to age 26)

Basic Provisions of the ACA: Prohibitions on Certain Insurer Practices

  • Medical Loss Ratios (80/20 Rule or Medical Loss Ratio Rule)
  • Insurers required to meet a minimum loss ratio (MLR) of 80% for individual and small group market and 85% for large group market. The MLR refers to the percentage (share) of premiums paid for health insurance coverage that is used to pay claims and activities that improve the quality of care. In other words, insurers must pay a minimum of 80% or premium collected on benefits and keep only 20% for expenses and profits.

Basic Provisions of the ACA: Individual Mandate, Essential Health Benefits

  • Individual Mandate
  • ACA requires US citizens and legal residents to have qualifying health insurance of pay a penalty--$695 in 2016 or 2.5% of family income. After 2016, the penalty will be increased by annual cost of living adjustment.
  • Eliminated as part of 2017 Tax Reform Legislation! Effective in 2019
  • Was at the center of the Nov. 2020 US Supreme Court hearing
  • Essential Health Benefits
  • ACA requires that most medical expense policies provide a comprehensive package of benefits and services called essential health benefits.

Basic Provisions of the ACA: Health Insurance Marketplace

  • Health Insurance Marketplace
  • ACA creates a Health Insurance Marketplace in each state where individuals and small businesses can purchase affordable and qualified health insurance plans. These state “exchanges” enable people to comparison shop for standard health insurance plans, facilitate enrollment in the various plans and administer health insurance premium credits so that people of all incomes can purchase coverage.

Basic Provisions of the ACA: Advance Premium Tax Credits

  • Advanced Premium Tax Credits
  • ACA provides advanced premium tax credits to eligible individuals and families to make coverage more affordable. Available to people with incomes between 100% and 400% (up to $46,680 for an individual and up to $95,400 for a family of 4 in 2015). The idea is to limit the share of income that must be spent on health insurance (from 2% for the poorest people to 95% for those earning 300% - 400% of the FPL. of the federal poverty level (FPL).

Basic Provisions of the ACA: Cost-Sharing Subsidies, Employer-Shared Responsibility Requirements (Employer Mandate)

  • Cost-Sharing Subsides
  • Low-income individuals can receive subsidies for out-of-pocket costs such as deductibles and co-insurance
  • Employer-Shared Responsibility Requirements
  • Also know as the “Employer Mandate.” As of 2015, requires employers with 100+ FT equivalent workers to either offer health insurance or pay $2,000 per employee annually. Same requirement applies to business with 50+ employees beginning in 2016.

Basic Provisions of the ACA: Small Business Healthcare Tax Credits, Expansion of Medicaid

  • Small Business Healthcare Tax Credits
  • ACA provides tax credit s(subsidies) to business with fewer than 25 workers with average wages < $50,000.
  • Expansion of Medicaid
  • Expands Medicaid to adults with incomes up to 133% of FPL. Not all states comply with this provision as parts of this provision were ruled unconstitutional by the US Supreme Court.

Basic Provisions of the ACA: Improving Quality and Lowering Healthcare Costs

  • Improving Quality, Lowering Healthcare Costs
  • Rebuilding the primary care workforce
  • Preventing disease and illness
  • Strengthening community health centers
  • Cracking down on healthcare fraud
  • Reducing paperwork and administrative expenses
  • Create incentives for physicians to join Accountable Care Organizations, which are doctor groups that seek to better coordinate patient care and improve outcomes at lower cost. ACO can keep some of the savings.
  • Pay physicians based on value not volume

Individual Medical Expense Insurance

  • Individual Medical Expense Insurance: Protects and individual or family for covered medical expenses because of sickness or injury
  • Under the ACA, insurers are prohibited from imposing lifetime or annual limits on essential health benefits)
  • Essential Health Benefits must (at a minimum include)
  • Ambulatory patient services
  • ER services
  • Hospitalization (such as surgery)
  • Pregnancy, maternity, newborn care
  • Mental health and substance abuse
  • Prescription drugs
  • Rehabilitation services
  • Preventive and wellness services, chronic disease management
  • Pediatric services (including oral and vision care)

The ACA and Coverage Options

  • The ACA Offers 4 Coverage Tiers (Marketplace Policies)
  • Coverage is based on actuarial value of the plans (i.e., the expected share of healthcare expenses the plan will pay for the specific group of enrollees)
  • Platinum (90%) (most expensive plan)
  • Gold (80%)
  • Silver (70%)
  • Bronze (60%) (least expensive plan)
  • All offer Essential Health Benefits

The ACA and Coverage Options (cont’d)

  • The ACA Also Offers “Catastrophic Plans” Coverage
  • Catastrophe Plans offer, on average, less than 60% of total average annual cost of care
  • Available only to people under 30 or those with a “hardship” exemption (e.g., homeless, death of close family member)
  • Low monthly premiums but deductibles and co-payments are high (up to $6,450 for an individual and $12,900 for a family in 2015)
  • Includes 3 primary care visits at no cost

Example of Health Insurance Exchange Monthly Premiums in SC from HealthCare.gov

  • NOTE: All plans are offered through BC/BS of SC, the only exchange insurer in SC.
  • 25-Year Old Single Adult, No Tobacco Use
  • Average Silver Plan monthly premium in 2018 for 25-yr. old single adult will rise by 33.7%, to $446.15 from $333.67.
  • 60-Year Old Single Adult, No Tobacco Use
  • Silver Plan for a 60-yr. old single adult will rise by 33.7%, to $1,206.03 from $901.97.

Example of Health Insurance Exchange Monthly Premiums in SC from HealthCare.gov

  • NOTE: Platinum plans not currently available in SC.
  • 25-Year Old Single Adult, No Tobacco Use
  • Average BC/BS Silver Plan monthly premium in 2024 for 25-yr. old single adult actually fell by 1.9% to $461.10 from $469.82

Use of Health Status in Underwriting and Rating Before the ACA

  • Many very common pre-existing conditions could result in coverage declinations pre-ACA

ACA Marketplace Policy Medical Expense Insurance

  • Calendar-Year Deductibles: An aggregate deductible that must be satisfied only once during the calendar year. All covered medical expenses can be applied toward the deductible which must be satisfied before any benefits are paid. Once the deductible is met, no additional deductible has to be satisfied during the calendar year.
  • Deductibles for individuals are $1,000, $1,500 or $2,500 and for families $5,000 or $10,000
  • The idea is that my eliminating small claims, administrative costs are reduced and insurers can offer high limits with reasonable premiums
  • Coinsurance: Individual medical expense policies typically contain coinsurance provisions. Coinsurance is the percentage of the bill in excess of the deductible which the insured must pay out-of-pocket up to some maximum dollar limit. Typical coinsurance percentage is 20%, 25% or 30%.
  • Example: Bob has covered medical expenses of $10,000, the calendar-year deductive is $1,000 and the coinsurance percentage is 20%. How much does Bob pay beyond his deductible?
  • $10,000 - $1,000 = $9,000 x 0.2 = $1,800; Insurer pays remaining $7,200.
  • 2 Basic Purposes of Coinsurance
  • Reduce premiums (or keep them low)
  • Prevent over-utilization of plan benefits

ACA Marketplace Policy Medical Expense Insurance

  • Out-of-Pocket Maximum Limits: Marketplace policies have an out-of-pocket maximum limit beyond which 100% of the covered medical expenses in excess of the deductible.
  • Purpose is to reduce crushing burden of a catastrophic loss (e.g., cancer)
  • Exclusions: Acupuncture, bariatric surgery, cosmetic surgery, LTC, private-duty nursing, routine eye care (adults), weight loss programs, routine foot care…

Health Savings Accounts (HSAs)

  • Health Savings Account: A tax-exempt (qualified) account established solely with the purpose of paying qualified medical expenses of the account beneficiary who is covered under a high deductible health insurance plan.
  • HSAs have 2 components
  • A high-deductible health insurance policy that covers catastrophic medical bills (deductible of at least $1,300 for individuals and $2,600 for families in 2015)
  • An investment account from which the account holder can withdraw funds tax-free for medical costs (contributions are made with pre-tax dollars; investment earnings accumulate tax-free)

Key Government Health Programs: Medicare

  • Medicare: U.S. social insurance program covering the elderly (defined as 65+)
  • Signed into law in 1965 by President Lyndon Johnson
  • Financed by a 2.9% payroll tax, shared equally by employers and workers plus
  • Effective in 2015 under the ACA, high income workers pay 3.8% a new 3.8% tax on unearned investment income of high earners
  • Medicare has 4 benefit plans, known as parts: A, B, C and D
  • Part A applies to all participants; B,C and D are optional

Key Govt. Health Programs: Medicare (cont’d)

  • Medicare Part A: Covers hospital and nursing home care facility charges
  • Pays for hospital stays of up to 90 days subject to cost-sharing provisions (per hospitalization)
  • Part A also pays for up to 100 days in a skilled nursing facility
  • No coverage for non-institutional care in the home or on an outpatient basis

Key Govt. Health Programs: Medicare (cont’d)

  • Medicare Part B: Pays for physician, nursing and testing services as well as durable medical equipment such as mobility devices (e.g., wheel chairs), prosthetic devices, oxygen supply machines, etc.
  • Medicare Part C: Provides the option of receiving Medicare benefits under private insurance plans known as Medicare Advantage Plans, which are available to people with both Medicare A and B coverage
  • Insurers offering these plans receive a capitation (per person) fee from the social insurance program
  • Coverage may require and additional premium but if so benefits also tend to be more liberal
  • Plans have managed care features and require the use of approved providers

Key Govt. Health Programs: Medicare (cont’d)

  • Medicare Part D: Pays for prescription drug benefits
  • Sold as standalone plans distinct from Parts A, B, C
  • Sold only by private insurers
  • Provides basic and catastrophic coverage
  • But there is a coverage gap between the max basic benefit and the minimum catastrophic benefit in which the beneficiary must pay 100% of the costs out-of-pocket; Gap known as the “donut-hole.”
  • Insurers may charge supplemental premium but provide more liberal benefits

Key Govt. Health Programs: Medicaid

  • Medicaid: Jointly funded federal/state insurance program for certain low-income and needy people
  • Started in 1965, also under President Johnson
  • Administered by the states and is the largest of all govt. funded health care programs for low-income people (~60 million covered)
  • Includes low income adults, their children; people with certain disabilities
  • Jointly funded by federal and state governments but federal govt.’s funding share is inversely proportional to the state’s poverty rate
  • ACA significantly expanded eligibility for and federal funding of Medicaid
  • Federal government would pay all/most of additional costs associated with expansion through 2022. However, some states balked at Medicaid expansion arguing that this would place a burden on them beyond 2022.
  • US Supreme Court sides with those states that refused to expand Medicaid and wanted to stick with previous levels of funding.
  • Consequently, about 5.7 million fewer people are covered than would have otherwise been the case

Medicaid: Federal/State Cost Sharing

  • The federal government’s funding share is high in SC

Other Private Insurance Products with Health Insurance-Related Features- Long-Term Care Insurance-Disability Income Insurance-Workers Compensation

Long-Term Care Insurance (LTC)

  • Long-Term Care Insurance: Type of coverage that pays a daily or monthly benefit for medical or custodial care received in a nursing facility, hospital or home.
  • Cost of long-term care facilities (e.g., nursing homes) is staggering: ~$90 - $130K+ per year
  • Medicare only pays 100 days/year and custodial care excluded entirely
  • Most elderly not eligible for Medicaid, at least initially, as their assets/wealth may exceed limits
  • Odds of entering a nursing home are very high
  • Recent study: 44% of men and 58% of women age 65+ will need nursing home care at some point
  • Average stay (for those 65+) for men is 10 months and women 16 months
  • About half of men and 39% of women never have a stay exceeding 3 months
  • LTC is typically tax-qualified (can deduct from taxable income)

Long-Term Care Insurance: Characteristics (Cont’d)

  • Covered Services
  • Nursing home care
  • Home healthcare
  • Respite care for a caregiver
  • Hospice care
  • Assisted living facilities
  • Adult daycare
  • Elimination Period: Waiting period during which benefits are not paid, typically 20 to 90 days
  • Longer waiting period reduces premium but would increase out-of-pocket costs if needed right away

Long-Term Care Insurance: Characteristics (Cont’d)

  • Eligibility for Benefits: Tax-qualified policies have benefit triggers that determine if the insured is chronically ill and eligible for benefits. An insured meet one of the triggers to receive benefits.
  • Trigger 1: Inability to perform a certain number of Activities of Daily Living (ADLs)
  • Eating, bathing, dressing, transferring from a bed to a chair, using the toilet, incontinence
  • Trigger 2: Insured must need substantial supervision to be protected against threat to health and safety because of severe cognitive impairment (e.g. Alzheimer’s)
  • Guaranteed Renewable: BUT rates can be increased for the underwriting class in which the insured has been placed
  • Longer lifespans, higher medical costs, low interest rates have forced premiums up
  • As premiums rise, people let policies lapse
  • Has adversely impacted sales
  • Expensive Coverage: LTC can be expensive, especially at older ages.
  • Example: Annual premium for a 40-year old male w/daily benefit of $150 and 90-day elimination period ~$1,750 (in NE) but $2,861at 65 and $4,511 at 70.

Disability Income Insurance

  • Disability Income Insurance: Provides periodic incomes payments when the insured is unable to work because of sickness or injury.
  • The amount of disability insurance a person can purchase is a related to earnings
  • To prevent overinsurance, moral hazard and malingering insurers limit coverage to 60% - 70% of earnings

Disability Income Insurance

  • Total Disability (TD): Insurers use different definitions
  • Inability to perform the material and substantial duties of your regular occupation
  • Inability to perform the material and substantial duties of your regular occupation, and are not engaged in any other occupation
  • Inability to perform the duties of any occupation for which, and are not engaged in any other occupation
  • The amount of disability insurance a person can purchase is a related to earnings
  • To prevent overinsurance, moral hazard and malingering insurers limit coverage to 60% - 70% of earnings

Disability Income Insurance

  • Partial Disability (PD): Means that you can perform some but not all of the duties of your occupation
  • Usually paid as a share of wage for a fixed period of time (e.g., 50% of wage for 3, 6 or 12 months)
  • Residual Disability: Definition varies among insurers
  • General Idea: Residual Disability means that you are gainfully employed and not totally disabled but, solely because of sickness or injury, your loss of income is at least XX% of your prior income (e.g., 15%)
  • Benefit Period: Insured has a choice of period such as 2, 5 or 10 years or until age 65 or 70

Workers Compensation (WC)

  • Workers’ Compensation Laws: State WC laws require employers to provide benefits to employees for losses resulting form work-related injuries
  • WC benefits provide for medical and indemnity (wage loss) costs
  • Medical costs account for about 70% of all WC costs
  • Medical costs include all work-related medical expenses, rehabilitation; Death benefits paid in case of fatal accidents.
  • Focus is on return-to-work
  • Costs entirely borne employer
  • WC is a “no-fault” system. This means that no determination of negligence is needed to trigger benefits (therefore lawsuits are theoretically avoided). The worker is always covered even if they contributed in some way to the accident
  • In some states, benefits can be denied in the event of intoxication or willful disregard of safety rules
  • Employers usually comply with state law through the purchase of WC coverage from a private insurer or in some states and WC “state fund”

Risk Management And Insurance

  • END OF MATERIAL FOR
Swamp Fox Insurance Agency · Insurance Knowledge Tool · Internal reference
